# Getting building records for 1719 SE Ladd Ave

## Plain-English answer first

The fastest, cheapest path is to start free on the city's online property map at https://www.portlandmaps.com/ and click into the **Permits** panel for your address; for most homes that covers permits issued from June 1, 2012 through December 2023, and full plan sets are usually attached as PDFs (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). For anything older (and your house is from 1911, so most of its history is older), you'll need to file a formal public records request with **Portland Permitting & Development**, the bureau formerly called BDS that now handles building permits; that costs a minimum of $8 per address (about a fast-food lunch), and right now it can take two weeks or more to get a response (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). **Sewer connection records, sidewalk and driveway records, and fire/oil-tank records live with different bureaus, and you have to ask each one separately.** None of these records *prove* work was done correctly; they only prove a permit was pulled and (if it has a "Final Inspection Approved" status) that an inspector signed off. If you're planning a renovation, the most important thing to look up *before* spending a dollar on plans isn't the permit history at all; it's whether your house is a "contributing" resource in the **Ladd's Addition Historic District**, which it is, and which means most exterior changes need a separate **Historic Resource Review** before you can pull a permit.

---

## What I already found on file for 1719 SE Ladd Ave

This part is your free shortcut. A lot of what people pay $8 to ask for is already visible on PortlandMaps if you know where to click. Here's what's there for your address right now (PortlandMaps property page: https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/1719-SE-LADD-AVE/R200340_did/).

**Built 1911, classified as a "Contributing" historic resource in the Ladd's Addition Historic District, with the historic name "North, H.R., Residence."** The state's historic database has a record on it at https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=49624. "Contributing" means the house counts toward the district's historic character, which triggers stricter review for exterior changes (Portland City Code 33.846, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846).

**Base zoning is R5 (Residential 5,000), no overlays beyond the historic district** (PortlandMaps zoning record for R200340; zoning code reference https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/base/R5). You can confirm this for free anytime by typing your address into PortlandMaps and clicking the **Zoning & Districts** button.

**Six permits show in the computerized system, going back only to 2004.** Everything before that is on microfilm or paper. The six are:

- A 2019 electrical permit for a 100-amp garage subpanel and a NEMA 14-50 receptacle (likely for an EV charger), application 2019-242222-ET, **Final Inspection Approved**.
- A 2014 electrical permit (3-circuit modification) and a 2014 mechanical permit (gas furnace and A/C), both showing **VOID** status — meaning they were pulled but never finalled, so the work either wasn't done under that permit or was never inspected.
- A 2013 plumbing permit for an inside sewer line connection, **Final**, application 2013-209630-PT.
- A 2013 plumbing permit for sewer service line repair (about 40 feet, on private property only, no work in the public right-of-way), **Final**, application 2013-206680-PT.
- A 2004 plumbing permit for a 60-foot water service plus 2 backflow preventers, **Final**.

**One historic plumbing record (a 51 KB scan) is available right now for free download** under the Historic Plumbing section of the PortlandMaps permit panel. For a 1911 house, that scan is likely your single most valuable old document; it's where the original sewer connection or cesspool replacement is usually noted (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/portlandmaps-help-guides/portlandmaps-general-search).

**Six historic sewer board files for the area are available free** (3231.PDF, 3232.PDF, plus the pre-1980 PBOT/Maintenance sewer board JPGs for the same quarter sections). These are scans of the old paper maps the city used to track sewer connections house by house; they often show your service lateral better than any modern record. Scroll to the **Utilities → Sewer Assets** section on PortlandMaps to see them.

**Your house ties into the public combined sewer via lateral compkey 291254 (UnitID AJC133)**, on a sanitary gravity main between manholes ATV776 and ABV087. The block was rebuilt under BES job E10368, "Sunnyside South & Ladd's Add Recon & GS." That's a free clue for any future sewer scope or repair.

**There are old underground heating-oil tank records on file for 1629 SE Ladd, a few houses down.** Nothing on file for your address, but if you ever need to satisfy a lender's oil-tank question, the absence of a record is not the same as proof there was never a tank; that's a separate paid records request to **Portland Fire & Rescue (PF&R)** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

That's the head start. Everything below is how to get the rest.

---

## Your legal right to all of this, and the city's deadlines

Two layers of law govern this. The state law sets the floor; the city rule fills in the local procedure.

**Oregon Public Records Law gives you the right to inspect or copy any non-exempt city record.** The custodian of the records has to give you "proper and reasonable opportunities for inspection and examination" of the records during normal business hours (ORS 192.314, https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.314). When you submit a written request, the city has **5 business days to acknowledge receipt** and confirm whether it has the records (ORS 192.324(2), https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.324). Then it has to either complete the response or give you a written estimated completion date "as soon as reasonably possible but not later than 10 business days" after acknowledging the request (ORS 192.329(5), https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.329). The city can charge a fee "reasonably calculated to reimburse" actual costs, but if the estimate is over $25 it has to give you a written notice and your okay before doing the work (ORS 192.324(4)(c), https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.324). Roughly, plan on a couple of business days for an acknowledgment and a few weeks for the records.

**The city's own rule is ARA-8.03 (Auditor's Office Administrative Rule on Public Records Requests).** It tells city bureaus how to handle requests and is at https://www.portland.gov/policies/adopted-rules-auditors-office/archives-records-management/ara-803-public-records-requests. Public records requests in Portland generally go through the City Auditor's central portal at https://www.portland.gov/prr.

**Permitting & Development's own page acknowledges that "due to limited staffing and a large workload, it could take two weeks or more to fulfill requests for building permit records,"** and the minimum building-permit records fee is **$8 per address** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). $8 is "less than a movie ticket"; the two-week-plus wait is "about a long weekend, sometimes more like a vacation."

---

## The free path: doing it yourself on PortlandMaps

This is where everyone should start; it costs nothing and answers most questions. If you've never used the city's online property map before, the official walk-through is at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/portlandmaps-help-guides/portlandmaps-general-search.

Type your address into the search bar at https://www.portlandmaps.com/. On the property page, scroll the right-side panel to **Permits & Zoning** and click **Permits**. You'll see a list of permits and cases; clicking the **Application Number** link opens the activity log, and from there you can usually click document IDs to view the full PDF plan set. To view documents, you'll be prompted to sign in or create a free City of Portland account; that's a one-time five-minute thing (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

Three things this lookup will *not* show you in most cases: (1) plans for permits issued before June 1, 2012 (those scans aren't online); (2) plans for permits issued after December 2023 (the city is behind on uploading newer ones); and (3) any record that was originally on microfilm or paper and never digitized (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). For a 1911 house like yours, that's most of the history.

**Three other free sources worth knowing about:**

The **old plumbing-permit and pre-2003 building-code-appeals search** is a separate database with free scans of permits as old as the 1950s in many parts of the city. It's at https://www.portlandoregon.gov/bds/plumbing_permits/ (warning: it asks for Adobe Reader and the interface is dated, but it's free and has records nothing else has).

For **completed land use decisions** (historic resource reviews, design reviews, partition cases, and the like), the city has a free document portal called **eFiles** at https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/Search. It covers 2017 and 2020 to the present. Search by case number (for example, "21-001350 LU") (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

For **zoning and overlays**, click the **Zoning & Districts** button on PortlandMaps; it shows base zoning, overlays, comprehensive plan designation, plan district, and any historic, conservation, or national register district (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/portlandmaps-help-guides/portlandmaps-general-search). For your address, that confirms **R5 base zoning, no overlay zones beyond the Ladd's Addition Historic District (LA)**.

---

## The formal path: filing a public records request

When the free lookup runs out (which it will, for a 1911 house), this is the next step. There are three sub-paths depending on which bureau holds the record.

The summary in one sentence: **for building/electrical/mechanical/plumbing permit records, file with Permitting & Development; for sewer connection or stormwater records, file with Environmental Services; for sidewalk, driveway, curb cut, or street tree records, file with the Bureau of Transportation; and they're all submitted through the same central portal at https://www.portland.gov/prr but routed to different bureaus.**

| Bureau | What they have | Cost (FREE noted) | Typical timeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D, formerly BDS)** | Building, electrical, mechanical, plumbing permits and plans; inspection cards; certificates of occupancy; code-enforcement case records; land use review files | $8 minimum per address; more if it takes over an hour of staff time, with an estimate provided first per ORS 192.324(4)(c) | Two weeks or more; sometimes a month; expect a 5-business-day acknowledgment first per ORS 192.324(2). (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records) |
| **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES, the city's sewer and stormwater bureau)** | Sewer connection (UC) and lateral repair (UR) permits, sewer scope videos, industrial pretreatment, spill cleanup records, septic records | No standard published fee; subject to ORS 192.324(4) actual-cost reimbursement; **FREE** sewer-access permits for things like camera scopes (https://www.portland.gov/bes/guide-requesting-permit-sewer-access) | Same Oregon law deadlines as PP&D; varies with request size |
| **Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT)** | Sidewalk and driveway permits, curb-cut records, encroachment permits, street tree pruning permits, posting affidavits | Same actual-cost rules under ORS 192.324(4); fees vary | Same Oregon law deadlines; varies with request size |

**For PP&D specifically, the steps are these.** Set up a one-time free account at the city's records portal at https://www.portland.gov/prr. From the **Community and Economic Development** service area, pick **Permitting and Development**. In the request, name the records you want by their actual category names: *"plans for building," "plans for site development," "trade permits (electrical, mechanical, plumbing)," "zoning permits," "permit inspection cards," "sign permits," "Certificates of Occupancy"* (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). Naming them this way matches how the city catalogs them and reduces back-and-forth.

PP&D will email you with an $8 charge upfront and, if the search will take more than an hour of staff time, a written estimate for additional fees that you have to approve before they keep going (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records, and the legal basis is ORS 192.324(4)(c), https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.324).

**If you'd rather mail it in or hand-deliver,** PP&D requires *both* the Uniform Public Records Request form (https://www.portland.gov/auditor/documents/adm-803-fillable-public-records-request-form/download) *and* a Copyright Indemnification Form (because old building plans are sometimes copyrighted by the architects and the city wants you to take that risk; https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/request-reproduction-copyrighted-materials/download). The Development Services Center is at 1900 SW 4th Avenue, 1st floor lobby, open Tuesdays and Thursdays 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). Mail it to:

> Public Records Coordinator, Portland Permitting & Development, 1900 SW 4th Avenue, Suite 5000, Portland, OR 97201

The PP&D phone line is **503-823-7300**, Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. (https://www.portland.gov/ppd).

If you ever feel the city is dragging its feet, you can petition the **Oregon Public Records Advocate** (a state office set up in 2017 to mediate these disputes); reach them at PublicRecordsAdvocate.PRC@oregon.gov or 503-378-5228 (https://www.oregon.gov/gov/pages/public-records-requests.aspx).

---

## Microfilm and archived plans (the old stuff)

For a 1911 house, this is most of the history. There is no separate "microfilm fee" published by PP&D; under Oregon law, the city can charge actual cost for retrieval, scanning, and review (ORS 192.324(4), https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.324). The minimum $8 records fee per address still applies (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

Realistic expectations for old paper or microfilm records: a few weeks to a couple of months in busy periods, and the records may simply not exist. PP&D explicitly notes that some categories of records they keep go back only to specific years; for example, they hold electrical permits from 1983 to 1994, mechanical from 1960 to 2005, and sign permits from the 1970s to 2005 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). Earlier work may have been done legally under permits the city no longer has on file. **No record does not equal no permit.** I couldn't find a specific code section that sets PP&D's microfilm scanning fee per page; if you need that confirmed, call PP&D at 503-823-7300.

When you make the request, ask in this order: (1) **the property's complete permit jacket** (this is the umbrella term); (2) **the original Certificate of Occupancy (CO)**, which is the official sign-off that says the building is legally finished and approved for the listed use; (3) **inspection cards**; (4) any **microfilm or scanned plan sets** for the address; (5) any **building or trade permits not currently visible on PortlandMaps** for the address; and (6) for a Ladd's Addition house, **any historic resource review or design review case files** under Portland City Code 33.846 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846).

---

## Considerations specific to your Ladd's Addition home

**The historic district is the single biggest factor in any future work on your house.** Ladd's Addition was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1988 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ladd%27s_Addition) and the City of Portland regulates it as a Historic District under Portland City Code 33.846 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846 and the printable rule text at https://www.portland.gov/sites/default/files/code/33.846-historic-resource-reviews.pdf). General background on Portland's historic, conservation, and national register districts is at https://www.portland.gov/bps/planning/historic-resources/historic-and-conservation-districts.

What that means in practice: as a "Contributing" resource, **most exterior alterations visible from the public right-of-way (the strip the city owns between sidewalk and street) need a Historic Resource Review before you can pull a building permit.** Window replacement, siding changes, roof material changes, additions, fences, even some paint and trim changes can trigger it. Small projects under 150 square feet on a non-street-facing wall may qualify for an exemption (Portland City Code 33.846.060, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846). Interior work and rear-only changes are generally less restricted.

**Two free resources that punch above their weight here:**

The **Ladd's Addition Conservation District Guidelines** (adopted 1988, used by the Historic Landmarks Commission to review exterior projects) are linked through the neighborhood association at https://handpdx.org/ladds-addition-historic-guidelines/. Read these before talking to a contractor.

The **Historic Landmarks Commission** meets the 2nd and 4th Mondays of each month and publishes its hearings on the PP&D events calendar at https://www.portland.gov/ppd. If you're planning a project, attending one meeting before you spend money on plans will tell you more than a stack of records.

**Zoning summary: R5 (Residential 5,000), no other overlays.** R5 is the city's standard single-dwelling zone with a 5,000-square-foot minimum lot size; the live page is https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/base/R5. Confirm this on PortlandMaps before any plans are drawn, because zoning and overlays for individual lots can vary and are occasionally amended.

**Sewer connection and side-sewer records are at BES, not PP&D.** The lateral on your property is the line from your house out to the public main, and the city splits responsibility at the curb (you maintain your part; the city maintains the part beyond, called the "branch"). The rules are in Portland City Code 17.32 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32) and BES Administrative Rule ENB-4.17 (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-417-administrative-rules-sewer). For older Portland homes, a "non-conforming" sewer connection is common and worth knowing about before the line fails or before the city does a street project that disturbs it. **The single most accurate way to know what you have is a sewer scope** (a contractor pushes a camera down the line; usually $100 to $200, "about a tank of gas to a fancy dinner") (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/understanding-sewer-connection). Free city records are good supporting evidence; the scope is the truth. To request sewer connection records from BES, go to https://www.portland.gov/prr, pick the Public Works service area, and select Environmental Services; for sewer-access questions specifically, BES's lateral and connections line is **503-823-1026** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/news/2019/4/29/bureau-environmental-services-bes-update).

**Sidewalk, driveway, curb cut, and street tree records are at PBOT.** These cover the public right-of-way, the strip and sidewalk between your house and the street. Driveway rules are in Portland City Code 17.28.110 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/28) and Transportation Administrative Rule TRN-10.40 (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/development/driveways). Sidewalk and driveway permit administration is in TRN-10.17 (https://portlandoregon.gov/citycode/article/40993). Sidewalks and driveways usually need either a **Minor Improvement Permit** (simple repairs) or a **Public Works Permit** (engineering review needed) (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/development/permit-and-appeal-applications). To request PBOT records, file at https://www.portland.gov/prr and pick the Public Works service area, then Transportation. Street tree pruning permits are issued by Urban Forestry; the safety/visibility hotline is **503-823-SAFE (7233)** and the sidewalk-tree line is **503-823-1711** (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-concerns-and-problems/trees-and-street-visibility-clearances-safety-and-sidewalks).

**If your records show unpermitted work, you are not in immediate trouble, but you have options to clean it up.** Portland enforces unpermitted work mostly on a complaint-driven basis through the **Property Compliance** team at PP&D (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/code-enforcement). The hotline is **503-823-2633** and the email is codec@portlandoregon.gov. You can voluntarily apply for a permit "after the fact" (sometimes called a retroactive permit); the city will assess the work, may require parts of it to be opened up for inspection, and will apply an investigation fee on top of the regular permit fee (the framework is in Portland City Code 18.18 for enforcement and penalties at https://www.portland.gov/code/18/18, and the electrical-specific investigation fee provision is in Portland City Code 26.03.050 at https://www.portland.gov/code/26/03/050). Under city code, an investigation fee equal to the actual additional cost of confirming the work is up to code may be charged; the exact dollar figure isn't fixed in code, so call PP&D at 503-823-7300 for a current estimate before you apply. Lenders, insurers, and buyers care about this; buyers and title companies routinely pull permit records during a sale.

**Sequencing for a renovation: do this before paying for anything.** First, free, on PortlandMaps: confirm zoning, overlays, and historic status (already done above for you). Second, free: download all available plans, plumbing scans, sewer board scans, and historic plumbing records from PortlandMaps. Third, paid ($8): file a public records request with PP&D for the full permit jacket, original Certificate of Occupancy, inspection cards, and any historic resource review case files. Fourth, paid ($100–$200): get a sewer scope. Fifth, free: read the Ladd's Addition Conservation District Guidelines and (optional) attend one Historic Landmarks Commission hearing. Sixth, only then: meet with a contractor. The point of this order is that the historic district often shapes the design before plans are useful.

---

## What to do next: numbered steps, in order

This is the order I'd actually do it in.

1. **(FREE, 10 minutes.) Open https://www.portlandmaps.com/, type "1719 SE LADD AVE," click your property, and download every PDF in the Permits and Historic Plumbing sections.** Create a free city account when prompted. This is your free baseline (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/portlandmaps-help-guides/portlandmaps-general-search).

2. **(FREE, 10 minutes.) On the same page, click Zoning & Districts to confirm R5 base zoning and the Ladd's Addition Historic District designation,** and download the historic plumbing scan and sewer-board PDFs from the Utilities section. The state's historic resource record for your house is at https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=49624.

3. **(FREE, 5 minutes.) Search the legacy plumbing-permit database at https://www.portlandoregon.gov/bds/plumbing_permits/ for any older plumbing scans not surfaced through PortlandMaps.** For a 1911 home this can pick up records the modern map misses.

4. **(FREE, 30 minutes.) Search eFiles at https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/Search for any prior land use case file (Historic Resource Review, partition, design review) for the address.** Use the address and any case number you find on PortlandMaps.

5. **(PAID, $8 minimum, 2 weeks or more.) File a public records request with Portland Permitting & Development at https://www.portland.gov/prr.** Pick the **Community and Economic Development** service area, then **Permitting and Development**. Use the script below (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). PP&D phone: 503-823-7300.

6. **(PAID, varies, similar timeline.) File a separate request with the Bureau of Environmental Services at the same portal,** picking the **Public Works** service area then **Environmental Services**. Ask for sewer connection records, lateral records, and any historic side-sewer documentation. BES sewer connection line: 503-823-1026 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/news/2019/4/29/bureau-environmental-services-bes-update).

7. **(PAID, varies, similar timeline.) File a separate request with the Bureau of Transportation at the same portal,** picking **Public Works → Transportation**. Ask for sidewalk, driveway, curb cut, encroachment, and street tree records for the address. PBOT general sidewalk-tree line: 503-823-1711 (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-concerns-and-problems/trees-and-street-visibility-clearances-safety-and-sidewalks).

8. **(PAID, $100–$200, 1 hour on site.) Hire a plumbing contractor for a sewer scope.** This is the only thing that *proves* the actual condition of the line, beyond what the records say (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/understanding-sewer-connection).

9. **(FREE, optional, 1 evening.) Read the Ladd's Addition Conservation District Guidelines** at https://handpdx.org/ladds-addition-historic-guidelines/ and skim Portland City Code 33.846 at https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846 before any renovation conversation.

10. **(FREE if needed, ongoing.) If anything the city tells you feels off (timeline, fee, denial), petition the Oregon Public Records Advocate** at PublicRecordsAdvocate.PRC@oregon.gov or 503-378-5228 (https://www.oregon.gov/gov/pages/public-records-requests.aspx).

---

## Script you can paste into the city's portal

Use this in the **"Describe Records Requested"** field of the public records portal at https://www.portland.gov/prr. I've written it for PP&D first; mild edits make it work for BES and PBOT too. (No em dashes, semicolons and parentheses only; matches your preference.)

> **For Portland Permitting & Development:**
>
> Property address: 1719 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214. Property ID: R200340. State ID: 1S1E02CA 3100.
>
> I am the property owner and I am requesting the complete public record set for this address. Specifically, please provide all records the bureau holds for this address, including but not limited to: (1) the complete permit jacket for any building, electrical, mechanical, plumbing, zoning, or sign permit ever issued for this address, including permits not currently visible on PortlandMaps; (2) the original Certificate of Occupancy and any later Certificate of Occupancy on file; (3) all building plans and site plans, including microfilm and scanned plan sets; (4) all permit inspection cards; (5) all checksheets and review-process comments associated with each permit; (6) any code enforcement case records, including closed cases, for this address; (7) any Historic Resource Review or design review case files for this address; and (8) any Building Code Appeals records.
>
> I am requesting copies in electronic form (PDF) where available, per ORS 192.324(3). Please send the records by email at no charge if practical. If a fee will apply, please send a written cost and time estimate before doing the work, per ORS 192.324(4)(c). If the total estimated fee will exceed $25, please confirm with me in writing before proceeding. If you determine that any record is exempt from disclosure, please cite the specific exemption.
>
> Thank you. (Your name, phone, email.)

> **For the Bureau of Environmental Services (Public Works → Environmental Services):**
>
> Property address: 1719 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214. Property ID: R200340.
>
> I am the property owner. Please provide all records the bureau holds for this address, including but not limited to: (1) historic side-sewer connection records and any sewer connection drawings (UC permit records); (2) any lateral repair (UR) permit records; (3) any sewer-asset as-builts and sewer service request records associated with this address; (4) any TV inspection records; and (5) any easement records. The lateral compkey associated with this property in the city's GIS appears to be 291254 (UnitID AJC133); please include records associated with that lateral. (Same fee/timeline language as above.)

> **For the Bureau of Transportation (Public Works → Transportation):**
>
> Property address: 1719 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214. Property ID: R200340.
>
> I am the property owner. Please provide all records PBOT holds for this address and its frontage, including but not limited to: (1) sidewalk and driveway permits, including Minor Improvement Permits and Public Works Permits; (2) any sidewalk posting affidavits; (3) any curb cut or driveway design exception records; (4) any encroachment permits; (5) any street tree pruning, planting, removal, or root pruning permits; and (6) any temporary street use permits at this frontage. (Same fee/timeline language.)

If you'd rather call: PP&D at **503-823-7300** (Monday–Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m.); BES sewer connections at **503-823-1026**; PBOT sidewalk/tree at **503-823-1711** or for safety issues **503-823-SAFE (7233)** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd; https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-concerns-and-problems/trees-and-street-visibility-clearances-safety-and-sidewalks).

---

## Common gotchas

A permit doesn't prove the work was *done correctly*; it proves a permit was *issued*. The thing that proves the work passed is a **"Final Inspection Approved"** status and a **Certificate of Occupancy** on the jacket. A "VOID" status on a permit (you have two of those, both 2014) means the permit was pulled but never finalled; the work may or may not have been done. A "Cancelled" or "Expired" status means the permit lapsed before inspection.

**Finding no permit is not the same as proving none was ever issued.** PP&D's own page lists specific year ranges for which they hold electrical, mechanical, and sign permits (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). Earlier work may have been done legally under permits the city no longer keeps. For pre-1960s work in particular, "no record found" is common and not, by itself, evidence of unpermitted work.

**Records older than about the early 1960s often don't exist at all.** The city's record-keeping changed several times in the twentieth century, and some categories of records were destroyed under retention schedules (Oregon's framework for record disposal is at ORS 192.105–192.108, https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.105).

**Historic district approvals are tracked separately from building permits.** A Historic Resource Review case file lives in the land use system, not the building permit system; you have to ask for it by case number, usually formatted like "21-001350 LU" (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

**The county and city sometimes show different square footage.** PP&D is explicit: "Non-permitted items will be assessed by the county, but City of Portland will acknowledge permitted items only" (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/portlandmaps-help-guides/portlandmaps-general-search). If your county-assessed square footage is bigger than what city permits show, that gap is *exactly* where unpermitted work tends to live.

**The two-week or longer wait at PP&D is real.** Their own page says it (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records). Don't start a records request a week before a closing.

**Don't pay extra for things that are already free.** Plans for permits issued June 1, 2012 through December 2023 are on PortlandMaps for free; if you ask PP&D for them in a paid request, they will (politely) tell you so. Same for the historic plumbing scan, the sewer board PDFs, and most zoning information (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

**Three contact points everyone confuses:** PP&D handles building/permits/code-enforcement; BES handles sewer/stormwater; PBOT handles sidewalks/driveways/right-of-way. Asking the wrong bureau wastes your week. If in doubt, call PP&D first at **503-823-7300** and they'll redirect you (https://www.portland.gov/ppd).

**Friendly accuracy heads-up:** zoning and overlays for an individual lot can be amended, and the historic district is itself reviewed periodically; confirm everything on PortlandMaps before spending money on plans. The Ladd's Addition Conservation District Guidelines you'll find online are the 1988 adopted set; for the most current Historic Resource Review thresholds and procedures, the authoritative source is Portland City Code 33.846 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846).

**Fee figures change.** PP&D was actively proposing fee changes for the upcoming fiscal year as of late April 2026 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd). The $8 minimum and the actual-cost rule under ORS 192.324(4) are stable, but specific dollar figures should be confirmed at the time you file.

---

## Glossary

**BDS** (Bureau of Development Services): Portland's old name for the building-permit bureau. As of 2024, the bureau was reorganized and renamed **Permitting & Development**. URLs and references that say "BDS" still mostly work and redirect (https://www.portland.gov/ppd).

**BES** (Bureau of Environmental Services): Portland's sewer, stormwater, and watershed bureau. Holds sewer connection, lateral, and as-built records (https://www.portland.gov/bes).

**CO** (Certificate of Occupancy): The official sign-off that says a building is legally finished and approved for the listed use. Often abbreviated "C of O." Issued at the end of the original construction permit and sometimes after major changes (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

**Contributing resource**: A property in a Historic District that adds to the district's historic character because of its age and integrity. The opposite is "non-contributing." Contributing resources face stricter review for exterior changes (Portland City Code 33.846, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846).

**eFiles**: The city's free document portal for completed land use decisions from 2017 and 2020 to the present (https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/Search).

**Historic Resource Review**: The land use review that applies to most exterior changes on contributing resources in Historic Districts (Portland City Code 33.846, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/846).

**IPR** (Information & Public Records request): Generic city term for a public records request. The city's portal is at https://www.portland.gov/prr.

**IVR number** (Inspection Voice Response number): A unique numeric identifier the city assigns to each permit; useful for searching by permit, not address (https://www.portland.gov/bds/find-permit-status-portland-maps).

**Lateral / branch**: The pipe that connects your house to the public sewer main. The "lateral" is the part on private property and the city-owned strip; the "branch" is sometimes used for the city-side portion. Responsibility splits at the curb under Portland City Code 17.32 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32).

**ORS** (Oregon Revised Statutes): Oregon's state laws. The public records law is in ORS 192.311 through 192.478 (https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.311).

**Overlay**: A zoning layer added on top of base zoning that adds extra rules; common Portland overlays include "d" (design), "h" (historic), and environmental overlays. Your address has none beyond the historic district designation (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/portlandmaps-help-guides/portlandmaps-general-search).

**PBOT** (Portland Bureau of Transportation): The city's transportation bureau; holds sidewalk, driveway, curb cut, encroachment, and street tree records (https://www.portland.gov/transportation).

**PCC** (Portland City Code): Portland's local laws (https://www.portland.gov/code).

**PF&R** (Portland Fire & Rescue): The fire bureau; holds heating-oil tank installation/decommissioning permits, fire incident reports, and fire-code violation records (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/public-records).

**PortlandMaps**: The city's free online property map at https://www.portlandmaps.com/. Source of zoning, permits, sewer assets, and most free property information.

**PP&D** (Portland Permitting & Development): The current name of the bureau formerly called BDS; handles building permits, land use reviews, code enforcement, and inspections (https://www.portland.gov/ppd).

**ROW** (Right-of-Way): The strip of land the city owns and controls along streets, including the sidewalk, planting strip, and driveway approach. PBOT's territory (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/development/right-way-improvement-requirements-land-use-reviews-or-building-permits).

**SHPO** (State Historic Preservation Office): Oregon's historic resource agency; maintains the state heritage database that lists your house as the H.R. North Residence (https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=49624).

**UC permit / UR permit**: BES permits for new sewer **C**onnections and lateral **R**epairs in the right-of-way (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/ur-uc-permit-guide).

---

If you want, I can take any specific permit I found above (the 2019 EV charger circuit, the 2013 plumbing/sewer pair, the 2004 water service) and walk you through its full activity log so you can see what was inspected and what wasn't; or I can pre-fill a public records request with the specific historic-resource-review and Certificate of Occupancy items most useful for a Ladd's Addition renovation.

building-records.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know how to request building records for my property at 1719 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214. I'm trying to find out what permits, plans, inspections, and other building history the city has on file for my home — for example, to confirm work was done legally, to plan a renovation, to satisfy a lender or insurance company, or just to understand what I own.

Please walk me through the full process of requesting building records from the City of Portland, including every realistic option (free online lookups, formal records requests, in-person visits, and any paid options), and tell me which path is best depending on why I need the records.

Audience and reading level (this is non-negotiable):
- Write for a Portland resident or small-business owner with no code, permitting, or real-estate background. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but unfamiliar with city processes.
- Lead with a plain-English summary that answers my question directly in 3–5 sentences before any tables, citations, or code language. No property data table at the top. Answer in words first.
- Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it — BDS (Bureau of Development Services), BES (Bureau of Environmental Services), PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation), PP&D (Permitting & Development), PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue), PCC (Portland City Code), PortlandMaps, IPR (Information & Public Records request), CO (Certificate of Occupancy), etc. If you wouldn't use a term over the fence with a neighbor, define it.
- Use everyday words instead of code language wherever possible. Say "the city's online property map" before "PortlandMaps"; say "the official sign-off that a building is legally finished" before "Certificate of Occupancy."
- Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords inside sentences instead of dense tables. Use a table only if you're comparing 3+ records-request options on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above it.
- Translate any dollar figures and timelines into something I can plan around (e.g., "about the cost of a tank of gas," "roughly a long weekend's wait," "a few months"). Mark any step that is free.
- Do not use "shall," "promulgated," "thereunder," or other legal language. Write the way a friendly city employee would explain it to me over coffee.

What I want the answer to cover, beyond just "go to PortlandMaps":

1. **The free, instant options first.** Walk me through how to look up my own property's permit and inspection history on PortlandMaps and the city's online permit search — what I'll actually see, what's usually missing, and what those records do and don't prove.

2. **The formal records request path** — when free lookups aren't enough. Tell me exactly how to file a public records request with the Bureau of Development Services (BDS) and/or other bureaus, what it costs, how long it typically takes, and what I should ask for by name (microfilm, scanned plans, inspection cards, Certificate of Occupancy, etc.).

3. **Microfilm and archived plans.** A lot of older Portland homes have records only on microfilm or paper. Explain how to request those, what scanning fees look like, and the realistic wait time.

4. **What governs all of this.** Cite the specific Portland City Code chapters and Oregon public records statutes that establish my right to these records and the city's response timelines — inline, with full URLs.

5. **Related considerations a knowledgeable friend would flag for a Ladd's Addition home like mine:**
   - **Historic district overlay.** Ladd's Addition is a designated historic district — does that change what records exist or how I should use them? What does that mean if I'm planning future work?
   - **Zoning.** What is the base zoning for SE Ladd Ave (likely R5 or similar) and what overlays apply? Where do I confirm this for free?
   - **Sewer and side-sewer records.** These live with BES, not BDS. Explain how to request a sewer connection record separately and why I might want one.
   - **Transportation/right-of-way records.** PBOT keeps records for sidewalks, driveways, and street trees in the right-of-way (the strip the city owns between the sidewalk and street). Tell me how to request those.
   - **Unpermitted work.** What happens if my records show work that was done without a permit — am I in trouble, and what are my options?
   - **Sequencing.** If I'm doing this to plan a renovation, what should I look up *before* I pay for plans or talk to a contractor?

6. **A numbered "what to do next" list, in the order I should actually do it.** For each step, name the bureau or website, the phone number, what to bring or include, what it costs (mark FREE clearly), and roughly how long it takes. Start with the zero-cost online lookups and escalate from there.

7. **A "what to ask the city" script** I can paste into an email or read aloud on the phone when I contact BDS, BES, or PBOT. Not "call BDS" — the actual sentences I should say, with my address and what I'm asking for filled in.

8. **Common gotchas for Portland residents requesting records**, in plain language. For example: records older than a certain year may not exist; permit records don't prove work was done correctly, only that it was permitted; finding *no* permit isn't the same as proving none was ever issued; historic district approvals are tracked separately, etc.

9. **Friendly accuracy warnings** — things like "zoning and overlays for your specific lot may vary; confirm on PortlandMaps before spending money on plans," framed as a heads-up, not a legal disclaimer.

10. **A glossary at the end** of every acronym and technical term you used more than once (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, PCC, IPR, CO, ROW, overlay, etc.), in case I skipped around.

**Citations — this is the most important rule, please follow it exactly:**

Every single factual claim — every fee, phone number, timeline, code section, contact, URL, rule — must be cited inline, in the same sentence as the claim, with BOTH:

- the exact code section, statute, or administrative rule (e.g., `PCC 3.96.010` or `ORS 192.324`, not just "Portland City Code" or "Title 3"), AND
- a direct, working full `https://...` URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov (or oregon.gov for state law).

Format example: "The city must respond to a public records request as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay (ORS 192.324, https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_192.324)."

I am going to copy your entire answer and paste it as plain text into a document, so:
- Do NOT use footnote markers like "[1]" or "see source above."
- Do NOT use link text like "[here]" or "the city's website" — use the full URL.
- Do NOT put all citations in a "Sources" list at the end and leave the body uncited. A Sources list is fine as a bonus, but every claim in the body must already be cited inline.
- Do NOT cite vaguely to "Portland City Code" or "the BDS website" — give me the specific chapter/section and the specific page URL.
- If you can't find a specific code section or URL for a claim, say so out loud ("I couldn't find a specific code section for this fee — call BDS at 503-823-7300 to confirm") instead of citing vaguely.

Thanks — please answer in plain English first, then give me the steps, then the script, then the gotchas, then the glossary, with full inline citations the whole way through.

col1.response:
I'll research this thoroughly. A pergola sounds simple, but Portland has specific rules that depend on size, location on your lot, and your zoning — and the answer "do I need a permit" hinges on those details. Let me dig in properly.I have the general framework. Now I need to know the specific zoning for the user's property — that determines setbacks, lot coverage limits, and floor area ratio. Let me look up the address on PortlandMaps.Good — I have property facts (4,792 sq ft lot, 810 sq ft 2-bed/2-bath built 1988). Now let me look up the actual zoning from PortlandMaps.Let me try to pull the property's zoning directly from PortlandMaps.The neighborhood mostly shows R5 zoning, with some R2.5 nearby. Let me get the exact zoning for the user's property.I have the property ID format now. Let me pull the user's property record directly.Let me try the city's open data ArcGIS API to get zoning programmatically.Let me get more specific on how Portland treats pergolas — open-roof vs. covered makes a big difference, and so does attached vs. freestanding.Now I have the key distinction clear: open pergolas (just posts and beams) are classified differently from "covered" pergolas. Let me confirm the open-pergola rules, and try one more approach to identify zoning at this exact address.I have everything I need now. Let me write this up. (One note: I tried to pull your exact zoning from PortlandMaps directly but their site didn't return data through my search tools; I'll point you to where to verify it yourself in two clicks.)

---

**Short answer: yes, almost certainly. The design choices you make up front determine whether you can skip a permit entirely or need to file one.**

## The single most useful distinction

Portland classifies pergolas two different ways under Zoning Code 33.110.245, and the rules diverge sharply:

An **open pergola** (posts plus rafters, slats, lattice, or trellis material with no solid roof) is a *"detached uncovered vertical structure"*; the same category as trellises and arbors. Lightly regulated. It does not count toward your building coverage (which only counts impervious overhead structures), and it does not count toward floor area ratio (FAR).

A **covered pergola** (polycarbonate panels, fabric canopy, louvered aluminum that closes, shingled mini-roof) is a *"detached covered accessory structure"*; the same category as a shed or carport. It triggers building coverage and the 20-ft height cap, and you'll need to keep it inside the buildable area unless it qualifies for a setback exception.

This is the lever most homeowners don't realize they're pulling.

## The permit-free thresholds (Oregon state code, applied by Portland)

For a freestanding pergola: no building permit if it's ≤ 200 sq ft and ≤ 15 ft tall.

For a pergola attached to your house and covering a patio: no building permit if it's ≤ 200 sq ft, average roof height ≤ 12 ft, and at least 3 ft from any property line.

200 sq ft is roughly 14×14 or 12×16; plenty for a dining-and-lounging space. Important caveat: skipping the *building* permit doesn't skip *zoning*. Setbacks, building coverage, and FAR still apply, and you may still need a small zoning permit if the structure changes the building footprint.

## Setbacks for your property

R5 zone (the most common designation around your address) typically requires 10 ft front, 5 ft side, 5 ft rear for primary structures, and accessory structures can sometimes go closer to side and rear lot lines if certain conditions are met. R2.5 (also present in your area) is more permissive.

A useful loophole: an *open* pergola counted as an "uncovered vertical structure" is allowed inside required building setbacks if it's no larger than 3 ft in width/depth and no taller than 8 ft. So a slim, traditional pergola can hug a fence line. Bigger than that and the full setback rules apply. There's also a specific carve-out allowing a single arbor up to 6 ft wide, 3 ft deep, and 8 ft tall in the front setback (probably not what you want, but good to know).

## Your specific situation, things to flag

Your lot is 4,792 sq ft, just under the 5,000 sq ft R5 standard, so the FAR math is tight. The 2022 listing for your home mentions a "new backyard shed" already on the property; that shed counts as an existing accessory structure and eats into both the 20% accessory-structure-coverage cap and your overall FAR allowance. Worth measuring before you size the pergola.

Also worth knowing: there's a one-time 250 sq ft FAR exception per 5-year period for properties whose primary structure passed final inspection at least 5 years ago. Your house was built in 1988, so this is available if you ever need it for something more substantial.

## Verify your exact zoning before committing

I tried to pull your zoning directly but couldn't reach the live data from where I sit. You're looking for the base zone (likely R5 or R2.5) and any overlay letters (like "a", "d", or others). Overlays can change setback or design rules. **Free 15-minute zoning consultation.** Portland Permitting & Development offers a free 15-minute appointment with a planner who can confirm your zoning, setbacks, and whether your specific design needs a permit. They'll also tell you whether your block has any plan-district quirks.

You get shade from rafter spacing, climbing plants, or a removable shade cloth, while the structure stays in the lightly-regulated category. You can always add a removable canopy later for events. Scott-Arleta and doesn't appear to have an HOA based on typical patterns for that neighborhood, but check your title docs for any recorded covenants from the original 1988 subdivision. Permit-free at the city level doesn't mean permit-free at the deed level.

**Practical sequence.** Verify zoning on PortlandMaps, measure existing shed footprint, sketch a 12×16 or 14×14 open pergola sited at least 5 ft from side/rear lot lines, then call PP&D for the free 15-min consult to confirm. If everything checks out, you can build without a building permit; just keep it open-roof and within the thresholds above.

A traditional pergola with an open-slat / lattice top is classified as a *detached uncovered vertical structure* (Portland code calls these "trellises, arbors and other garden structures"). If you dig footings near where a tree used to be, or the pergola location intersects an existing tree's root protection zone, that record could resurface.
