## What to ask the city: ready-to-paste scripts

**Email script for PP&D Septic Program (paste to septic@portlandoregon.gov)**

> Subject: Records and decommissioning question for 6624 N Missouri Ave, Portland OR 97217
>
> Hi,
>
> I'm the resident at 6624 N Missouri Ave, Portland, OR 97217 (Multnomah County tax property R232318). I'm trying to figure out whether my property still has an active cesspool, or whether the cesspool was already legally decommissioned when the house was connected to the public sewer.
>
> When I look up the property on PortlandMaps, I see two historic "BES Connection" plumbing records on file. Can you confirm whether either of those records reflects a completed cesspool-to-sewer conversion, and whether a decommissioning permit was previously issued and inspected?
>
> If the cesspool has not yet been formally decommissioned, I'd like to start a Septic Decommissioning Permit application. Please send me the application form and let me know the next steps.
>
> Thanks very much.

**Phone script for BES / PP&D Utilities Engineering (call 503-823-7761)**

> Hi, I have a question about a single-family house at **6624 N Missouri Ave, 97217**. I think there's a cesspool on the property and I'd like to confirm three things.
>
> First, is this property currently billed for sanitary sewer service, or is it still on a private system?
>
> Second, when I look on PortlandMaps I can see a public combined sewer main about 90 feet away in N Missouri Ave with a manhole tagged unit ID AAK484. Can you confirm whether a sewer connection from my property to that main has already been made and inspected?
>
> Third, if I do need to make a new connection because of a failed cesspool, would I owe full residential System Development Charges, given that the post-July-2024 code only applies SDCs to "new or increased demand" and I wouldn't be adding any new fixtures? I'd appreciate getting the answer in writing if possible.
>
> Thanks for your help.

## Common gotchas (specific to Portland cesspool conversions)

**Old plumbing records say "Decommissioned" but the cesspool is still there.** "Decommissioned" historically meant "abandoned" not "physically filled in," per the PDX Additions account; the brick pit can still be sitting underground (https://pdxadditions.com/2017/08/decommissioning-a-cesspool-in-portland/). If you're doing any future addition or major landscaping, you may need to physically locate, fully fill, and inspect that cesspool before construction.

**Dual cesspools (sanitary plus rain gutter "drywells").** Many old Portland homes have separate cesspool-style pits for rain gutter drainage, also brick-lined and identical-looking; these are often still in use and are not always covered by your sanitary decommissioning (https://pdxadditions.com/2017/08/decommissioning-a-cesspool-in-portland/). If you find one in your yard, ask whether it's still active stormwater infrastructure before touching it.

**Choosing a contractor based on price alone.** BES specifically warns "the lowest bid is not necessarily the best bid" and recommends checking CCB history for complaints (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475). A bad sewer contractor can leave you with a backfilled trench that settles, a leaky tie-in that the city flags during the next sewer line camera survey, or a permit that never gets final-inspected and lingers as an open issue at sale.

**The "party sewer" trap.** If your sewer lateral runs across a neighbor's property or shares a line with another house, you may be on a **nonconforming sewer** under PCC 17.32.020 and may be required to convert to your own conforming connection under PCC 17.33.030.C (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020 and https://www.portland.gov/code/17/33/030). The portal's lateral records at distance zero from your parcel are worth confirming aren't a shared line. ENB-4.27 governs nonconforming sewer conversion (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-427-administrative-rules-bureau).

**Permit expiration.** UR and UC permits expire 180 days after issue, with one extension of up to 180 more days available before expiration only (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits). If your contractor stalls and the permit lapses you may have to re-apply and re-pay.

**Open permit at sale time.** A common nasty surprise during a real estate transaction is an unfinaled plumbing or decommissioning permit. Make sure each permit gets its **Final Inspection Approved** stamp before considering the project complete.

**Tree roots and street trees.** If your sewer lateral passes near a street tree, the work may require a tree permit from Portland Urban Forestry, separate from the UC permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

---

## Verify before you spend money

**Pull the actual permit cards before paying anyone.** I gave you property R232318 with two BES Connection records on file, but the metadata could mean a completed connection, abandoned application, or partial work. Don't pay a contractor until you've seen the actual scanned documents at https://www.portlandmaps.com or in person at 1900 SW 4th Ave (https://www.cozyhomeinspection.com/cesspools.html).

**Confirm zoning and overlays at PortlandMaps.** Your zoning summary at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/zoning/ shows R5 base, no plan district, no historic overlay, no environmental overlay. Verify this hasn't changed before paying for plans or contractors; overlays can add design constraints and review fees that materially change project cost.

**Confirm your contractor's CCB status the same week you sign.** Licenses can lapse. Run their CCB number at https://search.ccb.state.or.us/search/ and check insurance and bond status (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475).

**Get the SDC determination in writing.** As noted, whether your conversion triggers full SDCs under post-July-2024 rules is not crystal clear from the public-facing materials; ask BES for a written determination at https://www.portland.gov/bes/full-list-environmental-services-rates-and-charges before you finalize loan paperwork.

**Don't trust contractor permit-fee estimates without checking the city fee schedule.** Permit fees change every July 1; the FY2025-26 rate ordinance is at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/documents/fy-2025-26-sewer-and-stormwater-charges-and-fees/download.

**Be skeptical of "we'll just decommission it for cash" offers.** Skipping the permit means: no DEQ-required pump receipt, no inspection, no record of decommissioning, and a future buyer's inspector or the city's records system will eventually catch it. Title issues and post-sale lawsuits are far more expensive than the $935 permit fee.

## Glossary

**BDS / Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)**: Bureau of Development Services, now operating as Portland Permitting & Development. Issues building, plumbing, mechanical, and septic decommissioning permits on private property. Phone: 503-823-7300. Web: https://www.portland.gov/ppd.

**BES**: Bureau of Environmental Services. Runs the public sewer pipes, stormwater facilities, and treatment plants for the City of Portland. Issues permits for work on public sewer infrastructure. Phone: 503-823-7761. Web: https://www.portland.gov/bes.

**Cesspool**: A buried pit, often brick-lined, that takes raw sewage from a house and lets the liquid seep into surrounding soil. Banned for new construction in Oregon under OAR 340-071-0335 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0335).

**Combined sewer**: A sewer pipe that carries both sanitary sewage (from toilets and sinks) and stormwater runoff (from streets and roofs) in the same pipe, defined in PCC 17.32.020 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020). Your N Missouri Ave sewer is combined.

**Decommissioning**: The official process of shutting down and filling in an old cesspool, septic tank, or seepage pit so it's no longer a hazard. Required by OAR 340-071-0185 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0185).

**DEQ**: Oregon Department of Environmental Quality. State agency that writes Oregon's onsite wastewater rules under OAR Chapter 340, Division 71 (https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=1479) and delegates day-to-day permitting to local agents like the City of Portland.

**DFU (Drainage Fixture Unit)**: A standardized unit for measuring how much wastewater a plumbing fixture produces. Used to calculate sanitary SDCs under PCC 17.36 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/36).

**E-zone (Environmental Overlay)**: A zoning overlay used in Portland to protect natural resources like streams, wetlands, and wildlife habitat. Your address has none, per https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/6624-N-MISSOURI-AVE/R232318_did/zoning/.

**Lateral / side sewer**: The pipe that runs from your house to the city sewer line in the street, also called a building sewer. The portion on your private property is your responsibility; the portion in the public right-of-way has split responsibility between the city and the property owner per PCC 17.32 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32).

**Nonconforming sewer**: A private sewer system that crosses property lines, drains more than one property, or doesn't meet current city standards. Defined in PCC 17.32.020 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020) and governed for conversion by ENB-4.27 (https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-427-administrative-rules-bureau).

**OAR**: Oregon Administrative Rule. State-level rules issued by Oregon agencies; cesspool and septic rules are in OAR Chapter 340, Division 71 (https://secure.sos.state.or.us/oard/displayDivisionRules.action?selectedDivision=1479).

**PBOT**: Portland Bureau of Transportation. Owns and inspects the public right-of-way (streets, sidewalks). Inspects your sewer connection work in the street under the UC permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

**PCC**: Portland City Code. The City of Portland's local laws, indexed at https://www.portland.gov/code.

**Right-of-way (ROW)**: The strip of public land between your house and the street, including the sidewalk, planting strip, and the street itself. Defined in PCC 17.32.020 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/32/020). Work in the ROW requires a permit.

**SDC (System Development Charge)**: One-time fees the city charges to "hook into" their utility system, intended to recover a property's share of the cost of building and operating major sewer and stormwater facilities. Governed by PCC 17.36 and ENB-4.05 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/36 and https://www.portland.gov/policies/environment-built/sewer-stormwater-erosion-control/enb-405-system-development-charges).

**Seepage pit**: A type of onsite disposal pit similar to a cesspool but used after a septic tank as the absorption component. New seepage pits can replace failing cesspools or seepage pits only on lots too small for standard septic systems, per OAR 340-071-0335 (https://oregon.public.law/rules/oar_340-071-0335).

**Septic tank**: A watertight tank that holds sewage from a house, lets solids settle and decompose, and discharges treated liquid effluent to a drainfield. Different from a cesspool, which has no separate tank-and-drainfield distinction.

**UR / UC permit**: Utility Repair (UR) or Utility Connection (UC) permit issued by BES to allow work on the sewer in the public right-of-way. UR is for repairs to existing pipes; UC is for new connections (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/infrastructure/ur-and-uc-permits).

Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it (cesspool, decommissioning, BES, BDS, PCC, DEQ, ROW, SDC, lateral, side sewer, etc.). Use everyday words wherever possible — say "the pipe that runs from my house to the city sewer line in the street" before you say "side sewer lateral", and say "fees the city charges to hook into their pipes" before "System Development Charges." Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords in sentences instead of dense tables. Only use a table if you're comparing 3+ real options on the same factors, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table. No citations, no jargon, no tables in that opening. Just the answer the way a knowledgeable neighbor would tell me over the fence.

Then go deeper and cover the things I probably didn't know to ask:

- **The rule itself.** Is connecting to City sewer mandatory when a cesspool fails in Portland, or are there situations where I can repair, replace with a septic system, or do something else instead? Who decides what counts as "failed"? What role does the **Oregon Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ)** play vs. the City of Portland? What role does the **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES — the city bureau that runs sewers and stormwater)** play vs. the **Bureau of Development Services (BDS — the city bureau that issues building and plumbing permits)**?
- **Which Titles of Portland City Code govern this** — likely Title 17 (Public Improvements), Title 25 (Plumbing), and possibly Title 29 (Property Maintenance), plus any administrative rules on cesspool decommissioning. Tell me what each Title actually controls in plain English next to the number.
- **What's special about my specific address.** 6624 N Missouri Ave is in the **Overlook / Arbor Lodge area of North Portland**. Is there a city sewer line already running in N Missouri Ave that I can connect to, or would I need a longer connection? Are there any zoning overlays, environmental overlays (like the **e-zone / environmental overlay**), historic overlays, or stormwater requirements that affect this property? Tell me how to verify this myself on **PortlandMaps (https://www.portlandmaps.com)** in 2 minutes.
- **What permits I'll likely need and what triggers each one** — the cesspool decommissioning permit, the sewer connection permit, any plumbing permit, any right-of-way (ROW — the strip of public land between my house and the street, including the sidewalk and the street itself) permit if the work crosses the sidewalk or street. Who issues each permit, and roughly what does each one cost?
- **Phasing and sequencing.** What order do these things have to happen in? (E.g., do I have to get the sewer connected and inspected before the cesspool can legally be decommissioned, or the other way around?) What can I do in parallel vs. what has to be sequential?
- **Real cost and timeline ranges** for a typical Portland single-family home doing this — including the connection fee, **System Development Charges (SDCs — one-time fees the city charges to hook into their utility system)**, contractor cost, decommissioning cost, and any financial assistance programs like the BES sewer connection loan or hardship program if those exist.
- **Who I should actually contact**, with phone numbers, email addresses, and web pages — BES, BDS, DEQ, and any specific program (like a cesspool/sewer connection program or a financial assistance program). Tell me which one to call *first*.
- **Common mistakes Portland homeowners make on cesspool failures** — picking the wrong contractor, hiring someone who isn't licensed for decommissioning, missing the DEQ filing, paying SDCs they could have avoided or deferred, doing work in the right-of-way without a permit, etc.

Then give me these specific sections, in this order:

1. **A numbered "What to do next" list, in the order I should actually do it.** Each step should say: who to call or what website to visit, what to bring or have ready, roughly what it costs (mark **FREE** clearly when it's free), and roughly how long it takes. Step 1 should be something I can do today from my couch.

2. **A "What to ask the city" script** I can literally read aloud on the phone or paste into an email — one short script for BES and one short script for BDS, with the actual sentences to say, including my address and what I'm trying to find out. Not "call BES" — the actual words.

3. **A "Common gotchas" section in plain language** — the specific traps Portland homeowners fall into on cesspool-to-sewer conversions.

4. **A "Verify before you spend money" section** with friendly warnings (not legal disclaimers) — things like "zoning and sewer availability for your exact address can differ from what's typical for the neighborhood; check PortlandMaps and call BES before paying for any plans or contractors."

5. **A glossary at the end** defining every acronym and technical term you used more than once (cesspool, decommissioning, septic, lateral / side sewer, BES, BDS, DEQ, PCC, SDC, ROW, e-zone, etc.), in case I jumped to a section.

**Citation rules — this is the most important part, please follow it exactly:**

Do not use words like "shall", "thereunder", "promulgated", or other legal language — write the way a friendly city employee would explain this to me over coffee.

Let me check Portland's accessory structure rules and then circle back to the truss question.Let me verify Portland's design loads and the actual truss spacing standards before I synthesize.Let me quickly verify the zoning of this specific lot and Portland's snow load.I have enough now. Let me put together a visualization showing the actual lot/code constraint, which is the key issue here.Visualization timed out, so I'll deliver this in prose. Here's what I found, both for your direct question and for the bigger issue lurking behind it.

## Direct answer: max truss spacing for a 40' span

For a 40' clear-span pole barn, the practical maximums are:

- **8' on center**: most common with single trusses; engineered prefab trusses for post-frame are typically rated up to 9' OC max as single members
- **12' on center**: doable with **double (two-ply) trusses** sitting on each post, with 2x6 or 2x8 purlins on edge between trusses (joist-hung)
- **14' on center**: top end you'll see with metal/steel trusses
- **Beyond that**: requires custom engineering and beefier secondary framing

The "max" is performance-based, not prescriptive. A 40' span is engineered territory; the answer depends on your snow load (25 psf roof design in Portland; 20 psf + 5 psf rain-on-snow), wind pressure (18 psf design ASD), seismic category (Portland is **SDC D**, which does matter for tall, lightly-framed pole structures), and what's hanging off the bottom chord (open ceiling vs. drywall vs. storage loft).

If you want to put a loft in the bottom chord or hang drywall, that drives spacing tighter (often back to 4' OC for drywall without strapping).

## The bigger problem your question is sitting on top of

Your property is **0.18 acres** (~7,840 sq ft) with an existing 1,082 sq ft house, on a lot that's almost certainly zoned **R5** (5,000 sq ft min lot). Portland's accessory structure rules in **PCC 33.110.245** are going to be the binding constraint long before truss engineering becomes the issue:

**Hard limits you'll run into:**

1. **20% lot coverage cap for all detached accessory structures combined.** On your lot, that's a maximum of about **1,568 sq ft total**. A 40' x 40' pole barn = 1,600 sq ft, which is already over. A 40' x 30' = 1,200 sq ft, which fits but eats nearly your entire accessory-structure budget (no future shed, ADU, or garage).

2. **20-foot height max** outside setbacks (15' inside setbacks, with 10' max wall height inside setbacks). A 40' span with a 4/12 pitch and 10' walls puts you at ~16.7' to ridge (works). A 6/12 pitch with 10' walls hits 20' exactly (no margin for a cupola, vent, or measurement variance). Steeper pitches that shed Pacific Northwest rain better will not fit.

3. **Setbacks** in R5: typically 5' side and 5' rear (and the structure must be behind the rear wall of your house, or at least 40' from the front lot line). If your lot is roughly 50' wide (typical for 0.18 ac in this area), a 40' wide pole barn leaves you exactly 5' on each side; one survey-pin surprise and you're filing an Adjustment.

4. **Permit required.** Anything over 200 sq ft non-habitable needs a building permit, and at a 40' clear span you're firmly in **engineered design** territory (you can't use the ORSC prescriptive path). Plan on stamped drawings from an Oregon-licensed engineer; truss manufacturer drawings alone are not "engineered building plans."

5. **Use matters.** "Workshop" as a hobby/storage space is fine as accessory use. If any of your Yex Labs work, hardware tinkering for sale, or anything with customers happens in there, that crosses into **Home Occupation Permit** territory (PCC 33.203) with its own rules about square footage devoted to the business, customer visits, signage, and so on.

## What I'd actually recommend you do, in order

1. **Pull your exact zoning and lot dimensions on PortlandMaps before sketching anything.** Search your address, click into the Zoning section, and screenshot the base zone, any overlays (this neighborhood has had stormwater and tree canopy overlays in places), and the exact lot dimensions. The 20% rule and setbacks key off those numbers.

2. **Re-think the footprint, not the trusses.** A 40' x 30' (1,200 sq ft) at 8' OC truss spacing with a 4/12 pitch is the largest building that comfortably works under R5 accessory rules and gives you a real workshop. If you want more usable space, going **taller within the 20' envelope** (full 10' walls + an attic-truss design for storage) buys you more functional area than going wider. If you do not actually need a 40' clear span, dropping to a 30' or 32' span opens up the height/pitch budget and shrinks footprint pressure.

3. **Schedule a free 15-minute Portland city planner appointment** (503-823-7300) before paying anyone for plans. They will tell you exactly what you can and can't do on this specific lot in about 10 minutes, including any overlays I can't see from here. This is the single highest-leverage hour you can spend.

4. **For the engineering, get a kit quote from MWBSC out of Canby** (mentioned in one of my sources as serving Oregon, with in-house engineering) or **Hansen Pole Buildings** for an apples-to-apples bid; both will produce stamped, Oregon-specific plans that handle your seismic + snow + wind combo without you having to herd an engineer separately.

5. **Sanity-check with neighbors / aerial.** Open Google Maps satellite view on your block; if you don't see other detached structures of comparable size on similar lots, that's a signal that the code constraints I described above are the reason.

## Honest caveat

I cross-referenced PCC 33.110, the ADU/accessory worksheet from the Bureau of Development Services, and the Oregon Residential and Structural Specialty Codes, but I'm working from a desk and have not seen your lot in person, your survey, or any overlays specific to your address. Treat this as a strong starting framework, not a permit-ready answer; the planner appointment will replace my best-guess on lot width and overlays with real numbers.

If you want, I can sketch out the height/footprint envelope as a quick scale diagram once we have your actual lot dimensions, or draft the email to PBOT/BDS to schedule that planner appointment.

The short answer to your stated question is buried under a much bigger one, so I want to surface it.

## The truss spacing answer

For a 40' clearspan post-frame building, **8' on-center is the industry default and 12' OC is the practical maximum** for a single-truss design. Beyond 12' you start needing double trusses (paired together at each post) with 2x6 or 2x8 purlins on edge between them; 14-16' bay spacing is doable that way. Stick-frame standard is 24" OC, but pole barns aren't governed by that prescriptive rule.

Important caveat: a 40' span is not in IRC's prescriptive tables. Post-frame falls under performance-based code (ANSI/ASABE EP 484.3 / ICC AC322), so **whatever spacing you pick has to come back from a truss manufacturer with sealed engineered drawings** for your specific snow, wind, and seismic loads. Portland is light on snow (~25 psf ground) but the building also has to cover the Cascadia + Portland Hills Fault seismic risk that shows up on this exact parcel as "Very Strong" expected shaking. The truss designer takes care of that math; you just need to give them the right inputs.

If your goal is the cheapest path: 8' OC, single trusses, 2x4 purlins flat. If your goal is open interior with fewer obstructions: 12' OC, single trusses, 2x6 purlins on edge in joist hangers. Above 12' OC the cost savings on trusses get eaten by heavier purlins and column hardware, so most builders top out there.

## The bigger question you didn't ask

I pulled the parcel data for 450 NE 103rd. **Your lot is 0.18 acres (about 79' × 102'), zoned R2.5, with a 1,082 sq ft house from 1951 already on it.** That changes the conversation, because Portland rewrote the accessory structure rules in 2021 and they're tight inside city limits. Specifically:

**Height ceiling: 20 feet total.** Detached accessory structures (workshops included) max out at 20' to peak; this dropped from the old 30-35' limit. On a 40' span at usable eave heights (12-14'), you're forced into a low-pitch roof: roughly 4/12 if you want a 12' eave, or 3/12 for a 14' eave. Anything steeper and you blow through 20'.

**Compatibility design standards kick in at 15'.** Once you're over 15' tall, siding has to be in a shingle pattern, or horizontal clapboard/shiplap with boards 6" or less wide. So no big plain metal panels on a 16'-tall workshop; you'd need real residential-style cladding.

**20% lot coverage cap on detached accessory structures.** 20% × 7,840 sq ft = **1,568 sq ft maximum footprint** for the workshop. Some quick math:
- 40' × 40' = 1,600 sq ft → over the limit
- 40' × 39' = 1,560 sq ft → just fits
- 40' × 30' = 1,200 sq ft → comfortable

**Setbacks (R2.5, accessory structure outside setbacks):** 10' front, 5' side, 5' rear. The structure also has to sit behind the rear wall of the house OR at least 40' from the front lot line, whichever applies. With the house likely eating ~36-40' of depth from the front, your buildable zone for the workshop is roughly the back 50-55' of the lot. A 40' span building with its long axis running east-west fits, but barely.

## What I'd actually do next

The best $0-spent move here is the **free 15-minute appointment with a Portland city planner** (503-823-7300). Bring your sketched footprint and confirm the building coverage math, the specific Table 110-4 setbacks for your exact configuration, whether the over-15' design standards exempt non-habitable workshops, and whether your existing house's footprint affects "established building lines" for your accessory.

Two sanity checks worth running before you commit to 40' span:

1. **Do you actually need 40'?** A 30' span (very common for shops) drops your truss cost meaningfully, opens up the height envelope for a steeper pitch within the 20' limit, and gives you a building that's far easier to fit on this lot. A 30' x 40' shop with 12' OC trusses on a 5/12 pitch and 12' eave comes in around 17' total height and 1,200 sq ft. That clears every constraint with room to spare.

2. **Get the truss quote first, plans second.** Call MWBSC in Canby (the Oregon post-frame outfit mentioned in builder forums) or Hansen Pole Buildings; both will sell engineered packages that include sealed truss drawings sized for Portland's load conditions. The price delta between 8' and 12' OC is usually under 15% of the truss line item, so it's not the cost lever people think it is.

One thing to flag on the source quality: forum posts and pole barn vendor blogs converge cleanly on the 8-12' OC range, which is reassuring, but they're not authoritative for code compliance. The Portland zoning numbers above come from Title 33 directly (portland.gov/code/33), and the 20-ft / 15-ft / 20% figures are from the city's own Detached Covered Accessory Structures worksheet. Anything you build needs the city's permit reviewer signing off, not a forum.

---

# Building a workshop at 450 NE 103rd Ave, Portland, OR 97220

## 1. Plain-English answer to the truss question

There is no "Portland says X feet" number for truss spacing. Portland follows Oregon's residential building code, which simply says trusses have to be engineered to carry the snow and wind loads, and the **truss maker's engineer** is the one who picks the spacing. For a 40-foot clear span workshop in Portland, the standard answer is **4 feet on center** because that's what almost every truss company defaults to and it sails through inspection; 8 feet on center is real, common in true pole barns, and totally legal in Portland, but it requires beefier trusses plus a layer of 2x4 or 2x6 purlins running the long way across the top to hold up your roofing; 10 feet on center is at the edge of what's reasonable for a hobby shop and you'd want to be sure your truss engineer is comfortable with it. So tell your truss supplier "40-foot span, 25 pounds per square foot snow load, Portland wind, what's the cheapest way to do this" and let them come back with stamped drawings; whatever spacing those stamped drawings specify is the spacing Portland will approve.

**One thing nobody told you yet, and this is important:** your lot at 450 NE 103rd is small (about 0.18 acres, so roughly 7,840 square feet) with an existing 1,082 square foot house, and Portland's zoning code caps a single detached accessory building at the size of your primary house's footprint, so a **1,200 square foot workshop is not allowed on this lot**. You're capped at roughly **1,082 square feet**, which means a 40 by 27 foot building works, but a 40 by 30 doesn't. Details and the code citation are in Section 4 below.

## 2. What to do next, in order

Each step lists who to talk to, what to bring, rough cost, rough time, and whether it's free.

**Step 1 — Confirm zoning yourself, then with a real human at the city. (FREE)**
Pull up your property at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/ and skim the "Permits & Zoning" section to confirm what I'm telling you below: base zone **R2.5**, plan district **East Corridor**, no overlays, no flood plain, no landslide hazard. Then book a **free 15-minute zoning question appointment** with a city planner at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/free-15-min-appointments and confirm the workshop sizing limits in person. Bring a rough site sketch (just hand-drawn) showing the existing house, the lot lines with rough dimensions, and where you want to put the workshop. **Cost: $0. Time: 15-minute appointment, usually bookable within a week or two.**

**Step 2 — Get a property survey or pull your plat. (Sometimes free, sometimes $500-$1,500)**
Before you spend money on a stamped truss design, you need to know exactly where your property lines are. Pull the plat for free from PortlandMaps property detail or the Multnomah County Surveyor's office at https://multco.us/dart/county-surveyor. If the plat is unclear or the lot has been adjusted at any point, hire a licensed surveyor to set or confirm corner pins. **Cost: $0 if the plat is clear; $500-$1,500 for a corner survey, roughly the cost of a decent set of tires. Time: 1-3 weeks for a surveyor.**

**Step 3 — Decide on building size and structural approach.**
Based on Step 1, plan for a workshop of **no more than ~1,080 square feet**, which gives you 40 by 27 feet, or you could do 36 by 30, or any combination under that footprint cap. Decide whether you want stock trusses (cheaper, faster) or stamped engineered post-frame plans (better for non-standard wide spacing). **Cost: $0 to plan; truss quotes are free. Time: 1-2 weeks of phone calls.**

**Step 4 — Get truss/structural plans. ($800-$3,500)**
Either order pre-engineered trusses from a local truss plant (they ship with stamped drawings included), or hire a post-frame package supplier (Mule-Hide, Lester Buildings, MQS, or a local Oregon supplier) for a stamped pole-barn package, or hire an Oregon-licensed structural engineer to stamp a custom design. **Cost: stamped trusses are usually included free with the truss order; a full stamped post-frame package adds $1,500-$3,500 to the materials cost (about the price of a used motorcycle); a custom engineer-stamped design from scratch runs $1,500-$5,000. Time: 2-6 weeks.**

**Step 5 — Apply for the building permit through Portland's online system. ($1,500-$3,500 in fees)**
Go to https://www.portland.gov/ppd and apply through Portland Permitting & Development's online portal. You'll submit a site plan, foundation plan, framing plan, stamped truss drawings, and an erosion control plan. **Cost: rough estimate $1,500-$3,500 in city fees for a ~1,000 square foot workshop with no plumbing (about the cost of a high-end laptop, less than a used car). Time: 4-8 weeks of plan review for a residential accessory; can be faster on a simple application.**

**Step 6 — Pull electrical permit separately. ($150-$400)**
Electrical permits in Oregon are pulled separately from the structural permit, by a licensed electrician (or by you if you sign as the homeowner-occupant). **Cost: $150-$400 for a workshop sub-panel; about a tank of gas plus a nice dinner. Time: same-day issuance if filed online.**

**Step 7 — Build, with required inspections.**
Required inspections typically include footing/foundation, framing, electrical rough-in, electrical final, and final building. Schedule each one through the IVR system or the online portal. **Cost: included in your permit fee. Time: 4-12 weeks of construction, depending on whether you DIY or hire it out.**

## 3. Scripts you can read out loud

These are the actual sentences. Copy and paste; the parts in brackets are the only things you change.

**Script A — call to BDS / Portland Permitting & Development zoning line, 503-823-7300, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact**

> "Hi, my name is Chris and I own 450 NE 103rd Avenue, 97220. The property ID is R319825. I want to build a detached workshop on the lot, no plumbing, no kitchen, no bedroom; just a hobby shop with electricity. The lot is zoned R2.5 with no overlays per PortlandMaps. Two questions. First, can you confirm in writing that under PCC 33.110.245.C.3.b the maximum footprint for my single detached accessory structure is the building coverage of my existing house, which is about 1,082 square feet according to the assessor? Second, can you confirm the required setbacks under Table 110-4 for R2.5 are 10 feet front, 5 feet sides, and 5 feet rear, and that the maximum accessory structure height is 20 feet under 33.110.245.C.1? I'd like to schedule a free 15-minute appointment with a planner if it'd help nail this down."

**Script B — call to BDS structural / building inspection line, 503-823-7300, https://www.portland.gov/ppd**

> "Hi, I'm planning a roughly 40-by-27-foot detached workshop on a residential lot in the R2.5 zone. Single story, non-habitable, no kitchen, no bedroom, just heated space and outlets. I'm looking at a post-frame / pole-barn style with engineered roof trusses spanning the full 40 feet. Two questions. First, will the project be reviewed under the Oregon Residential Specialty Code R105.2 or under the structural code? Second, for a 40-foot truss span, do you need the truss spacing to be at any particular default like 24 inches, or is it whatever the manufacturer's stamped truss drawings specify? I want to make sure my plans match what your structural reviewer expects to see."

**Script C — call to the Bureau of Environmental Services stormwater line, 503-823-7761, https://www.portland.gov/bes**

> "Hi, I'm planning a roughly 1,000 to 1,080 square foot detached workshop on a residential lot at 450 NE 103rd Avenue. The lot is in the Inverness combined sewer basin per PortlandMaps. New roof area will be the only new impervious surface. Will I trigger the Stormwater Management Manual based on the 1,000 square foot impervious threshold, and if so what are the simplest on-site management options for a single-family residential lot, like a drywell, soakage trench, or downspout disconnection?"

**Script D — call to BDS to ask about permit fees, 503-823-7300**

> "I'm planning a roughly 1,000 square foot detached residential workshop, post-frame construction, no plumbing, electrical only. I'd like a fee estimate. Can you walk me through what to expect for the building permit fee, plan review fee, the residential development services fee, the 12 percent state surcharge, the erosion control fee, and the stormwater plan review fee? And can you confirm whether Parks, Transportation, BES, and Water System Development Charges apply to a non-habitable accessory structure with no new dwelling unit?"

## 4. The detailed answer, by topic

### 4.1 Zoning at 450 NE 103rd Ave (this is the part that limits your size)

Your property at 450 NE 103rd Ave is in the **R2.5** base zone, also called **Residential 2,500**, which is Portland's smallest single-dwelling lot zone (PCC 33.110.020, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/020 and confirmed at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/). It sits in the **East Corridor (EC) plan district**, which is a non-restrictive overlay focused on transit corridors and doesn't add hard rules for backyard workshops (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/plandistrict/EC). There are **no environmental overlay zones**, **no historic district**, **no conservation district**, **no special flood hazard area**, and **no mapped landslide or potential landslide hazard area** on your property (https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/). That's actually good news; it means you can skip a lot of the Portland-specific complexity that hits properties in the West Hills or near streams. The lot is **0.18 acres or roughly 7,840 square feet**, and your existing house has a footprint of about **1,082 square feet** built in 1951.

A workshop is a **detached accessory structure** under Portland's zoning code (PCC 33.110.245, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/245), and detached accessory structures are allowed by right in the R2.5 zone as long as you stay within the rules. The four rules that bite on a small lot like yours, in plain English:

The **maximum height** for any detached accessory building is **20 feet** measured from the lowest grade within 5 feet of the building to the midpoint of a sloped roof (PCC 33.110.245.C.1, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/245).

The **building has to sit at least 10 feet back from the front property line** (the side facing NE 103rd Ave), at least **5 feet from each side property line**, and at least **5 feet from the rear property line**, per Table 110-4 in the R2.5 column (PCC 33.110.220.B and Table 110-4, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/220).

The **combined building coverage of all detached accessory structures** on the lot can't exceed **20 percent of the lot area**, which on your 7,840 square foot lot is about **1,568 square feet** (PCC 33.110.245.C.3.a, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/245).

The single most important rule, and the one that limits you to about 1,080 square feet: **each individual detached accessory structure can't have a footprint bigger than the building coverage of your smallest primary structure on the lot, OR 900 square feet, whichever is greater** (PCC 33.110.245.C.3.b, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/245). Your house is 1,082 square feet, so your single workshop maximum footprint is **1,082 square feet**. A 40 by 27 = 1,080 sf works; a 40 by 30 = 1,200 sf is over the cap and would need an Adjustment, which is a discretionary review costing time and money you don't want to spend.

There's also a **lot-wide building coverage cap** (all buildings on the lot, including the house) of about **2,676 square feet** for your 7,840 sq ft lot, calculated as 2,250 + 15% of lot area over 5,000 sq ft (PCC 33.110.225 Table 110-5, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/225); 1,082 + 1,080 = 2,162 sf, so you've got room under that cap.

There's a **side-and-rear-setback exception** in 33.110.245.C.2.b (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/245) that lets you build closer to the side or rear property lines, but only if the structure is no more than 24 feet by 24 feet (576 sf), no more than 15 feet tall with walls under 10 feet, and at least 40 feet from the front line. That's a "small shed" exception, not useful for a 40-foot wide workshop.

### 4.2 The truss / structure question, in detail

Your workshop, as a **non-habitable detached accessory building** to a one- or two-family dwelling, is reviewed under the **Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC)**, the residential building code Oregon adopts and Portland enforces (https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/pages/residential-structures.aspx). Portland references this through Title 24 of the City Code (PCC 24.10, https://www.portland.gov/code/24).

ORSC **Section R802.10** says wood roof trusses "shall be designed, manufactured, and installed to comply with approved standards" and that **truss design drawings prepared by the manufacturer must be supplied to the building official and approved prior to installation** (ORSC R802.10, https://up.codes/viewer/oregon/irc-2021/chapter/8/roof-ceiling-construction). The drawings have to show slope, span, **spacing**, design loads, lumber grades, and connections. So the **spacing comes from the truss manufacturer's stamped drawings**, not from a single number in the code. The prescriptive (no-engineer-needed) tables in ORSC R802 only cover **rafters and ceiling joists at 12, 16, 19.2, and 24 inches on center**, and they top out at much shorter spans than 40 feet, so for a 40-foot clear span you must use engineered trusses; there is no "rafter" path that gets you to 40 feet without a beam-and-post system.

For Portland's design loads: the **minimum design roof snow load is 25 pounds per square foot**, made up of 20 psf base snow plus a 5 psf rain-on-snow surcharge (Oregon Structural Specialty Code 1608.1, summarized at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/structural-engineering/one-and-two-family-residential-structures and confirmed by SEAO at http://snowload.seao.org/). The **prescriptive design wind pressure is 18 psf allowable stress, corresponding to roughly a 95-100 mph 3-second-gust basic design wind speed** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/structural-engineering/one-and-two-family-residential-structures). Portland is **Seismic Design Category D1** for most residential parcels, including yours; you can verify yours at the **Oregon Design Criteria Hub** at https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/pages/design-criteria.aspx.

So the practical answer for your 40-foot span: tell the truss supplier "40-foot clear span, 25 psf design roof snow load, 95 mph 3-second-gust wind, Exposure B, single-story unheated to lightly heated workshop, no ceiling load below," and ask for quotes at **4 feet, 8 feet, and (just for comparison) 10 feet on center**. You'll get back stamped drawings showing what each option costs and what truss depth is required. **4-foot spacing** is the universal default; standard plywood/OSB sheathing with 2x6 nailers spans 4 feet just fine, and the trusses can be relatively light. **8-foot spacing** is the classic pole-barn approach and is fully Portland-legal, but requires 2x4 or 2x6 **purlins** running on edge across the top of the trusses to support metal roofing; the trusses themselves get heavier (2x6 or 2x8 chords instead of 2x4). **10-foot spacing** pushes truss chords into 2x8 or 2x10 territory and the purlins into doubled or 2x6-on-edge; it's not common for residential workshops and your truss salesperson will probably steer you back to 8.

For the **walls and posts** of a true post-frame building, ORSC doesn't have prescriptive tables for embedded-post construction; the post sizes, embedment depths, and bracing all come from **stamped engineering**, which is part of the post-frame package you'll buy or have designed (ORSC R301.1.3, https://up.codes/viewer/oregon/irc-2021/chapter/3/building-planning, allows alternative designs by a registered design professional). The **foundation** can be one of three types: a continuous concrete footing with stem wall (most house-like, most expensive), concrete piers with embedded posts (the classic pole barn), or a reinforced concrete slab-on-grade with post anchors set into it (cleanest, easiest to insulate). All three are accepted in Portland; the choice is structural and cost, not regulatory.

A **concrete floor is not required** by code for a non-habitable workshop; you can have a gravel floor. But if you want to run electrical floor outlets, mount fixed equipment, or ever heat the space, a slab is worth it.

You will need **stamped engineering** for the trusses (always, for a 40-foot clear span; ORSC R802.10) and for any post-frame foundation system (ORSC R301.1.3). You can avoid stamped engineering for the rest of the building by using ORSC's prescriptive provisions for the walls and braced wall lines, which is the cheapest path; or you can have the whole thing engineered, which is what most pre-fab pole-barn packages from companies like MQS, Lester, or Mule-Hide already include.

### 4.3 Permits, by trigger

The **building permit is required** because your structure is over 200 square feet (ORSC R105.2 Item 1; Portland's worksheet at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/garage-shed-and-accessory-structures). Below 200 square feet and 15 feet tall and non-habitable, you'd be permit-exempt for the structure itself; you're well above that.

The **zoning permit** is folded into the building permit application; a separate zoning-only permit isn't needed unless you go below the 200 sf structural threshold (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/garage-shed-and-accessory-structures).

The **electrical permit** is required for any new wiring, separately filed, by a licensed electrician or by you as the owner-occupant per **OAR 918-309** (https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/Pages/electrical.aspx).

The **plumbing permit** does not apply because you're not adding water, sewer, or gas.

The **mechanical permit** applies only if you install fuel-burning heat, ducted HVAC, or a permanent ventilation system; a portable space heater or a 240V mini-split installed by your electrician (since mini-splits are electrical work) typically don't require a separate mechanical permit, but call BDS to confirm because mini-split rules have shifted recently.

The **stormwater management permit** is rolled into the building permit but can add real cost. Portland triggers stormwater management for projects creating **1,000 square feet or more of new impervious area**, which a 1,000+ sf roof does (https://www.portland.gov/bes/stormwater/swmm-and-right-way-projects/determine-requirements). For a backyard workshop, the simplest compliance options are a **drywell**, **soakage trench**, or **downspout disconnection** to a vegetated swale; the city's **Stormwater Management Manual** at https://www.portland.gov/bes/stormwater-manual covers sizing. Your soils are **Hydrologic Soil Group B** (well-drained), so infiltration approaches should work (per your environmental report at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/).

The **erosion control plan** is required for any project that disturbs ground; it's a one-page form for small projects, included in your building permit application (PCC Title 10, https://www.portland.gov/code/10, with Erosion Control Manual at https://www.portland.gov/bes/development/erosion-control-manual).

The **driveway / curb cut permit (PBOT)** applies only if you're creating a new vehicle entrance from NE 103rd Ave; if you're using your existing driveway, no curb-cut permit (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permits/driveway).

The **tree permit / tree review** is potentially required because your lot is **larger than 5,000 square feet**, which means you do NOT qualify for the small-lot exemption in Title 11 (PCC 11.50.040.B.2, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50/040). If your construction involves any ground disturbance within the **root protection zone** of a private tree 12 inches or larger in diameter, or any street tree 3 inches or larger, you'll need a **Tree Plan** (PCC 11.50.030, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50/030). If you don't have any trees that big where you want to build, you're fine; if you do, you'll need to retain at least 1/3 of non-exempt trees 12 inches or bigger, or pay mitigation fees.

### 4.4 Which Portland City Code titles actually apply

**Title 24 — Building Regulations** applies because you're building a structure (https://www.portland.gov/code/24); this is where Portland adopts the ORSC and OSSC.

**Title 33 — Planning and Zoning** applies through Chapter 33.110 Single-Dwelling Zones (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110), specifically 33.110.220 (setbacks), 33.110.225 (lot coverage), and 33.110.245 (accessory structures).

**Title 26 — Electrical Regulations** applies for the wiring (https://www.portland.gov/code/26).

**Title 25 — Plumbing Regulations** does NOT apply because you're not plumbing it (https://www.portland.gov/code/25).

**Title 27 — Heating, Ventilation, and Refrigeration** applies only if you install fixed mechanical equipment beyond a portable heater (https://www.portland.gov/code/27).

**Title 10 — Erosion and Sediment Control** applies because you're disturbing ground (https://www.portland.gov/code/10).

**Title 11 — Trees** applies because your lot is over 5,000 square feet, in particular Chapter 11.50 (https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50).

**Title 17 — Public Improvements**, specifically Chapter 17.38 — Drainage and Water Quality (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/38), is what gets you into the Stormwater Management Manual.

### 4.5 Process, sequencing, and where to apply

Apply online through Portland's permitting portal at https://www.portland.gov/ppd, or in person at the Development Services Center at **1900 SW 4th Avenue, Portland, OR 97201**, open Tuesdays and Thursdays 8 a.m. to 4 p.m. for walk-ins (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/development-services-center). Most residential accessory structure permits go fully online now; you upload a site plan, foundation plan, framing plan, stamped truss drawings, an erosion control plan, and a stormwater plan, then pay fees and wait for plan review comments.

Plan review for a residential accessory typically takes **4-8 weeks** in Portland in 2026, sometimes faster for clean applications, sometimes longer if reviewers come back with corrections. Add 1-3 weeks if BES wants stormwater clarifications. Add another 1-3 weeks if Urban Forestry wants a tree plan. After permit issuance, your construction is on your schedule with required inspections at footing, framing, electrical rough-in, electrical final, and final building.

A **pre-application meeting (Early Assistance)** is available but probably overkill for a workshop on a clean lot; it's more useful for new houses, ADUs, and complicated subdivisions (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/early-assistance-program). The **free 15-minute zoning appointment** is more useful and is what I recommend for you (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/free-15-min-appointments).

### 4.6 Who handles what (the bureau cheat sheet)

**Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)**, formerly **Bureau of Development Services (BDS)** until mid-2025, handles building permits, zoning review, plan review, and inspections; this is your primary contact (503-823-7300, https://www.portland.gov/ppd). **They are who you call first.**

**Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)** handles sewer, stormwater, and watershed protection (503-823-7761, https://www.portland.gov/bes). Your property is in the Inverness combined sewer basin per https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/.

**Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT)** handles streets, curb cuts, sidewalks, driveways, and right-of-way work (503-823-5185 for driveway/curb cut questions, https://www.portland.gov/transportation).

**Urban Forestry**, which is a division of **Portland Parks & Recreation (PP&R)**, handles street trees and tree code reviews (503-823-4489, https://www.portland.gov/trees).

**Portland Fire & Rescue (PF&R)** sets your fire-code requirements; for a residential accessory you generally don't talk to them directly because BDS handles the fire review for residential, but they show up if you have a structure within 3 feet of a property line (which triggers fire-rated wall assemblies under ORSC R302.1) (https://www.portland.gov/fire).

**Portland Water Bureau** handles your domestic water service; not relevant unless you tap a new water line for the workshop (503-823-7404, https://www.portland.gov/water).

**Multnomah County Surveyor** is who has your plat on file (https://multco.us/dart/county-surveyor); the lot is in Multnomah County.

### 4.7 Cost and timeline, in human terms

For a roughly 1,000 square foot non-habitable detached workshop with no plumbing, expect city fees in the range of **$1,500 to $3,500**, roughly the cost of a high-end laptop or a couple of monthly mortgage payments. The breakdown: structural building permit fee based on the **ICC Building Valuation table** (about $30-$45 per square foot for a Group U "utility" occupancy in 2026 dollars, so a roughly $30,000-$45,000 valuation for a 1,000 sf shop), plan review fee at 65% of the permit fee, a **12% Oregon state surcharge** on both, a **Development Services Fee** (residential, valuation-based, see Page 3 of the Land Use Services Fee Schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules), an **erosion control fee** (around $200), and a **stormwater plan review fee** (varies by complexity). These fees update every July 1; the current schedule is at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules.

**System Development Charges (SDCs)** are one-time fees the city collects for new buildings to pay for sewers, parks, water, and roads. Good news: for a **non-habitable accessory structure with no new dwelling unit**, the residential SDCs typically do NOT apply because there's no new household, no new water meter, and no new traffic trips (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sdc-fees). Confirm this with BDS during fee estimation; it's the biggest cost variable on this project.

The **Construction Excise Tax (CET)**, 1% of permit valuation, only kicks in at $100,000 valuation or higher, so you're under it (https://www.portland.gov/phb/cet).

**Electrical permit** for a sub-panel and shop wiring runs roughly **$150-$400** with the state surcharge.

**Total construction budget**, materials and labor: a 40 by 27 ft pole-barn shop in Portland in 2026, DIY-ish with hired truss/concrete crews, runs roughly **$40,000-$80,000** (about the cost of a new mid-tier pickup); fully contractor-built, $80,000-$150,000.

**Total timeline from "I want to do this" to "I can start framing"**: about **3 to 6 months**, roughly one summer's worth of weekends to plan plus one summer to build. Two weeks of planner appointments and surveys, 3-6 weeks for stamped trusses or post-frame package, 4-8 weeks of plan review, then construction.

## 5. Common gotchas Portland homeowners actually hit

The lot-size cap on accessory structures is the #1 gotcha for your address; most people assume "it's my yard, I can build whatever fits." Portland's 33.110.245.C.3.b cap based on the primary house's footprint catches people who didn't realize their existing house is unusually small for the area; you fall in this bucket because your 1,082 sf house caps your single workshop at 1,082 sf when most of your neighbors with bigger houses can build bigger workshops.

Building too close to a property line without a real survey. The plat dimensions are sometimes wrong by a foot or two, and a 5-foot side setback that turns out to be 4-foot-7 is a permit-killer; spend the $500-$1,500 on a corner survey before you pour footings.

The **3-foot fire rule** under ORSC R302.1: any building wall less than 3 feet from a property line has to be a 1-hour fire-rated wall assembly with no openings, which gets expensive fast and limits your windows. Stay 5 feet or more off side and rear lines and you avoid this entirely.

Finishing the inside and accidentally creating "habitable space." If you add a kitchen sink, a toilet, a sleeping area, or insulate-and-drywall the whole thing as a livable hangout, Portland will reclassify the building as habitable, which triggers ADU rules, full code compliance, SDCs, and possibly a different permit pathway (BOD 21-05, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bod-21-05-habitable-vs-non-habitable-accessory-structures). Heat is fine and so is electricity; a kitchen, bedroom, or bathroom is what flips the switch.

Running power without an electrical permit. Oregon enforces this aggressively; if you DIY the wiring, sign as the homeowner-occupant and pull the permit at https://www.oregon.gov/bcd (cheap, fast, online).

Building without realizing the **1,000 square foot stormwater trigger** kicks in. The cheapest way to handle it is to disconnect downspouts to a soakage trench in your back yard; your soils (Hydrologic Soil Group B, well-drained, 6 ft to fragipan per https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/) are favorable for infiltration.

Forgetting that the **garage entrance setback is 18 feet from the front lot line** (PCC 33.110.220 Table 110-4, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/110/220). If your shop has a vehicle door facing NE 103rd Ave, that door has to be 18 feet back, not just 10. Easiest fix: orient the shop so its big door faces the side or rear, not the street.

Tree code surprises on lots over 5,000 sf. If there's a 12-inch-or-larger tree in the path of your foundation, you'll either need to retain it (and stay outside its root protection zone), pay mitigation, or get an arborist's letter that it's dead/dying/dangerous (PCC 11.50.040, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50/040). Map your trees before you map your slab.

Pulling the building permit but discovering you also needed a **separate sewer cap permit** because you disconnected an old downspout drain that tied into the combined sewer; this is a BES item handled at https://www.portland.gov/bes.

## 6. Friendly accuracy caveats (warnings from a friend, not legal disclaimers)

Zoning rules change. Portland updated its accessory-structure rules in 2020 and again in 2023, and the residential code edition in force as of late 2026 is the 2023 ORSC; if this answer is more than 6 months old when you read it, verify the section numbers haven't shifted. The PortlandMaps detail at https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/450-NE-103RD-AVE/R319825/ is the canonical, real-time source for your specific property's zoning; trust it over any third-party summary including this one.

The **assessor's reported house footprint of 1,082 square feet** is the number that drives your accessory-structure cap, but the city's planner can use the actual measured footprint at the foundation line, which can vary by 50-100 square feet from the assessor's figure depending on overhangs, decks, and how the assessor measured it. Before you commit to a 1,080 sf workshop, get the planner to confirm the cap number in writing; if your house footprint is actually 1,050 sf, your cap is 1,050 sf.

Lot dimensions in PortlandMaps are derived from county GIS, which can be off by a foot or more from the actual surveyed corners, especially on older platted lots from the 1920s-1950s like yours (built 1951). The **plat or a fresh corner survey** is the only thing that tells you for certain where your property lines are.

The truss spacing answer above assumes a typical pole-barn or post-frame workshop with a metal roof and standard purlins; if you're picturing a heated, insulated, drywalled space with a finished ceiling, the loads change (more dead load, possible snow drift on adjacent roof) and the spacing might tighten. Tell the truss engineer the actual configuration.

**System Development Charges** can be the biggest variable in your budget; the rule of thumb that "non-habitable accessory structures don't trigger SDCs" is generally true but BES can charge a stormwater SDC if you create new impervious area without on-site management, and that can run $1,000-$3,000. Confirm with BES (503-823-7761) when you confirm your stormwater approach.

If at any point a planner or inspector tells you something different from what's in this document, **trust them, not me**; they're looking at your actual file with your actual measurements.

## 7. Glossary

**Accessory structure**: a separate, smaller building on the same lot as a main house, used for storage, parking, hobbies, etc.; a workshop or garage is an accessory structure.

**ADU (Accessory Dwelling Unit)**: a second small dwelling on the same lot as the main house. You're not building one of these; if you accidentally added a kitchen and bed, you would be.

**ASCE 7**: a national engineering standard, "Minimum Design Loads for Buildings and Other Structures", that defines snow, wind, and seismic loads.

**BDS (Bureau of Development Services)**: the old name (until mid-2025) for what's now called Portland Permitting & Development; same organization, new name.

**BES (Bureau of Environmental Services)**: handles Portland's sewer, stormwater, and watershed work.

**BOD (Building Official Determination)**: a written interpretation of the building code by Portland's building official; cited like "BOD 21-05".

**Building coverage**: the total ground area covered by buildings on a lot, expressed in square feet or as a percentage of lot area.

**CET (Construction Excise Tax)**: a 1% tax on permit valuations over $100,000, going to affordable housing.

**FAR (Floor Area Ratio)**: total floor area of all buildings divided by lot area; not the same as building coverage.

**Header**: a beam over a door or window opening that carries the load above.

**Inline citation**: a code section number and URL placed in the same sentence as the claim being supported.

**IRC (International Residential Code)**: the national model code for one- and two-family dwellings, which Oregon adapts and adopts as the ORSC.

**ORSC (Oregon Residential Specialty Code)**: Oregon's adopted version of the IRC, the residential code Portland enforces.

**OSSC (Oregon Structural Specialty Code)**: Oregon's commercial/structural code, used for buildings that fall outside the residential code.

**Overlay zone**: an extra layer of zoning rules on top of a base zone, like flood plain, environmental conservation, historic, or design overlays. Your lot has none.

**PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)**: handles streets and rights-of-way.

**PCC (Portland City Code)**: the city's code, organized by Title (Title 24, Title 33, etc.).

**PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue)**: the fire bureau.

**PP&D (Portland Permitting & Development)**: the new (post-2025) name for what used to be BDS.

**PP&R (Portland Parks & Recreation)**: handles parks and Urban Forestry.

**Plan district**: an area-specific overlay defining special rules for a part of town; East Corridor (EC) is the one your lot is in.

**Plat**: the recorded survey map showing your lot and its dimensions.

**Pole barn / post-frame**: a building style where vertical posts (often embedded in the ground or anchored to concrete piers) carry the roof, instead of stick-framed walls on a continuous foundation.

**Purlin**: a horizontal framing member that runs across the top of trusses or rafters to support the roofing material; common in pole barns where trusses are spaced 8 feet apart.

**ROW (right-of-way)**: the public street area, usually owned by the city.

**R2.5**: the zone code for "Residential 2,500", Portland's smallest single-dwelling lot zone.

**SDC (System Development Charges)**: one-time fees the city charges new buildings to pay for sewers, water, parks, and roads.

**Setback**: the minimum distance a building has to sit back from each property line.

**Span**: the distance a beam, rafter, or truss covers between supports; your 40-foot clear span means no posts in the middle.

**SWMM (Stormwater Management Manual)**: Portland's rules for handling rainwater on developing sites; triggered by 1,000+ sf of new impervious area.

**Title 33**: Portland's zoning code; Chapter 33.110 specifically governs single-dwelling zones like yours.

**Truss**: a triangulated, factory-built roof framing unit that spans long distances without interior support; an alternative to traditional rafters.

**On-center (o.c.)**: how truss or stud spacing is measured, from the center of one member to the center of the next; "8 feet on center" means trusses 8 feet apart, center to center.

---

**One last thing.** The single most valuable next step you can take, before you spend any money, is to book the free 15-minute zoning appointment at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/free-15-min-appointments and walk in with a hand-drawn site sketch and the question "what's the largest single detached accessory structure I can build on this lot under PCC 33.110.245.C.3.b given my existing house footprint?" Get the planner's answer in writing (an email confirmation works), and that becomes the maximum size you design to. Everything downstream gets cheaper and faster once that number is locked in.
