col3.response:
I'll dig into this carefully, since you want specifics for your actual address with real code citations. Let me start by looking up your property and pulling current Portland rules.Important early finding: this property is more interesting than a typical single-family house. Let me grab a few more pieces and then pull current Portland re-roof permit rules.I've got what I need. One important thing surfaced while I was looking up your address that I want to flag right at the top: the property records show some quirks worth knowing about before you make any calls. I'll walk through everything below.

---

## 1. The plain-English summary (start here)

Good news first: in Portland, if you're doing a normal **like-for-like re-roof** on a one- or two-family home (peeling off old asphalt shingles, putting new asphalt shingles back on, similar weight, no big structural changes), **you do not need a building permit**. That's per the city's Permitting & Development page (Portland City Code Title 24, Chapter 24.10; https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits). You still need the work done correctly, by a properly licensed contractor if you hire one, and any dumpster in the street or tree work crosses into permits from other bureaus.

But here's the wrinkle for your specific address. **5500 SE Belmont is not a typical single-family house** in the city's records. It's the historic Mt. Tabor Telephone Exchange building (built 1914), zoned **RM1 (Residential Multi-Dwelling 1)**, and past permits have classified it as **R-2 Multi-Family** (3+ units). If that's still the case, your roof falls under the **commercial re-roof program**, not the residential exemption, and a permit IS required. So the very first thing you actually need to do is confirm with the city how your building is currently classified. I'll give you a script for that call.

The rest of this answer covers both scenarios so you're prepared either way.

---

## 2. Does my specific situation need a permit?

**Two big variables decide this for you:** (a) is your building treated as a 1- or 2-family home, or as a 3+ unit building, and (b) what exactly you're doing to the roof.

### Scenario A: If your building is a 1- or 2-family home (residential rules)

Here's what the city's plain-language page says about reroofing without a permit: "**Reroofing with a roofing material of similar weight does not need a building permit. This includes the replacement of some or all existing roof sheathing**" (Portland City Code 24.10.040, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits and https://www.portland.gov/code/24/all). "Sheathing" means the wood panels under the shingles.

| Your situation | Permit needed? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Asphalt to asphalt, similar weight, no structural changes | **No** | The most common case. |
| Asphalt to asphalt, plus replacing some or all rotten wood under the shingles | **No (residential)** | The city site explicitly allows this without a permit. |
| Switching to a much heavier material (asphalt to clay/concrete tile) | **Yes**, almost certainly | Heavier material can overload the framing; structural review is needed. Call before buying material. |
| Switching to a similar-weight material (asphalt to standing-seam metal, or to architectural composite) | **Often no**, but verify | "Similar weight" is the test in PCC 24.10.040 (https://www.portland.gov/code/24/all). Get the city to confirm in writing. |
| Adding skylights, changing roof pitch, changing the roofline | **Yes** | This is an alteration to the structure, not a re-roof. |
| Patching a small section after a leak or storm | **No** | Ordinary repair. |
| Solar panels or solar-integrated roofing | **Yes** | Required for any photovoltaic install (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/solar-development/solar-permits). |
| Re-roofing in a designated **Wildfire Hazard Zone** | **Yes** | Your property is NOT in one, per PortlandMaps (`wild_lands_fire_hazard_area: false`). So this doesn't apply to you. See https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-projects/wildfire-hazard-areas-residential. |
| Re-roofing a townhouse | **Yes** | Yours isn't classified as a townhouse, so doesn't apply. |

The plain-English summary above each row: a simple shingle swap is exempt; anything that changes structure, weight, layout, or adds solar pulls you into permit territory.

### Scenario B: If your building is treated as 3+ units / commercial (which past permit records suggest)

For 3-or-more-unit buildings, Portland uses a separate **Commercial Reroof Permit** program (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/commercial-permitting/commercial-permit-inspections/commercial-reroof). Even a like-for-like re-roof needs a permit. The contractor (who must be registered with the city's Commercial Reroof program) buys a "reroof label" tied to your project, schedules an inspection of the wood under the roof, and then a final inspection. Sheathing replacement is limited to roughly 10% before a full commercial building permit may be required (per Program Guide Section V.(A)(2) at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/commercial-reroof-permit-program-guide/download).

**Bottom line for your address: confirm classification first.** I'll give you the exact phone script in section 6.

---

## 3. Which Portland bureau handles this, and which code applies

A heads-up first: **"BDS" no longer exists as its own bureau**. As of July 1, 2024, the Bureau of Development Services merged with permitting teams from Urban Forestry, Bureau of Environmental Services, the Bureau of Transportation, and Water into a single bureau called **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** (announced at https://www.koin.com/local/portland-launches-new-bureau-to-streamline-permitting-process-07012024/ and confirmed at https://www.portland.gov/ppd). The merger is supposed to make permits simpler. People (including the city's own pages) still occasionally say "BDS"; treat them as the same thing for now.

**Who does what, in plain English:**

- **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** is the agency you call about roofs, building permits, zoning, inspections, and most construction work. Phone **503-823-7300** Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. (https://www.portland.gov/ppd). The walk-in office is the **Development Services Center, 1900 SW 4th Ave**, open Tuesdays and Thursdays 8 a.m. to 4 p.m.; appointments are recommended (https://www.portland.gov/service-locations/development-services-center). For inspections, the automated line is **503-823-7000** (you'll need an IVR number, which is your permit's tracking number).

- **Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT)** runs the streets and sidewalks, including the public area in front of your house ("**right-of-way**" or **ROW**, the strip from your property line to the street, including the sidewalk and parking lane). You only deal with PBOT on a re-roof if you're putting a dumpster, pod, or storage container in the street or parking lane; that needs a **Temporary Street Use Permit** (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting/apply-renew-or-change-temporary-street-use-permit). PBOT general inquiries: **503-823-5185** or 311 (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting).

- **Portland Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)** handles sewers and stormwater. You'd only hear from them if you're changing how rainwater leaves your roof (new downspouts that don't connect properly to the old system, switching to a drywell, or anything that might affect the storm or sanitary sewer). BES contact: **503-823-7740** (https://www.portland.gov/bes).

- **Portland Fire & Rescue (PF&R)** doesn't typically get involved in residential re-roofs unless there's a separate fire safety review (highly unusual). PF&R: **503-823-3700** (https://www.portland.gov/fire). Note: roofing material in **Wildfire Hazard Zones** must meet ignition-resistance standards, but again, your property is not in a Wildfire Hazard Zone.

- **Urban Forestry** (now part of PP&D) handles the tree code, **Portland City Code Title 11** (https://www.portland.gov/code/11/all). If you need to prune or remove a **street tree** (a tree in the planting strip between sidewalk and curb, or in unimproved right-of-way), you need a permit; even pruning branches requires a permit unless certain narrow exemptions apply (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/street-tree-pruning-permits/pruning-street-tree-permits-faqs). For pruning your own trees on private property, no permit is needed unless the tree is a designated Heritage Tree or in certain overlay zones (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/all). Urban Forestry phone: **503-823-TREE (8733)**.

**The codes that govern roof work:**

- **Portland City Code (PCC) Title 24, Building Regulations** (https://www.portland.gov/code/24/all) is Portland's building code. Section **24.10.040** lists when permits are required and adopts the Oregon state codes by reference (https://www.portland.gov/code/24/all). Specifically, PCC 24.10.040.A says: "No person, firm, or corporation may erect, construct, enlarge, alter, repair, move, improve, remove, convert, change occupancy group of, or demolish any building or structure ... without first obtaining the proper permit" (https://www.portland.gov/code/24/all). The exemptions live within the state codes that Title 24 adopts.

- **Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC)** is the state's residential building code; it applies to **one- and two-family dwellings and townhouses** and is adopted by Portland via PCC 24.10.040 and by reference in OAR 918-480-0005 (https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/Pages/residential-structures.aspx). The ORSC's Section R105.2 lists permit-exempt work; reroofing where the work doesn't exceed 30% of the required live-load design capacity and the roof isn't required to be fire-resistant is on that list (industry summary at https://www.oregonbuildingofficials.com/assets/docs/opta_realtor_presentation.pdf). Portland's residential permitting page restates this in plain English (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/do-you-need-permit/residential-permits).

- **Oregon Structural Specialty Code (OSSC)** is the state's commercial code; it applies to buildings with three or more units, businesses, and other commercial structures. Portland adopted the 2022 OSSC by reference via Ordinance 191148 (https://www.portland.gov/council/documents/ordinance/passed/191581) under PCC 24.10.040 (https://www.portland.gov/code/24/all). If your building is treated as R-2 Multi-Family, OSSC governs.

- **Title 33, Zoning** (https://www.portland.gov/code/33) governs land use, zone rules, and overlays. Routine re-roofing doesn't normally trigger Title 33 unless you're in a designated **Historic District** or working on a **Historic Landmark** or **Significant Resource**, which would trigger **Historic Resource Review** under PCC 33.445.100 through 33.445.220 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33). Your building is a "**Documented Resource**," which is the lowest historic tier and is **explicitly not subject to historic preservation land use regulations** (https://www.portland.gov/bps/planning/historic-resources/types). Big relief.

- **Title 11, Trees** (https://www.portland.gov/code/11/all) governs tree pruning and removal, which can come up if branches block roof access.

- **Title 17, Public Improvements** (https://www.portland.gov/code/17) and PBOT administrative rules govern dumpsters and storage in the public street.

If you can't find a specific code section + URL for a claim, I'll say so out loud rather than make one up.

---

## 4. Your specific property at 5500 SE Belmont St (this is where it gets interesting)

Quick warning before the data: zoning, overlays, and historic status for any address can change. I pulled this from PortlandMaps just now, but you should always **verify yourself before spending money** by going to https://www.portlandmaps.com, typing your address, and reading the "Zoning" and "Permits & Cases" panels.

Here's what your property looks like in the records right now:

- **Owner of record:** Hudson Ventures LLC (acquired September 26, 2024 for $1.4 million per the assessor; https://www.portlandmaps.com).
- **Year built:** 1914 per historic records, 1921 per assessor records.
- **Base zoning:** **RM1, Residential Multi-Dwelling 1** (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/base/RM1). In plain English, this zone allows multiple dwelling units on a single lot at low density; small apartment buildings, duplexes, triplexes, and fourplexes are common. Single-family homes are allowed too. Roof rules don't change between RM1 and the single-dwelling zones; what changes is whether the building itself is treated as 1-or-2-family vs. 3+ units, which is a separate occupancy classification, not a zoning issue.
- **Comprehensive Plan designation:** MD-N (Multi-Dwelling Neighborhood) per PortlandMaps.
- **Historic status:** **Documented Resource**, listed as "Mt. Tabor Telephone Exchange" in the city's Historic Resources Inventory (https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=51451). As I noted above, **Documented Resources are not subject to historic preservation land use rules** (https://www.portland.gov/bps/planning/historic-resources/types), so no historic review is required for re-roofing. Cool building, no extra red tape from the historic side.
- **Overlays:** None per PortlandMaps. No design overlay (`d`), no historic overlay (`h`), no environmental overlay (`c`, `p`, `v`), no plan district. So no Sunnyside-specific design rules. (The property is technically in Mt. Tabor neighborhood, not Sunnyside.)
- **Wildfire Hazard Zone:** No (`wild_lands_fire_hazard_area: false`). So standard roofing materials are fine.
- **Steep slope flag:** Yes (`steep_slope_area: true`); this matters for site disturbance and grading, not for re-roofing.
- **Past permit history flag (this is the important one):** A 2012 permit on this property was filed under permit type "**Apartments/Condos (3 or more units)**" with occupancy group "**R-2 Residential Multi-family**" and construction type "III-B" (PortlandMaps permit application 2012-122232-000-00-CO). A 2001 change-of-occupancy permit converted parts of the building from office use to apartments and primary residence (PortlandMaps permit application 2001-140127-000-00-CO). A recent 2025 permit for a wood stove was filed as "Commercial or Multi-Family Dwelling/Structure" (PortlandMaps permit application 2025-082428-000-00-MT). A 2023 radon mitigation permit, however, was filed as "One or Two Family Dwelling/Structure," which is inconsistent. **The records suggest the building is currently a multi-unit R-2, which puts your re-roof under commercial reroof rules.** This is the single biggest thing to confirm before you do anything else.

### How to verify all of this yourself on PortlandMaps

1. Go to **https://www.portlandmaps.com**.
2. Type **5500 SE Belmont St** in the search box and select your address from the suggestions.
3. On the property page, scroll through the panels: **Zoning**, **Historic**, **Permits & Cases**, **Property** (for hazards and overlays).
4. Click the **"Zoning"** link at the top to see the base zone, overlays, and any plan districts. The zone code is the key to most of the rules.
5. Open **"Permits & Cases"** to see the full permit history. The "occupancy group" and "type" columns tell you how the city has been classifying your building.
6. If anything looks off, write down the specific permit numbers and the building classification, then call PP&D.

A friendly nudge: zoning maps and overlays do change. The Portland Zoning Code can be amended; new overlays can be drawn. **Check PortlandMaps the day you're getting bids.** Don't rely on what I (or anyone) wrote a week or month ago.

---

## 5. What to do next, in order

Each step lists who you contact, what to bring, what it costs, and roughly how long.

1. **Verify your property and zoning on PortlandMaps.** Go to https://www.portlandmaps.com, look up 5500 SE Belmont St. Note your zoning, occupancy classification on past permits, and any flags. **Free. About 15 minutes.**

2. **Book a free 15-minute virtual appointment with PP&D** to confirm whether your roof falls under residential or commercial rules. This is the most important step for your specific building. Schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/15-min. Have ready: your address, the property ID (R114587), past permit numbers (especially 2012-122232-000-00-CO which classified the building as R-2 Multi-family), and a description of the roof work you're planning. **Free. 15 minutes.**

3. **Call the General Inquiries line at PP&D for follow-up questions.** 503-823-7300, Monday-Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. (https://www.portland.gov/ppd). Use the script in section 6. **Free. About one phone call (15-20 minutes).**

4. **Get bids from licensed roofing contractors and verify their CCB license.** The Construction Contractors Board (CCB) is Oregon's contractor licensing agency. Verify any contractor's license at https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/index.aspx and search by name or CCB number. Get at least 3 bids; for the size of your building, I'd budget for the bid range to land roughly between **$15,000 and $50,000+** depending on whether you go asphalt vs. metal vs. tile, and how much wood under the shingles needs replacing. **Free to get bids. 1-3 weeks to gather.**

5. **Pull the permit, if needed.** If PP&D says your project needs a permit, the easiest path is to have your contractor pull it through their Commercial Reroof account (for R-2 multi-family) or through the residential application portal (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/permitting). Building permits can be applied for online at https://devhub.portlandoregon.gov. **Permit fees vary; expect roughly $300-$1,000+ for a project at this scale, plus a 12% state surcharge** (per the 2025-2026 fee schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules). For commercial reroof labels, see the fee schedule with Ctrl+F "roof". **Permit issuance: same day to a week for over-the-counter; longer if plan review is needed.**

6. **Schedule the work and inspections.** Your contractor schedules inspections by calling **503-823-7000** with the IVR (permit tracking) number (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-inspections). For a commercial reroof, the required inspections are **Code 285 (Roofing Inspection** of the wood and underlying structure**)** and **Code 999 (Final Inspection)** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/commercial-permitting/commercial-permit-inspections/commercial-reroof). Inspectors usually come within 1-2 business days of request. Don't cover the wood before the 285 inspection; that's a re-do problem if you do.

7. **If a dumpster goes in the street, pull a Temporary Street Use Permit from PBOT** at https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting/apply-renew-or-change-temporary-street-use-permit. **Application takes 5-10 minutes; permit typically issued in 5-10 business days. Fees vary by duration and location; budget roughly $100-$400** for a standard residential dumpster permit. If the dumpster sits on your driveway or private property, no PBOT permit is needed.

8. **If trees need pruning to access the roof:** pruning your own private tree branches is fine without a permit unless it's a Heritage Tree or in a `c`, `p`, or `v` overlay zone (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/street-tree-pruning-permits/pruning-street-tree-permits-faqs). For street trees (in the planting strip), you need a Street Tree Pruning Permit; some routine clearance pruning can be self-issued online (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/street-tree-pruning-permits). Heritage Trees always require a permit. Urban Forestry: 503-823-TREE (8733).

9. **Final inspection and permit close-out.** After roofing is done, the inspector signs off and the permit is "Final Inspection Approved" in the city's records. **This is critical for resale**; an open permit can stall a sale. Verify it's closed by checking PortlandMaps later (https://www.portlandmaps.com) under "Permits & Cases."

---

## 6. What to ask the city: a script you can use

Read this on the phone or paste it into an email to PP&D (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact). Adjust the bracketed bits.

> Hi, my name is **[your name]** and I own the property at **5500 SE Belmont St, Portland, OR 97215**, property ID **R114587**. I'm planning to replace the roof and I want to make sure I'm following the right rules before I hire a contractor.
>
> Looking at PortlandMaps, my property is zoned **RM1** and a 2012 permit (application **2012-122232-000-00-CO**) classified the building as **R-2 Residential Multi-Family**. A 2023 permit, though, classified it as a **One or Two Family Dwelling**. I want to confirm: under the current Building Code, is my building treated as **1-or-2-family** or as a **commercial multi-family (3+ units)** for the purposes of a re-roof?
>
> Based on that answer:
>
> 1. If it's 1-or-2-family, can I do a like-for-like asphalt-to-asphalt re-roof, including replacing rotten sheathing, **without a building permit** under PCC 24.10.040 and the Oregon Residential Specialty Code?
> 2. If it's a 3+ unit building, do I need to go through the **Commercial Reroof Permit** program, and does it matter whether some or all of the existing wood under the shingles needs to be replaced?
> 3. The property is a **Documented Resource** in the Historic Resources Inventory ("Mt. Tabor Telephone Exchange"). I understand Documented Resources are not subject to historic preservation rules; can you confirm that no Historic Resource Review applies to my re-roof?
> 4. Are there any other reviews I'm missing: stormwater, fire, anything specific to RM1 or to this address?
> 5. If I do need a permit, what's the typical fee range for my building's size, and how long does issuance usually take? Do System Development Charges apply to a re-roof?
>
> Could you also email me a written confirmation, or give me a permit reference number for this conversation, so I can show it to my contractor? Thanks!

Two pro moves: (a) ask for written confirmation; (b) book the free 15-minute appointment too (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/15-min) so you have a city expert walk through it with you on screen.

---

## 7. Contractor vs. DIY

**Can you re-roof your own house yourself?** In Oregon, an owner can do construction work on a home they own and occupy without a CCB license (the "owner-occupant exemption" under ORS 701.010(7); https://oregon.public.law/statutes/ors_701.010). But there's a catch: if the home is intended for sale, lease, or rent in the near future, the work must be done by a licensed contractor. And for commercial buildings (R-2 included) **on a rental investment property**, the owner-occupant exemption generally doesn't apply; you need a licensed contractor.

For your address, given the LLC ownership and apparent multi-unit status, you should plan on hiring a licensed contractor.

**If you hire someone, verify these:**

- **CCB license number** (Construction Contractors Board, Oregon's contractor licensing agency). All contractors must be licensed; verify at **https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/license-search.aspx**. Type in the contractor's name or CCB number; the page shows their license status, bond, insurance, and any complaints. **Do this before signing any contract.** A real contractor will hand you their CCB number without flinching.
- **Bond.** CCB-licensed residential contractors carry a $20,000-$25,000 bond; commercial contractors $75,000+. Visible on the license search page.
- **General liability insurance.** Visible on the same page.
- **Workers' comp insurance** if they have employees. Verify at https://wcd.oregon.gov.
- **City of Portland Business License Number.** Anyone doing business in Portland needs one. Ask for it.
- **References from recent local jobs**, ideally on similar buildings (older multi-family, historic).

**Tradeoffs in plain English:**

| Path | Cost | Warranty | Permit responsibility | Liability if inspection fails |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hire licensed contractor | Higher (labor markup); typically $15K-$50K+ for your size | Manufacturer warranty + contractor warranty (often 5-10 years on workmanship) | Contractor pulls and closes the permit | Contractor is on the hook to fix |
| DIY (if eligible as owner-occupant of a 1-or-2-family) | Lower direct cost; high time investment; risk of mistakes | Manufacturer warranty only | You pull and close the permit | You are on the hook |

Quick warning above that table: I'd lean strongly toward hiring out for a building this size, age (1914), and likely R-2 classification. A 1900s building's roof framing is rarely standard, and an inspection failure on a multi-unit building can cascade into bigger problems.

---

## 8. Cost and timeline reality check

- **Permit fees for a residential re-roof:** if a permit is required (most likely under the commercial reroof program for your building), expect **roughly $300 to $1,000+** plus a **12% state surcharge** for the typical project, based on the city's Building and Other Permits Fee Schedule effective July 1, 2025 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/building-and-other-permits-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download). Fees update each July. For commercial reroof labels (the cheaper path most contractors use for like-for-like work), the per-label fee is in the same schedule (Ctrl+F "roof" in the PDF).

- **System Development Charges (SDCs):** these are one-time fees that fund city infrastructure (sewer, stormwater, transportation, parks, water) when development adds new units or new impact. **SDCs do not apply to a like-for-like re-roof**, since you're not adding units or expanding impact (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sdcs). You can skip that worry. If your project ever changes scope to add a unit or expand the building's footprint, SDCs come back into play.

- **Permit timeline:** for a like-for-like reroof under the commercial reroof program, your contractor uses pre-purchased "labels" and the permit is essentially same-day. For a residential building permit (when needed), expect **same day to about a week** over-the-counter; longer if plan review is needed.

- **Actual roof work:** for a building of this size, expect **roughly 3-7 working days** for a competent crew on standard asphalt; tile or metal can take 1-2 weeks. About the time of a typical work week.

- **Inspection wait times:** typically **1-2 business days** after the contractor calls in. The contractor calls 503-823-7000 with the IVR number and selects an inspection code (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-inspections).

- **What if you fail an inspection:** the inspector writes corrections, you fix them, and you call back for a re-inspection. Re-inspection fees apply per the fee schedule; budget **about $100-$200 per re-inspection** in worst case. Don't cover work before the 285 (roofing) inspection; that's a do-over.

---

## 9. Common gotchas (the stuff Portland homeowners actually mess up)

- **Starting work before the permit is issued.** Doubles your fees in some Oregon jurisdictions and exposes you to a stop-work order. Always confirm the permit is issued (not just "applied for") before tear-off.
- **Hiring an unlicensed contractor.** Verify the CCB number at https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/license-search.aspx **before** signing. If they push back on showing it, walk away.
- **Putting a dumpster in the street without a PBOT Temporary Street Use Permit.** The city will tag it; ROW Compliance is real (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting/row-compliance). Get the permit at https://www.portland.gov/transportation/permitting/apply-renew-or-change-temporary-street-use-permit, or have the dumpster on your driveway.
- **Discovering rotten wood mid-project.** Under residential rules, you can replace the wood without a new permit; under commercial rules, replacing more than ~10% may push you into a full commercial building permit at the inspector's discretion (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/commercial-reroof-permit-program-guide/download). Brief your contractor about this risk before they start.
- **Trimming a street tree without an Urban Forestry permit.** Even a few branches can require a Street Tree Pruning Permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/street-tree-pruning-permits). Self-issued online for routine clearance pruning; otherwise call 503-823-TREE.
- **Re-roofing a Historic Landmark or building in a Historic District without Historic Resource Review** (PCC 33.445.100-220, https://www.portland.gov/code/33). **Yours is a Documented Resource, which is exempt** (https://www.portland.gov/bps/planning/historic-resources/types). But if your building's status ever gets upgraded to Significant Resource or Landmark, the rules change dramatically.
- **Forgetting the final inspection.** A re-roof permit isn't really done until inspection code **999 - Final** is signed off (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/commercial-permitting/commercial-permit-inspections/commercial-reroof). An open permit shows up in title searches at resale and can hold up the closing. Always verify the permit shows "Final Inspection Approved" on PortlandMaps after the work.
- **Stormwater issues.** If you change downspouts, gutter routing, or where rainwater hits the ground, you may need to coordinate with BES on the **Downspout Disconnection Program** or stormwater rules (https://www.portland.gov/bes/stormwater). Most like-for-like re-roofs don't trigger this; ask if you're rerouting.
- **Assuming "single family" because the assessor says so.** Your assessor record says "Single Family Residential" but past building permits class it as R-2 Multi-Family. Permits beat assessor records for code purposes. Confirm with PP&D before you start.

---

## 10. Friendly accuracy caveats

A few neighborly heads-ups, not legal disclaimers:

- **Zoning, overlays, and historic status can change.** Always check PortlandMaps the week you're getting bids. https://www.portlandmaps.com.
- **Fee schedules update each July.** The figures I gave are based on the July 1, 2025 schedule. Call PP&D at 503-823-7300 for current numbers (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules).
- **The biggest wildcard for your address is the building classification** (1-or-2-family vs. R-2 multi-family). Confirm this before anything else; everything downstream depends on it.
- **If the property is rental or held by an LLC and not owner-occupied, the owner-occupant DIY exemption likely doesn't apply.** Plan for licensed contractor.
- **City staff are generally helpful**, but their answers are only as good as the question you ask. The script in section 6 is designed to get you a usable answer in one call.
- **I'm an AI assistant. None of this is legal advice.** It's based on public city sources I read today (May 7, 2026). Any judgment call about your specific building is for the city's plan reviewer to make, not me.

---

## 11. Glossary

- **BDS**: Bureau of Development Services. Portland's old building permits agency. Merged into Portland Permitting & Development on July 1, 2024. People still say "BDS"; treat it as the same as PP&D.
- **BES**: Bureau of Environmental Services. Sewer and stormwater agency. https://www.portland.gov/bes.
- **CCB**: Construction Contractors Board. Oregon's contractor licensing agency. https://www.oregon.gov/ccb.
- **Decking** or **sheathing**: The wood (usually plywood or OSB panels) under the shingles, attached to the rafters. The roof's structural skin.
- **Documented Resource**: The lowest tier of historic recognition in Portland's Historic Resources Inventory. Inventoried but not protected under preservation rules. Yours is one ("Mt. Tabor Telephone Exchange").
- **Drip edge**: A metal strip that runs along the edge of the roof, directing water away from the wood underneath. Required by code on most modern roofs.
- **Heritage Tree**: A specifically designated tree in Portland; any work on one needs Urban Forestry permission.
- **Historic District**, **Conservation District**, **Historic Landmark**, **Significant Resource**: Higher-tier historic protections that DO trigger design review for re-roofing. Yours doesn't fall in any of these; don't worry about them.
- **Ice and water shield**: A peel-and-stick rubber membrane installed at roof edges and valleys for extra waterproofing. Required in some climates; common best practice in Portland.
- **IVR number**: The phone-tracking number tied to your permit. You use it to schedule inspections at 503-823-7000.
- **Like-for-like (re-roof)**: Replacing the same kind of roofing material with the same kind, in the same configuration. The simplest case, usually permit-exempt for 1-or-2-family homes.
- **Occupancy group**: A code classification (R-2, R-3, B, etc.) for what kind of building it is. **R-3** is one- and two-family residential; **R-2** is multi-family residential (apartments, 3+ units); **B** is business/office. Past permits show your building has been classified as both, depending on the year.
- **ORSC**: Oregon Residential Specialty Code. The state code for one- and two-family homes and townhouses. Adopted by Portland under PCC 24.10.040.
- **OSSC**: Oregon Structural Specialty Code. The state code for commercial buildings, including 3+ unit residential.
- **Overlay zone**: An extra layer of zoning rules on top of the base zone. Examples include design (`d`), environmental (`c`, `p`), historic (`h`). Yours has none.
- **Overlay (re-roof)**: Putting new shingles on top of old shingles without tearing off. **Portland generally prefers full tear-off**; overlay only allowed in specific conditions.
- **PBOT**: Portland Bureau of Transportation. Streets, sidewalks, dumpsters in the right-of-way. https://www.portland.gov/transportation.
- **PCC**: Portland City Code. The whole body of Portland law, organized into Titles. Title 24 = Building Regulations, Title 33 = Zoning, Title 11 = Trees, Title 17 = Public Improvements.
- **PF&R**: Portland Fire & Rescue. https://www.portland.gov/fire.
- **PP&D**: Portland Permitting & Development. The bureau that handles building, zoning, trade, and other permits. Phone 503-823-7300. https://www.portland.gov/ppd.
- **RM1**: Residential Multi-Dwelling 1. Your zoning. Allows multi-unit residential at low density.
- **ROW**: Right-of-Way. The public area in front of your property: the sidewalk, planting strip, parking lane, and street.
- **SDC**: System Development Charge. One-time fee on new construction or unit additions to fund city infrastructure. **Doesn't apply to a like-for-like re-roof.**
- **Square** (as in "20 squares of shingles"): A roofer's measurement; 1 square = 100 square feet of roof surface. So 20 squares = 2,000 sq ft.
- **Tear-off**: Removing the old shingles all the way down to the wood (sheathing/decking) before installing new shingles. The opposite of an overlay.
- **Title 24, Title 33, Title 11**: see PCC. Title 24 = Building Regulations; Title 33 = Zoning; Title 11 = Trees.
- **Underlayment**: A water-resistant layer (asphalt felt or synthetic) installed on top of the wood sheathing, before the shingles. The second line of defense if water gets past the shingles.
- **Urban Forestry**: City team handling tree code under PCC Title 11. Now part of PP&D. 503-823-TREE (8733).

If you want, after your call with PP&D, send me back what they tell you about the building classification and I'll help you pressure-test the permit/contractor path that actually applies. Good luck; the building has a great story (a real telephone-exchange building from 1914 is a fun thing to own a roof on).

roof-permit.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know whether I need a permit to replace the roof at my house at 5500 SE Belmont St, Portland, OR 97215, and what the whole process actually looks like from start to finish so I don't get blindsided. Please answer as if you are a knowledgeable, friendly neighbor walking me through it — I am a regular Portland homeowner with no background in permitting, zoning, building code, or construction.

**Audience and reading level (please follow exactly):**
- Write for a Portland resident or small-business owner with no code or permitting background. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but unfamiliar with city processes.
- Lead with a plain-English summary that answers my question directly in 3–5 sentences before any tables, citations, or code language.
- Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, PF&R, PCC, ORSC, SDC, ROW, re-roof, tear-off, overlay, sheathing, decking, ice and water shield, drip edge, etc.). If you wouldn't use the term with a neighbor over the fence, define it.
- Use everyday words instead of code language wherever you can. Say "the wood under the shingles" before "sheathing"; say "tearing off the old shingles down to the wood" before "complete tear-off."
- Keep paragraphs short. Bold the key terms inside sentences instead of building dense tables. Use a table only when comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table.
- Translate dollar figures and timelines into something I can plan around ("about the price of a nice dinner out," "about one weekend of work," etc.).

**What I actually need to understand:**

1. **The direct answer first.** Do I need a permit to replace my roof in Portland? Cover the realistic scenarios a homeowner runs into:
   - Just replacing shingles like-for-like (asphalt to asphalt) with no structural changes.
   - Replacing shingles AND replacing some or all of the wood underneath (sheathing/decking) because it's rotten.
   - Switching roofing materials (asphalt to metal, asphalt to tile, adding solar-ready underlayment).
   - Adding skylights, changing roof pitch, or changing the roof line at the same time.
   - Just patching or repairing a small section after a leak or storm damage.
   - Tell me clearly which of these need a permit, which don't, and what the dollar or square-footage threshold is (if any) that flips it from "no permit needed" to "permit required."

2. **Which Portland city bureau handles this**, in plain English:
   - **BDS (Bureau of Development Services)** — what they do, when I'd talk to them, phone number, walk-in hours, and the specific website page for residential roofing/re-roof permits.
   - Whether **PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue)**, **BES (Bureau of Environmental Services — sewer and stormwater)**, **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation — streets and sidewalks)**, **PP&D (Permitting & Development)**, or **Urban Forestry** ever get involved with a roof replacement (e.g., dumpster in the street, tree trimming over the roof, stormwater changes), and when.

3. **What city code actually governs this.** Tell me which Title(s) apply (Title 24 Building Regulations, the Oregon Residential Specialty Code / ORSC adopted by Portland, Title 33 Zoning if any overlays apply, Title 11 Trees if branches need cutting, etc.) and cite the specific PCC sections for each rule you state. Every factual claim — every rule, fee, timeline, threshold, phone number, contact, or process step — must be followed in the same sentence by:
   - the exact code section or rule number (e.g., `PCC 24.10.040`, not just "Title 24"), AND
   - a full working URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov, portland.gov/code, the specific Administrative Rule PDF, PortlandMaps, or the specific BDS program page — written as a complete `https://...` URL, not as link text like "[here]" or "[BDS website]."
   - Example format: "Replacing more than X squares of roofing requires a permit (PCC 24.XX.XXX, https://www.portland.gov/code/24/...)."
   - I will copy this entire answer and paste it into a document, so every citation must survive plain-text copy-paste. Do **not** use footnote markers like "[1]," do **not** put citations only in a Sources list at the end, do **not** say "Portland City Code" or "the BDS website" without a specific section and URL. If you can't find a specific section + URL for a claim, say so out loud ("I couldn't find a specific code section for this — please verify with BDS at 503-823-7300 or https://www.portland.gov/bds") instead of citing vaguely.

4. **My specific property.** Use 5500 SE Belmont St, Portland, OR 97215 to tell me:
   - What zoning is on this property and what that means in plain English (e.g., "single-dwelling residential — most normal house projects are allowed").
   - Whether this property sits inside any **overlay zones** or **special districts** (historic district, conservation district, design overlay, environmental overlay, Sunnyside neighborhood-specific rules, etc.) that change roof rules — for example, requiring a specific roofing material, color, or design review before re-roofing.
   - How to verify all of this myself on **PortlandMaps** (https://www.portlandmaps.com), with the exact steps to look up my address.
   - A friendly warning that zoning and overlays for a specific address can change and I should verify on PortlandMaps before I spend money on materials or labor.

5. **A numbered "what to do next" list, in the order I should do it.** Each step needs to name:
   - Who to call or what website to visit (with full URL and phone number),
   - What to bring or have ready,
   - What it costs (rough dollar range, translated into everyday terms),
   - Roughly how long it takes (in days, weeks, or "one phone call"),
   - Whether the step is free.
   Cover: checking PortlandMaps, calling BDS, hiring a licensed contractor vs. DIY, pulling the permit (online vs. in person), scheduling inspections, what the inspector looks for, and final sign-off.

6. **A "what to ask the city" script** I can read aloud on the phone or paste into an email to BDS. Not just "call BDS" — give me the actual sentences to say, including my address, what I'm planning, and the specific questions to ask so I get a useful answer on the first call.

7. **Contractor vs. DIY.** In plain English:
   - Can I legally re-roof my own house in Portland as the homeowner?
   - If I hire someone, what license/bond/insurance should they have (CCB number, etc.) and how do I verify it (https://www.oregon.gov/ccb)?
   - What are the tradeoffs (cost, warranty, permit responsibility, who's liable if the inspection fails)?

8. **Cost and timeline reality check.**
   - Rough permit fee range for a residential re-roof in Portland, in plain dollars.
   - Whether **System Development Charges (SDCs — fees the city charges to fund infrastructure when you build or expand)** apply to a like-for-like re-roof (I assume no, but confirm).
   - How long the permit typically takes to get (over the counter same day? a week? longer?).
   - How long the actual roof work usually takes for a house this size.
   - Inspection wait times and what happens if I fail an inspection.

9. **Common gotchas — "what you might get wrong."** Plain-language list of the specific mistakes Portland homeowners actually make on a roof replacement:
   - Starting work before pulling the permit.
   - Hiring an unlicensed contractor.
   - Putting a dumpster in the street without a PBOT permit.
   - Discovering rotten sheathing mid-project and not knowing if that changes the permit.
   - Trimming a street tree or a neighbor's tree to access the roof without an Urban Forestry permit.
   - Re-roofing in a historic or conservation district without design review.
   - Forgetting that a final inspection is required before the permit closes, and what happens at resale if it never closed.
   - Stormwater / gutter / downspout disconnect issues if I'm changing how water comes off the roof.

10. **Friendly accuracy caveats** at the end — not legal disclaimers, but neighbor-style warnings: "Zoning for your specific address can change; verify on PortlandMaps before you spend money," "Fee schedules update each July, so call BDS for current numbers," etc.

11. **A glossary** at the very end defining every acronym and technical term used more than once in the answer (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, PF&R, Urban Forestry, PCC, ORSC, SDC, ROW, overlay zone, sheathing, tear-off, square (as in "20 squares of shingles"), ice and water shield, drip edge, etc.) so I can skim back to it.

**Things I do NOT want you to do:**
- Do not lead with a property data table; lead with the answer in words.
- Do not stack code section numbers without explaining each one in plain English right next to it.
- Do not assume I know what BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, Urban Forestry, or PF&R do — name what each one does the first time it appears.

Every citation must be a specific section number AND a full `https://...` URL, inline in the same sentence as the claim. I will copy this whole answer to a clipboard and paste it elsewhere, so anything that doesn't survive plain-text copy-paste is a failure.

col1.response:
I'll dig into this for you. Ladd's Addition is a notable Portland address, so there could be some wrinkles beyond the standard basement permit checklist.Now let me check the property's historic district status, since Ladd's Addition is a historic neighborhood and that affects what's required.Let me verify the property is actually in the historic district and check a couple more specifics.Here's the picture for 1860 SE Ladd Ave, with the Ladd's Addition angle factored in (since that changes a few things you might not see in a generic answer).

## The basic permit bundle

Finishing a basement to habitable space always requires a **building permit** in Portland; there is no DIY exemption, even if you're not adding a bathroom. The moment you frame walls and drywall to create livable area, the permit is on. You'll almost certainly want a **Residential Combination permit**, which bundles building plus the trade permits you need (electrical, plumbing, mechanical) under one IVR number with one set of inspections.

What goes into the application:

1. Building Permit Application (the city has a fillable PDF)
2. A site plan (sample on portland.gov)
3. Architectural plans drawn to scale; can be hand-drawn at 11x17 with 12pt or larger text, no architect required for a basic finish
4. Trade permit applications if doing combination (electrical, mechanical, plumbing)
5. Owner Doing Work form if you're self-permitting

You can do your own electrical only if you both own and occupy the home and are not planning to sell or rent within six months. Plumbing/mechanical can be DIY too if you sign the form.

The code that governs you is **Oregon Residential Specialty Code** plus Portland's **Building Code Guide 96-12 (the "Habitable Spaces Code Guide")**, which is the relaxed-standards document for converting older spaces. This is important because Ladd's Addition homes (mostly 1905-1925 vintage) almost never meet new-construction ceiling heights. Under BCG 96-12 you can get reduced clearances; new-construction standards would be much stricter. The companion document is **Brochure 9** ("Converting Attics, Basements and Garages to Living Space"). Both are free PDFs on portland.gov; pull them before you design anything.

## The Ladd's Addition wrinkle (this is where most generic guides miss the point)

Your property sits inside the **Ladd's Addition Historic District**, which has been on the National Register since 1988. That sounds scary, but here is the actual rule:

**Interior alterations are not subject to Historic Resource Review.** Framing, drywall, electrical, finishes, even adding a bathroom; none of that triggers historic review. You're treated like any other Portland homeowner for interior work.

**The trap is egress.** Oregon R310 requires every basement bedroom to have an emergency escape and rescue opening: 5.7 sq ft of clear opening (5.0 if it's at grade level), minimum 24" tall and 20" wide, with the sill no higher than 44" off the floor. If your basement doesn't already have a window that meets this, you'll need to cut a new opening in the foundation wall and likely build an egress well outside. **Cutting a new exterior opening is an exterior alteration**, which triggers Historic Resource Review (typically Type I, 4-8 week processing), plus the well, grate, and any window style/material may be reviewed for compatibility. The visible-from-street vs rear-of-house axis matters here; rear-facing egress is reviewed more lightly than street-facing.

The cleanest path: make any basement bedroom use an existing window opening if at all possible, and keep new openings (for non-bedroom rooms) on rear elevations. If you're not adding a bedroom at all and can keep the basement as living/family/office space, you avoid the whole egress-window-historic-review problem.

## What you're NOT in for (good news)

A basement-only finish (no exterior wall removal, no footprint expansion) does **not** trigger MRAA (Major Residential Alteration and Addition). That means no 35-day neighbor notification delay and no Recognized Organization notice. The MRAA threshold is 50%+ exterior wall removal or 500+ sq ft of new footprint; a basement finish is well below that.

You also do not need land use review for a normal basement finish. Zoning (Title 33) is reviewed as part of the building permit application.

## Pre-application checklist (the stuff that saves you real money)

Before you even file, do these:

**Pull the permit history for the property.** Portland keeps inspection cards and microfilmed plans for old houses. Order them through portland.gov public records. This tells you what's been permitted previously, which framing you can rely on, and (importantly) flags any prior unpermitted basement work that would need retroactive permitting before you could legally add to it.

**Test for radon.** Portland sits in an elevated radon zone per Oregon Health Authority. A passive sub-slab depressurization rough-in costs hundreds while the slab is exposed; retrofitting after finish costs thousands. Test now; if elevated, plan the rough-in into the framing inspection.

**Plan for lead paint.** Your house is pre-1978 (likely 1905-1925 vintage given the address). Federal RRP rule applies: any disturbance of painted surfaces requires a **Certified Renovator** working for a Certified Firm. If you DIY, you can get certified yourself (one-day class, ~$200-300). If you hire out, verify the CCB license number AND the EPA lead-safe firm certification.

**Check for asbestos** on any old pipe insulation, vinyl floor tile, or popcorn ceiling material before demo. OAR 340-248 governs handling; testing through a licensed lab is the way.

**Consider a voluntary seismic retrofit while walls are open.** Portland's brochure on residential seismic strengthening is free, and the cheapest possible window to bolt the sill plate to the foundation and add shear panels is exactly when the basement walls are framed but not yet drywalled. Ladd's Addition homes are almost always unbolted from their foundations as built. This is a "two-for-one" while you're already in there.

## Two things that will save you time and money

**The free 15-minute appointment with PP&D.** Portland Permitting & Development offers free 15-minute virtual appointments with a Building Code Reviewer, a City Planner, or a Permit Technician. Book one before you finalize plans. Get the egress question, the historic review interplay, and the BCG 96-12 ceiling-height numbers confirmed in writing for your specific situation. Phone is 503-823-7300.

**Oregon Special Assessment of Historic Property.** This is unrelated to your basement permit, but since you're sitting on a contributing structure in Ladd's Addition, you may qualify for a 10-year property tax freeze in exchange for a preservation plan. The tradeoff is that exterior changes get more scrutiny during the assessment period, but if you're doing interior work (basement finish, kitchen, bathrooms) and not touching the street-facing exterior, the math can be very favorable. Worth asking the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) about; applications are accepted in specific windows. To know if you qualify, you need to confirm your home is classified as "contributing" in the district, which you can check on Portland Maps under the Zoning and Districts tab.

## A note on sources

I leaned mostly on portland.gov primary sources (the basement conversion page, BCG 96-12, MRAA page) and the official Ladd's Addition Conservation/Historic District guidelines. Some of the contractor blogs (H&C, Arciform, Lamont Bros, Revive Works) are useful for context but they're also selling services, so I cross-checked their specifics against city documents before passing them along. The 15-minute city appointment is the authoritative way to confirm anything specific to your property.

Want me to dig into a specific piece next? I can pull the actual BCG 96-12 ceiling-height numbers, sketch what a code-compliant egress well looks like under historic review, walk through fee estimation for your likely scope, or check Portland Maps for your home's contributing-structure classification.

col2.response:
I'll dig into both the general permit requirements and the property-specific considerations for your address. Ladd's Addition is a historic district, which adds a layer most "how to permit a basement" guides won't cover, so let me pull both threads at once.Now let me verify the current Portland Permitting Services rules and Ladd's Addition Historic Design Review requirements.Here's the picture for your specific property, and there's one big wrinkle the generic "Portland basement permit" articles will never tell you.

## Your property in one paragraph

1860 SE Ladd Ave is a 1923 single-family home, R5 zoning, 2,662 sq ft, in the **Ladd's Addition Historic District**, classified as a **"Contributing" historic resource** ("Armacher, Max R., Residence" on the Oregon historic register). It's in a **Moderate liquefaction hazard zone** but not in a FEMA flood area. Permit history shows a 2009–2013 alteration/addition project that already went through Historic Design Review (LU 09-159670 HDZ), plus a sewer repair in 2008.

## The big wrinkle: historic review, not just a building permit

Most "finish your basement in Portland" guides assume a normal lot. Yours isn't one. Because the house is a Contributing structure in Ladd's Addition, **any change to the exterior triggers Historic Resource Review (HRR) before a building permit can issue**. For a basement finish, the exterior change you almost certainly need is **egress windows and window wells** for any sleeping rooms. There's a direct precedent two blocks from you: LU 15-165780 HR approved two basement egress aluminum-clad wood windows with concrete window wells, with findings explicitly stating "Historic resource review is required for basement windows and exterior alterations in historic districts that are not otherwise exempt from review". The approval criteria are the **Ladd's Addition Conservation District Design Guidelines, Exterior Rehabilitation** chapter; key practical implications: aluminum-clad wood is acceptable, vinyl is not, and the wells should be concrete (not corrugated metal) and placed below the lowest wood trim line so they don't disrupt the streetside façade.

If you can confine sleeping rooms to areas served by **existing** basement window openings at code-compliant size, you can sometimes avoid HRR; that's worth designing around if at all possible.

## What you'll actually need to permit

For a typical basement finish with bedroom(s) and a bathroom, you'll be filing:

- **Residential Building Permit** for the conversion itself. Portland requires a building permit any time you convert a basement to habitable space, and the project may also need electrical, plumbing, and mechanical permits. A "Residential Combination" permit bundles all of these under one IVR number, which is usually what you want.
- **Electrical, plumbing, mechanical** trade permits (bundled if combo).
- **Historic Resource Review (Type II Land Use Review)** if you're altering any exterior opening, window, or grade. Type II is staff-level (no public hearing) but includes a public notice period; budget 6–10 weeks.
- **Plot plan, floor plans (existing + proposed), section through the basement, and a smoke/CO alarm plan** as part of the building submittal.
- **Structural calcs** if you're cutting a new window opening, removing/modifying a bearing wall, or doing anything to the floor framing above.

## Code-specific things that bite older Ladd's homes

A 1923 house has some predictable issues; flagging in priority order:

1. **Ceiling height.** Code wants 7'0" for habitable space, but Portland's Building Code Guide 96-12 provides alternative standards for existing homes that are more forgiving than new construction. Get this confirmed in plan review before you finalize ductwork or soffits, because a single misplaced beam can cost you a usable room.
2. **Egress.** 5.7 sq ft clear opening, 20" min width, 24" min height, with the 5.0 sq ft reduction allowed at grade and at basements where the well bottom is no more than 44 inches below grade. The well needs a 36" x 36" clear area in front of the window. If the well is deeper than 44", a permanently attached ladder is required.
3. **Foundation bolting.** This is the one I'd push hardest on. Portland explicitly recommends voluntary seismic strengthening while the floor framing and basement walls are exposed; you're in a Moderate liquefaction zone, the house was built in 1923 (predates anchor-bolt requirements), and the walls will be open exactly once. Adding foundation bolts and shear plywood while the studs are exposed costs a fraction of what it does retroactively, and it materially changes the house's earthquake survivability.
4. **Knob-and-tube and 1923-vintage wiring.** Insurance carriers increasingly won't write policies on active K&T. If any is in the basement ceiling/walls, plan to replace it as part of the electrical permit; it's much cheaper open than later.
5. **Radon.** Portland-area homes show measurable radon risk and the Oregon Health Authority recommends testing homes with basements. Test before you frame; if it's high, a sub-slab depressurization rough-in while the slab is accessible is much cheaper than retrofitting.
6. **Moisture.** LVP or porcelain tile are the safe finish floor choices for Portland basements; standard hardwood, laminate, and carpet directly on concrete are not.
7. **Lead paint and possibly asbestos** (vinyl floor tiles, pipe wrap, plaster). RRP-certified contractor handling is required if disturbing painted surfaces.

## Suggested sequence

1. **Pull the existing plans first.** PP&D has microfilmed plans and inspection cards for older houses. The 2010-era HDZ approval (LU 09-159670) and 2011-2013 alteration (2011-111637-RS) on file probably contain framing, foundation, and possibly some basement-relevant drawings. Email PP&D Records or use their public records portal; this can save you a survey.
2. **Free pre-application meeting with PP&D.** They'll tell you exactly which permits and reviews apply, and a Historic Review staffer can flag specific window-well design issues before you spend on an architect.
3. **Bring a designer who has done HRR in Ladd's.** This is not generic remodeling work; familiarity with the Conservation District guidelines will save weeks.
4. **Run radon and consider seismic.** Both are time-of-construction decisions.
5. **Submit HRR (if needed) in parallel with building plan prep.** HRR can run concurrently with building plan check, but no permit issues until HRR is recorded with Multnomah County.
6. **Apply for the combination permit.** Do this through Portland's online permit portal.
7. **Build, inspect, final.**

## A small thing worth verifying

The Multnomah County assessor record currently shows the owner of 1860 SE Ladd as **Joseph T. Nelson** with a 2007 sale on file. If you've recently purchased it, that's normal lag; if not, worth confirming the parcel before you spend on plans, since some permits and HRR submittals require owner authorization.
