# Finishing Your Basement at 1860 SE Ladd Ave: A Plain-English Permit Guide

## The short answer

You'll definitely need permits, but the news is mostly good: **interior basement work in Ladd's Addition is not gated by historic review** — only changes you can see from outside are. So framing, drywall, electrical, and plumbing inside your basement walls do not need to go before any historic landmarks board (Portland Zoning Code Section 33.846.020.A.4 lists what triggers historic review, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846). The big exception is the **egress window** required for any new bedroom, because cutting a new opening in your foundation and installing a window well counts as an exterior change and *does* need historic review (the city has approved similar Ladd's basement-window cases before, like LU 15-165780 HR, https://efiles.portlandoregon.gov/Record/16230506/File/Document). Plan on roughly **$1,500 to $3,500 in city fees** for a typical basement finish with a bedroom and bathroom, plus extra time and money if you need an egress window or you're going for an actual second dwelling unit. Your single most important early step is **a free 15-minute virtual appointment** with a Portland Permitting & Development planner before you spend a dime on plans (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments).

---

## 1. Is Ladd's Addition special? (Yes, but mostly for outside stuff.)

Your address sits inside the **Ladd's Addition Historic District** — a National Register of Historic Places district designated in 1988 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ladd's_Addition). Portland's zoning system flags your specific lot with the historic district code "LA" and identifies your house as a **"Contributing" resource** built in 1923, recorded in the Oregon historic inventory as the *"Max R. Armacher Residence"* (https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=647931). "Contributing" means the building itself helps tell the story of the district, so the city pays closer attention to changes than it would for a non-contributing house.

Here's the key plain-English rule: **the rules about how rooms in your basement have to be built so they're safe to live in are written in the building code, not the historic code**. Interior changes by themselves are not subject to Historic Resource Review (the formal city process where a planner checks your project against historic district rules). A historic preservation Q&A document published by Restore Oregon, summarizing the city's policy, states it directly: interior alterations are not subject to historic resource review unless they cause an exterior change like adding, moving, or removing a window or door (https://restoreoregon.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/03/QA-Handout.pdf). The triggering rule itself is in Portland Zoning Code Section 33.846.060, which is the chapter that governs Historic Resource Reviews (PCC 33.846.060, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

**Your base zoning is R5 (Residential 5,000)** — the most common single-family zoning in Portland — with no overlays beyond the historic district itself (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/base/R5). No conservation district, no environmental zone, no design overlay, no plan district.

The catch: **adding a basement bedroom almost always means cutting a new egress window into your foundation wall**, because old Ladd's basements rarely have one big enough. That window — visible from outside, even partly — is an exterior alteration and does require Historic Resource Review under PCC 33.846.060 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846). Type I (staff-level, no public hearing) review is typical for one new basement window if it faces a side or rear yard and uses a compatible window style; Type II review (8–10 weeks, public notice) gets used for more visible or substantial work.

Your property has been through historic review before — there's a 2009 Type II Historic Design Review on file (LU 09-159670 HDZ, viewable through PortlandMaps permit history at https://www.portlandmaps.com), so this isn't unfamiliar territory for the lot.

## 2. What permits will you likely need?

Portland separates work into a **building permit** (the structural and life-safety side) and three **trade permits** — **plumbing**, **electrical**, and **mechanical** (heating/ventilation). You can bundle them all into a single "Residential Combination Permit" so one inspector schedule covers everything (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/attic-basement-or-garage-conversion).

**Just framing, drywall, and lighting (a finished family room, no bathroom, no bedroom):** Building permit + electrical permit. The state of Oregon explicitly lists "finishing an attic, garage, or basement to make additional living space" as a project that requires a permit (Oregon Building Codes Division, https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/lbdd/pages/oregon-permits.aspx).

**Adding a bathroom (and nothing else habitable):** You can use the **Simple Bathroom Permit** path — a faster, plans-light process — as long as the work is interior only, no load-bearing changes, the property has no more than three toilets total, and it's in your primary residence (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/simple-bathroom-permits). You apply by emailing one PDF to ppdsbp@portlandoregon.gov and the city reviews within five business days.

**Adding a bedroom:** Building permit + the egress window trigger discussed above. Bedrooms in basements must have an emergency escape and rescue opening (an "egress window") with a clear opening of not more than 44 inches above the floor, sized to the Oregon Residential Specialty Code Section R310 standard (PCC reference at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-projects/emergency-escape-and-rescue-openings). In Portland, the standard ORSC sizes are a clear opening of 5.7 square feet (5.0 sq ft if the sill is at grade), with a minimum opening height of 24 inches and minimum opening width of 20 inches (Oregon Residential Specialty Code R310, summarized by H&C Design-Build, https://www.hcdesignbuild.co/blog/portland-basement-finishing-guide/).

**Bedroom + bathroom (the typical basement suite):** Building + plumbing + electrical + mechanical, plus the egress window plus historic review for the window. Best approach is the combination permit.

**Adding a kitchenette — careful here:** Two paths. If the new kitchen is *not* closed off from the main house (no separating door, shared circulation), Portland may treat it as an **"accessory kitchen"** — allowed, but you have to sign a covenant (a deed restriction) saying it won't be used as part of an independent dwelling unit, per Building Official Determination 19-06 (BOD 19-06, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/code-policy/building-official-determinations/bod-19-06-accessory-kitchens). If the kitchen *is* closed off and combined with a bathroom and sleeping space, the city treats the basement as an **Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU)** — a separate, legally independent home (PCC 29.10, item U "Dwelling Unit," https://www.portland.gov/code/29/10). ADUs are allowed in the R5 zone (PCC 33.205, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205), but trigger their own permitting path with extra fees and requirements detailed in Section 4 below.

**Zoning permit:** A separate "zoning permit" usually isn't needed for an interior basement finish in R5 because you're not changing the building's footprint or height. Zoning compliance gets checked as part of the building permit review. The exception is if you create an ADU, which has its own zoning standards (PCC 33.205.030, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205).

**The "minor remodel" question:** Portland used to have a true over-the-counter (same-day) permit for small projects, but that's now mostly online review. There is a **Small Project Permit Program** that aims to review qualifying simple projects within about two days of intake and 15 days total (per a 2024 Mackenzie firm summary, https://www.mcknze.com/city-portland-permit-updates), and the Simple Bathroom Permit above is its own fast-track path. For a full basement conversion, plan on a regular plan review.

## 3. The Portland City Code titles that govern your basement

Portland's building rules live across several code "Titles," each handling a different slice of the work. You don't have to read these; your contractor or designer does. Here's what each one covers in plain English, with the specific sections that hit a basement finish.

**Title 24 — Building Regulations** (https://www.portland.gov/code/24) is the structural and life-safety code; Portland adopts the Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC) by reference for one- and two-family homes (PCC 24.10, https://www.portland.gov/code/24/10). The most important Portland-specific document for your project is **Building Code Guide 96-12: Habitable Spaces** (BCG 96-12, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide), which gives older homes a friendlier ceiling-height standard than new construction. New houses need 7 feet, but BCG 96-12 lets *existing* basements being converted to living space have a minimum ceiling height of **6 feet 8 inches** (with overhead beams as low as 6 feet allowed if they're within 2 feet of a wall), and **6 feet 2 inches** in bathrooms (BCG 96-12 Section 1, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide). For a 1923 Ladd's house, this is often the difference between a feasible project and an impossible one. And BCG 96-12 lets you use a recorded **covenant** — a deed restriction — to allow ceiling heights even slightly lower than 6 feet 8 inches, but only once every five years and never for an ADU or rental (BCG 96-12 Section 8, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide).

**Egress windows** are governed by Oregon Residential Specialty Code Section R310, which Portland enforces; a basement bedroom must have an emergency escape opening, and a basement with any sleeping rooms needs at least one such opening (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-projects/emergency-escape-and-rescue-openings). The clear opening sits no more than 44 inches above the floor (ORSC R310 / Portland code guide, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide). An exterior basement door at least 6 feet 2 inches tall and 30 inches wide can substitute for a window if it's at least one step below the floor above (BCG 96-12 Section 6.3, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide).

**Title 25 — Plumbing** (https://www.portland.gov/code/25) governs water supply, drains, vents, and fixtures. A new bathroom or kitchenette needs a plumbing permit. Portland adopts the Oregon Plumbing Specialty Code by reference (PCC 25.01, https://www.portland.gov/code/25). **Bathroom ventilation** must follow ORSC and is enforced through the plumbing/mechanical permits.

**Title 26 — Electrical** (https://www.portland.gov/code/26) is the wiring code. Adding circuits, outlets, lighting, or upgrading the panel needs an electrical permit. The state Electrical Specialty Code requires **GFCI** (ground-fault circuit interrupter — the protected outlets that trip when they detect a leak to ground) and **AFCI** (arc-fault) protection on circuits in finished basement areas, including bathrooms, kitchens, and unfinished portions; requirements are in the National Electrical Code as adopted by Oregon (Oregon Building Codes Division, https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/Pages/electrical.aspx). Bathroom outlets must be GFCI; bedroom and family-room outlets need AFCI.

**Title 27 — Heating and Ventilating** (https://www.portland.gov/code/27) covers furnaces, ducts, water heaters (the heating side), and bath fans. New ductwork or a relocated furnace needs a mechanical permit.

**Title 29 — Property Maintenance** (https://www.portland.gov/code/29) is the "keep your house safe and habitable" code; Section 29.10 contains the dwelling unit definitions that decide whether a kitchen plus a bedroom plus a bathroom equals an ADU (PCC 29.10, https://www.portland.gov/code/29/10).

**Title 33 — Planning and Zoning** (https://www.portland.gov/code/33) covers what's allowed where. The R5 single-dwelling rules are in Chapter 33.110 (PCC 33.110, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110), ADU standards are in Chapter 33.205 (PCC 33.205, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205), and the historic district rules that would apply if you cut a new exterior window are in Chapter 33.846 (PCC 33.846, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

**Smoke and CO alarms when you pull the permit:** This one surprises homeowners. Pulling a building permit for the basement triggers an upgrade to **the entire house's smoke alarms**, not just the basement. ORSC R314.2.2 says when alterations requiring a permit occur, the whole dwelling unit must be equipped with smoke alarms located as required for new dwellings (Portland summary at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/smoke-alarm-and-carbon-monoxide-alarm-requirements). That means a smoke alarm in every existing bedroom, outside every sleeping area, and on every floor — interconnected so when one goes off, they all do (ORSC R314, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/smoke-alarm-and-carbon-monoxide-alarm-requirements). Carbon monoxide alarms (ORSC R315) are required outside each separate sleeping area and within 15 feet of each bedroom door, in any home with a fuel-fired appliance or attached garage (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/smoke-alarm-and-carbon-monoxide-alarm-requirements). There's a narrow exception: trade-only work like swapping plumbing or repairing siding doesn't trigger the whole-house upgrade, but a basement *building* permit does.

**Insulation upgrades when walls are opened:** When you remove drywall (or there isn't any to remove) on an existing exterior basement wall, Portland's BCG 96-12 requires you insulate that exposed cavity to **R-15** (BCG 96-12 Section 7.2, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide). Existing R-11 or better insulation can stay. The rest of the house is not pulled into the energy code unless you're separately disturbing finishes elsewhere.

**Electrical panel upgrade:** Not automatic. Whether your current panel needs to be upgraded depends on the new circuits' total load. Your electrician runs the load calculation; if the basement adds a kitchen, electric heat, an EV charger, or a heat pump, an upgrade to a 200-amp service is often required. A panel upgrade is a separate electrical permit (about $225, per the 2026 PermitCalculator analysis of Portland fees, https://permitcalculator.com/cities/portland-or/).

**Kitchenette = ADU? It depends.** This is the single most consequential decision in your project. The legal definition of a "Dwelling Unit" in Portland City Code Section 29.10 (item U) requires a space that provides for living, sleeping, eating, *cooking*, and sanitation — independent of the rest of the house (PCC 29.10, https://www.portland.gov/code/29/10). A bedroom + bathroom + a sink and microwave bar that's open to the main house = not a dwelling unit; it's an "accessory kitchen" with a covenant under Building Official Determination 19-06 (BOD 19-06, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/code-policy/building-official-determinations/bod-19-06-accessory-kitchens). A bedroom + bathroom + a real kitchen (range/cooktop) + a separating door = ADU, with all the extras described in Section 4 below.

## 4. Sewer, stormwater, and the BES angle

Your house drains into the **Alder Combined Sewer Basin** — a "combined" system where sanitary sewage and stormwater share one pipe (PortlandMaps environmental data confirms this, https://www.portlandmaps.com). All of inner SE Portland uses combined sewers, which is normal for neighborhoods built before about 1950, but it has two practical consequences: (1) heavy rain can occasionally cause sewer backups in basements, so a backwater valve on any new basement bathroom is a smart investment, and (2) Portland's BES sometimes requires stormwater management when projects expand impervious surfaces, but a basement finish doesn't add any new impervious area, so this rarely applies to you.

**Adding one bathroom to your existing private sewer lateral:** As of a 2019 BES policy update, you only need a **plumbing trade permit (PT)**, not a separate BES sewer connection permit, as long as you're connecting to your existing sewer lateral on private property and not doing work in the right-of-way (https://www.portland.gov/bds/news/2019/4/29/bureau-environmental-services-bes-update). Big simplification.

**If your sewer lateral needs repair or a new connection in the street:** That requires a **UR Permit** (lateral repair) or **UC Permit** (new connection) from BES, in addition to the plumbing permit (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/ur-uc-permit-guide). UR and UC reviews take 1–2 business days and cost roughly $500 each, per the BES guide (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475). For a typical inner SE house with a working lateral, you almost certainly don't need this — but it's worth running a **sewer scope** ($100–$200 from a plumber) before you commit, especially because some Ladd's homes have shared "party sewers" that complicate things (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/bes/article/411807).

**System Development Charges (SDCs) and the basement bedroom myth:** A common worry is "if I add a bedroom, do I owe SDCs?" Generally no, for a remodel that doesn't create a new dwelling unit. Portland's SDC page lists "remodels that don't change the use of the building" as exempt (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/system-development-charges). SDCs are tied to creating new dwelling units, not adding bedrooms or bathrooms within an existing one.

**SDCs if you do create an ADU:** ADUs trigger four SDCs — Sanitary, Stormwater, Parks, and Transportation — that together can run roughly $15,000–$30,000 for a typical ADU at current rates, which would feel like buying a used Honda Civic worth of fees on top of construction. But Portland has an **ADU SDC Waiver Program** that waives all four SDCs in exchange for a **10-year covenant** that the unit (and the rest of the property) won't be used as a short-term rental like Airbnb (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver). To apply, you email a completed application packet to aducovenant@portlandoregon.gov.

**Temporary SDC exemption (worth knowing about):** From August 15, 2025 through September 30, 2028, Portland is temporarily exempting most newly created housing units from SDCs, but **ADUs are explicitly excluded** from this temporary program (PCC 17.14.070(J), https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/housing-sdc-exemption). For ADUs the only path to waived SDCs remains the 10-year covenant program above.

**BES contact for sewer questions:** PP&D Utilities Engineering at **503-823-7761** handles sewer and stormwater questions for development, including lateral permits (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475).

## 5. Costs and timelines, in everyday terms

Portland's permitting agency budgets for fees that update every July. Here's what a planner can estimate for you.

A typical basement finish that adds a family room, a bedroom (with new egress window), and a small bathroom for a construction valuation around $40,000–$60,000 will run roughly **$1,500–$3,500 in city fees combined** — building permit, plan review, plumbing, electrical, mechanical, and the small Development Services Fee — based on the FY 2025–26 Portland fee schedule (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/building-and-other-permits-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download) and a 2026 analysis by PermitCalculator estimating Portland fees by valuation (https://permitcalculator.com/cities/portland-or/). For perspective, that's about a third of what a single high-end appliance costs in the finished kitchen — small relative to the project as a whole.

Portland values residential alterations at **33.3% of new-construction cost per square foot** from the ICC valuation data table, so a 600-square-foot basement finish typically values around $30,000–$50,000 for fee purposes (Portland Valuation Methods page, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/valuation-methods). The **Oregon state surcharge of 12%** gets added to every permit (https://permitcalculator.com/cities/portland-or/).

**Plan review timeline:** Roughly **3–8 weeks** for a residential basement conversion that doesn't need historic review or Major Residential Alteration & Addition (MRAA) treatment. The Simple Bathroom Permit reviews in **5 business days** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/simple-bathroom-permits). If you add a new exterior window in the historic district, layer on **4–8 weeks** for a Type I Historic Resource Review or **8–10 weeks** for a Type II review (Lamont Bros. summary of Portland review types, https://www.lamontbros.com/learning-center/a-guide-to-remodeling-in-portland-historic-districts).

**Total project time** from "I have a sketch" to "passed final inspection" is realistically **4–8 months** — about one summer's worth of weekends plus the lead time before you start swinging hammers.

**Inspection sequence (the order people show up):** After your permit is issued, you call the city's automated inspection request line **503-823-7000** with your IVR (the unique permit number) and an inspection code (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/attic-basement-or-garage-conversion). The typical sequence is rough framing → rough plumbing → rough electrical → rough mechanical → insulation/drywall → final plumbing → final electrical → final mechanical → final building. An inspector checks each stage before it's covered up. Inspectors come from PP&D's residential inspections team, **PPDinspections@portlandoregon.gov, 503-823-7388** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-inspections).

**Comparing the three most common basement finish scenarios** — a one-paragraph summary above the table is: a family room is the cheapest and fastest path, a bedroom-and-bathroom suite is the typical project, and an ADU is dramatically more involved.

| Scenario | City fees (rough) | Plan review time | Triggers historic review? | SDCs apply? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Family room only (no plumbing, no bedroom) | ~$700–$1,200 | 3–6 weeks | No | No |
| Bedroom + bathroom (typical) | ~$1,500–$3,500 | 4–10 weeks | Yes — for the new egress window | No |
| Full basement ADU | ~$5,000–$8,000 + SDCs of $15k–$30k unless waived | 8–14 weeks | Likely yes | Yes (waivable with 10-year covenant) |

Fee ranges are planning estimates based on the Portland FY 2025–26 building permit schedule (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/building-and-other-permits-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download) and PermitCalculator's 2026 Portland analysis (https://permitcalculator.com/cities/portland-or/); your final number depends on construction valuation.

## 6. Who exactly to contact

The big change you may not know about: **the Bureau of Development Services (BDS) was phased out as of June 2024**, replaced by **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** (Radler White Parks & Alexander legal summary, https://radlerwhite.com/blog/major-structural-change-in-city-of-portland-permitting-administration/). PP&D absorbed permitting roles from BDS, BES, PBOT, Water, and Parks. So the modern front door for almost everything is PP&D.

**Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D)** — your main contact for everything. Phone **503-823-7300** (M–F, 8 a.m.–5 p.m.), main page https://www.portland.gov/ppd, contact form https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact, address 1900 SW 4th Avenue, Suite 5000, Portland, OR 97201. Free 15-minute appointments at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments. Online portal for applying and paying for permits is **DevHub PDX** at https://devhub.portlandoregon.gov.

**Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)** — sewer and stormwater. For development-related sewer questions, use **PP&D Utilities Engineering at 503-823-7761** (https://www.portlandoregon.gov/BES/article/298475). General BES site: https://www.portland.gov/bes.

**Portland Bureau of Transportation (PBOT)** — only matters if your project touches the public **right-of-way** (the area between your property line and the street, including sidewalks). For basement work it almost never does. Driveway-related questions: **503-823-7002, option 3** (https://www.portland.gov/transportation/development/driveways).

**Permitting & Development front door (the 2026 name):** That's PP&D. The "PP&D" acronym is the current branding — you'll see it on city pages and signage at 1900 SW 4th. The website is https://www.portland.gov/ppd. Some older city pages still link to portlandoregon.gov/bds — those are legacy pages; the current site is portland.gov/ppd.

**Urban Forestry** — under Portland Parks & Recreation, governs trees under Title 11. For a basement finish that doesn't disturb trees, you usually have nothing to do here. But Ladd's Addition is famous for its American elm canopy maintained by the Save Our Elms organization (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ladd's_Addition), and Urban Forestry reviews any project valued at $25,000 or greater that *might* impact a street tree, city tree, or heritage tree — including digging a window well near one (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit/tree-requirements-building-permits). Phone **503-823-TREE (8733)**, email **trees@portlandoregon.gov**, page https://www.portland.gov/trees.

**Portland Fire & Rescue (PF&R)** — for one- and two-family dwellings, fire-bureau review for a basement finish is uncommon and gets handled inside PP&D's normal building plan review. PF&R life-safety review is usually invoked only for ADUs with separation requirements or for unusual layouts. Their general info: https://www.portland.gov/fire. If you go the ADU route, the 1-hour fire separation between basement ADU and main dwelling is enforced through PP&D's plan check, not a separate fire permit (https://www.permitpaths.com/permits/portland/scenarios/basement-finishing).

**Historic Resource staff at PP&D** — for Ladd's Addition questions, ask the 15-minute appointment system to schedule you with a "City Planner" specifically for historic review questions (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments). General planning/zoning questions also flow through **503-823-7700** historically used for the Bureau of Planning & Sustainability; for permit-stage historic review, route through PP&D's main 503-823-7300 line.

## 7. Common gotchas in Portland for basement finishes

These are the mistakes Portland homeowners actually make.

**Framing in a bedroom without an egress window and getting red-tagged at framing inspection.** A "red tag" means the inspector orders the work stopped until corrected. This happens because owners frame the closet wall first, then realize the existing window is too small or too high. The fix is expensive — concrete cutting, structural header, window well, drainage, and Historic Resource Review for the exterior change. *Always* confirm your egress strategy on plans before you order lumber (https://www.vreshconstruction.com/egress-windows).

**Accidentally creating an unpermitted ADU by adding a stove.** A range or cooktop in the basement, plus a bedroom and bathroom, plus a separating door that creates an "independent living" space, equals an ADU under the dwelling unit definition in PCC 29.10 (https://www.portland.gov/code/29/10). If you don't go through ADU permitting, the city can issue a violation; future buyers will discover it during home inspection or a permit history check. A microwave and bar sink in an open-plan accessory kitchen, with a signed covenant under BOD 19-06, is the safe path (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/code-policy/building-official-determinations/bod-19-06-accessory-kitchens).

**Hiring an unlicensed contractor and being personally on the hook.** Oregon law requires construction contractors to be licensed by the **Oregon Construction Contractors Board (CCB)** with a current bond and insurance (https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/index.aspx). If an unlicensed contractor damages your house, hurts themselves, or walks off with a deposit, your legal recourse is dramatically weaker, and you can be on the hook for unpaid subcontractors and workers' comp. Verify any contractor's CCB license at https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/search/Pages/default.aspx before signing.

**Doing the work without a permit and having it surface during a future home sale.** Oregon real estate disclosure forms ask sellers to identify known code compliance issues. A non-permitted basement bedroom is a known nonconforming condition that has to be disclosed; below-grade bedrooms without egress are commonly called out in inspection reports (https://www.vreshconstruction.com/egress-windows). The cheaper-feeling option of skipping a permit becomes an expensive renegotiation — or a deal-killer — at closing.

**Investigation fees for work started before the permit.** Portland charges an investigation fee with a minimum of $153 per hour for unpermitted work the city catches mid-project (https://permitcalculator.com/cities/portland-or/). It's not a flat fine; it's hourly for as long as it takes to figure out what's there.

**Ceiling height being just under code in older Ladd's Addition basements.** Many 1900s–1920s SE Portland basements measure 6 feet 4 inches to 6 feet 6 inches floor-to-joist. The BCG 96-12 standard of 6 feet 8 inches is friendlier than the 7-foot new-construction rule but still hard to hit if joists are deep or ducts hang low. Solutions before you commit: lower the slab (expensive but possible), reroute ducts, or use a covenant to allow a small portion below code (covenant allowed only every 5 years, never for an ADU, per BCG 96-12 Section 8, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide). Measure carefully before drawing plans.

**Missing the whole-house smoke alarm upgrade** required by ORSC R314.2.2 when you pull a structural permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/smoke-alarm-and-carbon-monoxide-alarm-requirements). Inspectors check for hardwired, interconnected alarms in every existing bedroom, outside every sleeping area, and on every floor. Plan to have your electrician swap them all when they wire the basement — much easier than adding it as an afterthought.

**Combined-sewer backup risk in inner SE.** Heavy storms can briefly back combined-sewer systems up; if your basement bathroom doesn't have a backwater valve, you can get a sewage-water backup into your new bathroom. Backwater valves are required in many cases by the Oregon Plumbing Specialty Code and are a small added cost on the rough plumbing (https://www.portland.gov/code/25). Worth it.

**Phishing scam targeting permit applicants.** PP&D has issued a public warning about scammers posing as city officials and asking for wire transfers to "complete review" (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/development-permit-processes). The city does not request payment by wire. Pay only through DevHub or in person at 1900 SW 4th.

**Letting your designer skip the historic review pre-check.** If you cut even one new exterior opening, factor in 4–10 weeks of historic review on top of building plan review. Asking the staff planner during your 15-minute appointment whether your specific opening location qualifies for Type I (faster, staff-only) versus Type II (slower, public notice) Historic Resource Review can save weeks (PCC 33.846.060, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

## 8. Numbered next-step list (in the order to do them)

These are sequenced so the cheap, low-risk steps come first. Do not skip the early ones; they save real money.

**Step 1 — Look up your property on PortlandMaps. Free. 15 minutes.** Go to https://www.portlandmaps.com, enter 1860 SE Ladd Ave, click into the Permits, Zoning, Environmental, and Sewer tabs. Confirm zoning (R5), historic district (Ladd's Addition), and pull up the existing permit history so you know what's already been built and approved. This data is what an experienced designer charges you to look up.

**Step 2 — Book a free 15-minute appointment with a PP&D City Planner. Free. 15 minutes plus prep.** Schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments. Pick "City Planner" (for zoning, historic district, ADU questions) or "Building Code and Engineering Reviewer" (for ceiling height, egress, structural questions) — or both, in two separate appointments. Bring the questions in Section 9 below.

**Step 3 — Get a sewer scope. ~$100–$200. 1–2 hours on site.** Hire a plumber to camera-scope your sewer lateral. This catches problems (root intrusion, party sewer connections, broken pipe) before you spend money on plans. Find a plumber on the city's Sewer Contractors List (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/understanding-sewer-connection).

**Step 4 — Measure the basement. Free. 30 minutes.** Floor-to-joist height in five locations across the basement. If the lowest measurement is below 6 feet 8 inches, flag this for your designer; you may need to plan around beams or use BCG 96-12's covenant exemption (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide). Note any existing windows' rough opening dimensions and sill heights — most existing Ladd's basement windows are too small and too high to count for egress.

**Step 5 — Decide your scope (with your spouse, if applicable). Free. One conversation.** Family room only? Bedroom + bathroom? Kitchenette but not a full ADU? Full ADU? Each path has different cost, time, and complexity. The decision changes everything downstream.

**Step 6 — Hire a designer or design-build contractor experienced in historic districts. ~$2,500–$10,000 for plans depending on scope. 4–8 weeks.** Verify CCB license at https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/search/Pages/default.aspx. Ask specifically whether they've worked in Ladd's Addition; the historic review experience matters. They'll produce architectural plans, a site plan, and (if needed) structural calculations meeting the Convert an Attic, Basement or Garage submittal requirements (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/submittal-requirements-convert-attic-basement-or-garage/download).

**Step 7 — If you'll need an exterior egress window, file Historic Resource Review concurrently with the building permit. 4–10 weeks for review.** Type I review fees start around $250 for minor alterations; Type II fees range higher (per Portland's Historic Resource Reviews fee page at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/zoning-land-use/land-use-review-fees-and-types/historic-resource-reviews). Your designer or contractor handles this; you sign as property owner.

**Step 8 — Apply for your building permit (and trade permits, bundled as a Residential Combination Permit) on DevHub. ~$1,500–$3,500 in city fees. 3–8 weeks for review.** Submit at https://devhub.portlandoregon.gov. Upload the building permit application, site plan, architectural plans, and the trade permit applications (electrical, plumbing, mechanical) (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/attic-basement-or-garage-conversion).

**Step 9 — Pay the fees, receive the issued permit. ~Same week as approval.** Permits and IVR numbers are issued on DevHub once you pay. Print the inspection record card and post it on site.

**Step 10 — Request your first inspection. Free (included in permit). Same-week scheduling.** Call **503-823-7000** with your IVR number and the inspection code. Rough framing first; the inspector walks through and signs off before drywall goes up.

**Step 11 — Build through the inspection sequence to final.** Each stage gets inspected. Don't cover work until it passes.

**Step 12 — Final inspection. Free.** When the last inspector signs off (final building), the permit is closed and the work is officially recorded as legal. Save your IVR-stamped final inspection record forever; it lives in your house's permit history on PortlandMaps and protects you at sale.

## 9. The "what to ask the city" script

Read this aloud or paste it into the Send-us-a-Message form at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/contact, or use it during your 15-minute appointment.

> "Hi, my name is [your name]. I own the property at **1860 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214**. I'm researching an unfinished basement conversion and want to confirm a few things before I spend money on plans.
>
> First: my property is inside the Ladd's Addition Historic District as a Contributing resource per PortlandMaps. Can you confirm that interior basement work alone — framing, drywall, electrical, plumbing, mechanical — does *not* trigger Historic Resource Review, and that historic review is only needed if I add or modify exterior features like windows, doors, or a window well?
>
> Second: I'm considering finishing the basement in one of three ways — (a) family room only with no plumbing, (b) family room + bedroom + bathroom, or (c) family room + bedroom + bathroom + a small kitchenette. For each option, can you tell me which permits I'll need (building, plumbing, electrical, mechanical, zoning), whether option (c) would be considered an Accessory Dwelling Unit or could be handled as an accessory kitchen with a covenant under BOD 19-06, and whether any of the three options would trigger System Development Charges?
>
> Third: my basement has approximately [X] feet [X] inches of floor-to-joist clearance at its lowest point. Can you confirm whether Building Code Guide 96-12 lets me use the 6-foot-8-inch alternative ceiling height standard, and what the process would be for a covenant if a small area falls below that?
>
> Fourth: if I add a basement bedroom and need a new egress window cut into the foundation, what type of Historic Resource Review (Type I, Type Ix, Type II) would apply, and roughly how long does that review take in addition to the building permit review?
>
> Fifth: my house is in the Alder Combined Sewer Basin. If I add one bathroom that connects to my existing private sewer lateral on the property, is the plumbing trade permit alone sufficient, or would I also need a UC or UR permit through BES?
>
> Could you also confirm the current PP&D contact information for the historic resource staff planner I'd work with, and let me know whether a free 15-minute appointment with a planner is the right next step?
>
> Thank you."

## 10. Friendly accuracy warnings

These are not legal disclaimers; they're real things that can change between now and when you submit a permit, and double-checking saves money.

**Zoning, overlays, and historic status for your specific address can change.** Verify on PortlandMaps before spending money on plans (https://www.portlandmaps.com). Your house is currently shown as R5 zoning, Ladd's Addition Historic District, Contributing classification, no overlays — but city plan amendments happen periodically.

**Permit fees and SDCs are updated annually each July.** The numbers in this guide are based on the FY 2025–26 schedule effective July 1, 2025 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/building-and-other-permits-fee-schedule-city-portland-effective-july-1-2025/download). They go up about 5% most years (https://www.portland.gov/council/documents/ordinance/ppd-fee-change-ordinance-fy25-26). Use these figures for planning, not as a quote. Your final number depends on the construction valuation entered on your permit application.

**The BDS-to-PP&D transition is recent.** Some city web pages still link to portlandoregon.gov/bds — those redirect or are legacy. The current site is portland.gov/ppd. If you see "BDS" in a 2024-or-older PDF, mentally substitute "PP&D" and the same staff is doing the same work.

**The temporary SDC exemption sunsets September 30, 2028.** If you go the ADU route and want to time it around that program, remember ADUs are *excluded* from the temporary exemption — the only ADU SDC waiver is the 10-year covenant program (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/adu-sdc-waiver).

**Permit timelines are estimates, not guarantees.** Portland's permitting volume swings with the economy; review times can lengthen during busy periods. Build a buffer into your plans.

**This guide does not replace a free 15-minute appointment with a real PP&D planner.** I can be wrong, codes can change, and your address is the only one that matters. The 15-minute appointment is the fastest way to confirm everything specific to your project, and it's free.

## 11. Glossary

**ADU (Accessory Dwelling Unit)** — A second, complete, legally independent home on the same lot as a single-family house. Has its own kitchen, bathroom, sleeping area, and entrance. Allowed in R5 under PCC 33.205 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/200s/205).

**BCG (Building Code Guide)** — A Portland document that explains how the city interprets a tricky part of the building code. BCG 96-12 is the habitable spaces guide that gives older basements a friendlier ceiling height standard (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide).

**BDS (Bureau of Development Services)** — Former name for Portland's permitting bureau. Phased out and replaced by PP&D as of June 2024 (https://radlerwhite.com/blog/major-structural-change-in-city-of-portland-permitting-administration/).

**BES (Bureau of Environmental Services)** — Portland's sewer, stormwater, and watershed bureau. https://www.portland.gov/bes.

**BOD (Building Official Determination)** — A formal interpretation by Portland's Building Official on a specific code question, like BOD 19-06 on accessory kitchens (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/code-policy/building-official-determinations/bod-19-06-accessory-kitchens).

**CCB (Construction Contractors Board)** — Oregon's state agency that licenses construction contractors. Required for anyone doing construction work for hire (https://www.oregon.gov/ccb/Pages/index.aspx).

**Contributing resource** — A building inside a historic district that the city considers important to the district's historic character. Yours is one. Triggers more careful historic review than non-contributing buildings (PCC 33.846, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

**Combination Permit / Residential Combination Permit** — A bundled permit that includes building, plumbing, electrical, and mechanical, all under one IVR number with one set of inspections (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/attic-basement-or-garage-conversion).

**Combined Sewer** — A sewer system where sanitary waste and stormwater share one pipe. All of inner SE Portland uses combined sewers (https://www.portland.gov/bes).

**Covenant** — A deed restriction recorded against the property title, enforceable by the city. Used in Portland for accessory kitchens (BOD 19-06), ceiling-height exemptions (BCG 96-12), and the ADU SDC waiver.

**DevHub PDX** — Portland's online permit portal at https://devhub.portlandoregon.gov.

**DSC (Development Services Center)** — PP&D's in-person counter at 1900 SW 4th Ave., Suite 5000 (https://www.portland.gov/service-locations/development-services-center).

**Egress window / emergency escape and rescue opening** — A window large enough for a person to escape through during a fire. Required in every basement bedroom and in any basement containing a sleeping room (ORSC R310, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/residential-projects/emergency-escape-and-rescue-openings).

**GFCI (ground-fault circuit interrupter)** — An outlet (or breaker) that trips quickly when it senses electricity leaking to ground. Required in bathrooms, kitchens, and unfinished basement areas under the National Electrical Code (https://www.oregon.gov/bcd/codes-stand/Pages/electrical.aspx).

**Habitable space** — A space used for living, sleeping, eating, or cooking. Defined in BCG 96-12, has minimum ceiling height, light, ventilation, and egress requirements (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/codes-rules-and-guides/bcg-96-12-habitable-space-code-guide).

**Historic Resource Review (HRR)** — The land-use review process for changes to historic resources or buildings inside historic districts. Governed by PCC 33.846 (https://www.portland.gov/code/33/800s/846).

**IVR (Interactive Voice Response)** — The automated phone system at 503-823-7000 used to schedule inspections; also the unique number assigned to your permit.

**MRAA (Major Residential Alteration & Addition)** — A larger residential project that triggers neighborhood notification and a delay period (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/major-residential-alteration-addition-permits).

**ORSC (Oregon Residential Specialty Code)** — The state-level building code for one- and two-family homes, adopted by Portland under PCC Title 24 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/oregon-residential-specialty-code).

**Overlay zone** — An additional zoning rule layered on top of base zoning, like a design overlay or environmental overlay. Your property has no overlays beyond the historic district itself.

**PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)** — Portland's streets and right-of-way bureau (https://www.portland.gov/transportation).

**PCC (Portland City Code)** — The Portland city code. Cited as PCC followed by title and chapter (e.g., PCC 33.846).

**PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue)** — Portland's fire bureau (https://www.portland.gov/fire).

**PP&D (Portland Permitting & Development)** — Portland's permitting bureau as of June 2024, replacing BDS. https://www.portland.gov/ppd.

**Right-of-way (ROW)** — The publicly owned strip between your property line and the street, including sidewalks and the planting strip.

**SDC (System Development Charge)** — One-time impact fees Portland charges for new development to offset effects on parks, sewers, water, and streets (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules/system-development-charges).

**Setback** — The minimum distance from property line to building. Defined in the base zoning chapter (PCC 33.110 for R5, https://www.portland.gov/code/33/100s/110).

**Simple Bathroom Permit** — Portland's fast-track bathroom permit path, reviewed in five business days (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/residential-permitting/home-projects/simple-bathroom-permits).

**UC permit / UR permit** — BES sewer permits. UC is for new connections to the public sewer; UR is for sewer lateral repairs (https://www.portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/ur-uc-permit-guide).

---

## One last surprise — a property fact you didn't ask about

Your house is recorded in the Oregon historic inventory as the **"Max R. Armacher Residence,"** built 1923, classified as a *Contributing* resource in Ladd's Addition (https://heritagedata.prd.state.or.us/historic/index.cfm?do=v.dsp_siteSummary&resultDisplay=647931). That historic name is searchable when you talk to PP&D's historic planners, and it gives the staff context faster than just an address. Drop "the Armacher Residence on Ladd Ave" into your 15-minute appointment and watch how quickly the planner finds your property in the system. Small thing, big time-saver.

finish-basement.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know what permits I need to finish my basement at 1860 SE Ladd Ave, Portland, OR 97214, and I want the answer written for a regular Portland homeowner — not a contractor or a code official. Please write for a Portland resident or small-business owner with no code or permitting background. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but unfamiliar with city processes.

My situation: I own a house in inner SE Portland (the Ladd's Addition area) and I want to turn my unfinished basement into usable living space. I'm not sure yet whether I want a bedroom, a bathroom, a family room, or all of the above, but I want to understand the full permitting picture before I start spending money on plans or a contractor. I don't know what's allowed, what's required, what it will cost, how long it will take, or who at the city I'm supposed to call. Please answer all of that.

Lead with a plain-English summary that answers my actual question directly in 3–5 sentences before any tables, citations, or code language. Use everyday words instead of code language wherever possible — say "the rules about how rooms in your basement have to be built so they're safe to live in" before saying "habitable space requirements"; say "fees the city charges when you add a bedroom or bathroom" before saying "System Development Charges (SDCs)." Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, PF&R, PCC, ORSC, ADU, SDC, ROW, egress, etc.). Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords inside sentences instead of dense tables. Only use a table if you're comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table. Translate dollar figures and timelines into something I can plan around (e.g., "about the price of a used Honda Civic", "roughly one summer's worth of weekends").

Specifically, please cover:

1. **Is Ladd's Addition special?** 1860 SE Ladd Ave is inside the Ladd's Addition Historic District (a National Register district). Does that affect interior basement work, or only exterior changes? Does the Historic Resource Review or Historic Landmarks Commission have any say over what I do inside my basement, and if so, when? What's the base zoning at this address, and are there any overlays (historic, environmental, plan district, design overlay) that affect basement remodels?

2. **What permit(s) do I likely need?** Walk me through which combinations of **Building**, **Electrical**, **Plumbing**, **Mechanical**, and (if applicable) **Zoning** permits I'll need depending on what I do — for example: just framing and drywall vs. adding a bathroom vs. adding a bedroom (which usually triggers egress window requirements) vs. adding a kitchenette (which can turn the basement into an ADU and trigger a whole different process). What specifically *triggers* each permit type? Is there a "minor remodel" path I can use, or do I need a full plan review?

3. **What Titles of Portland City Code govern this?** I expect this touches **Title 24 (Building Regulations)**, **Title 25 (Plumbing)**, **Title 26 (Electrical)**, **Title 27 (Heating and Ventilating)**, **Title 29 (Property Maintenance)**, and **Title 33 (Zoning Code)**. Tell me which sections of each apply to a basement finish, and explain in plain English what each section requires. Specifically address:
   - Ceiling height minimums for a basement to count as "living space"
   - Egress window requirements for any room used as a bedroom (size, sill height, well dimensions)
   - Bathroom ventilation, GFCI outlets, smoke and CO alarms throughout the house when you pull a permit
   - Whether finishing the basement triggers an upgrade to the rest of the house's electrical panel, smoke alarms, or insulation
   - Whether adding a second kitchen (even a "kitchenette") legally creates an **Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU)** and what that means for permits, fees, and zoning

4. **Sewer, stormwater, and the BES angle.** If I'm adding a bathroom, what does the **Bureau of Environmental Services (BES)** require? Are there sewer connection fees or branch line issues for an old inner SE house? Is there anything special about Ladd's Addition's sewer system I should know?

5. **What will it cost (ballpark) and how long will it take?** Translate the fees into everyday terms. Cover:
   - Typical permit fees for a basement finish in Portland (building + trades)
   - Whether **System Development Charges (SDCs)** apply — and if so, when (e.g., adding a bedroom vs. adding an ADU)
   - Plan review timelines (over the counter vs. full review)
   - Typical inspection sequence (who comes out, in what order, and what they're checking)

6. **Who exactly do I contact, and how?** Give me the actual front door for each one — phone number, email, and the specific online portal page:
   - **BDS (Bureau of Development Services)** — the main permitting bureau
   - **BES (Bureau of Environmental Services)** — sewer and stormwater
   - **PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation)** — only if it touches the right-of-way (ROW)
   - **PP&D (Permitting & Development)** — note: I've seen the city reorganize permitting recently; tell me the *current* (2026) name of the permitting front door and which website to use
   - **Urban Forestry** — if any tree work is involved
   - **PF&R (Portland Fire & Rescue)** — for any fire/life-safety review
   - **Historic Resource staff at BDS** — for Ladd's Addition questions

7. **Common gotchas in Portland for basement finishes.** Name the specific mistakes Portland homeowners make — e.g., framing in a bedroom without an egress window and getting red-tagged at inspection, accidentally creating an unpermitted ADU by adding a stove, hiring an unlicensed contractor and being personally on the hook, doing the work without a permit and having it surface during a future home sale, ceiling height being just under code in older Ladd's Addition basements, and so on.

8. **A numbered "what to do next" list, in the exact order I should do it.** For each step, tell me: who to call or what website to visit, what to bring or upload, roughly what it costs, and roughly how long it takes. Mark which steps are free. Start with the free, low-risk steps (look up zoning on PortlandMaps, call BDS for a free pre-application chat) before the expensive ones (hiring a designer, submitting plans).

9. **A "what to ask the city" script** I can read aloud over the phone or paste into an email to BDS. Not just "call BDS" — give me the actual sentences to say, including my address, what I want to do, and the specific questions I need answered (e.g., "Is my address inside the Ladd's Addition Historic District for permitting purposes? Does finishing my basement with a bedroom and bathroom require historic review? Will adding a kitchenette make this an ADU?").

10. **Accuracy caveats.** Frame them as friendly warnings, not legal disclaimers — e.g., "Zoning and overlays for your specific address can change; verify on PortlandMaps before spending money on plans," and "Permit fees and SDCs are updated annually; the numbers here are a planning estimate, not a quote."

11. **A glossary at the end** of every acronym and technical term you used more than once (BDS, BES, PBOT, PP&D, PF&R, PCC, ORSC, ADU, SDC, ROW, egress, setback, overlay, etc.).

**Citations — this is the most important rule.** I am going to copy your entire answer and paste it into a document, so every citation has to survive plain-text copy-paste. For every factual claim — every rule, number, fee, timeline, contact, code section — put the citation **inline, in the same sentence as the claim**, in this format: the exact code section (e.g., `PCC 24.10.045`, not just "Title 24") AND a full working URL to the authoritative source (e.g., `https://www.portland.gov/code/24/10/045` or the specific Administrative Rule PDF or PortlandMaps page or BDS program page). Example: "Bedrooms in a basement must have an emergency escape window with a clear opening of at least 5.7 square feet (PCC 24.10.045 / 2023 ORSC R310, https://www.portland.gov/code/24/10/045)."

Do **not** use vague citations like "Portland City Code", "Title 33", "the BDS website", "city regulations", "[1]", "see source above", or footnote-only references. Do **not** put citations only in a Sources list at the end. Do **not** use link text like "[here]" or "the city's website" — write out full `https://...` URLs in the body. If you can't find a specific section and URL for a claim, say so honestly in that sentence ("I could not find a specific code section for this — verify with BDS at 503-823-7300") instead of citing vaguely. A sources list at the end is fine *in addition to* inline citations, but never *instead of* them.

Finally, do **not** lead with a property data table; lead with the answer in plain English. Do **not** use words like "shall", "promulgated", "thereunder", or other legal-register language — write the way a friendly, knowledgeable city employee would explain it to me over coffee.

col1.response:
I'll dig into this for you. Portland has specific rules for sandwich boards (A-frame signs), and the answer depends on both your zoning and the sidewalk situation. Let me check the actual rules and your specific location.Now let me get the full official rules and check the specific zoning at your address.Let me verify your specific zoning since the rules require a Commercial zone, and check sidewalk width.# Short answer: yes, but you have to register it first.

Your address sits in downtown Portland's CX (Central Commercial) zone, which is one of the zones where permanent portable signs are allowed. (You'll want to confirm CX on [PortlandMaps](https://www.portlandmaps.com); I'm inferring from the address since I can't pull the parcel directly, but downtown inside the 405 loop is CX with very few exceptions.)

## What you actually have to do

Register the sign with Portland Permitting & Development. Registration runs $114 for 1 year, $203 for 2 years, or $364 for 4 years (fees valid July 1, 2025 to July 1, 2026). The form is a PDF you mail in with a check made out to City of Portland; address is 1900 SW 4th Ave, Ste 5000, Portland OR 97201, Attn: A-Board Sign Permits. No design review needed.

Form: [Portable (A-Board) Sign Registration Form](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/portable-board-sign-registration-form/download)

## The placement rules people get wrong

The single most important thing (and the one most businesses screw up): in the sidewalk area, the sign must be within 6 inches of the curb, not against the building. Counterintuitive but firm; it keeps the pedestrian path clear.

Other constraints that matter for SW Morrison specifically:

- Sidewalk must be at least 8 feet wide; you must leave a clear 6-foot pedestrian path. Downtown sidewalks are usually 12 to 15 feet, so you're almost certainly fine, but measure to be safe.
- Max 8 sq ft per face, max 4 ft tall, max 30 inches wide when in the right-of-way (12 sq ft is only allowed if you're entirely on private property).
- One sign per public entrance; if you share an entrance with other tenants, only one sign total for the whole entrance.
- Not allowed at street corners, near transit stops, at ADA curb ramps, or at building exits/fire escapes. Worth flagging: your block has Portland Streetcar stops at 10th & Alder and 11th & Taylor, so the corners are extra constrained.
- Cannot be attached to anything (poles, meters, trees, the sidewalk itself); no lights, no electrical, no digital displays.

## Things worth knowing that the city page doesn't lead with

**Wind.** SW Morrison gets gusty, especially in winter. The standard hinged A-frame will eat it; either go with a wind-resistant suspended-panel design (frame stays put, the inner panel swings) or weight the base. Plan to bring it in nightly anyway since it must come in after business hours.

**Enforcement is real.** Inspections are random or complaint-based, and three citations on the same sign means it can be impounded, your registration revoked, and future registration prohibited. Getting registered first is much cheaper than fighting an impound.

**Free help before you commit.** PPD offers a [free 15-minute appointment](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments) with a Residential & Signs permitting tech; they'll confirm your zone, sidewalk situation, and entrance count before you spend the $114. Phone: 503-823-7300.

**Downtown Clean & Safe** is the enhanced services district covering your block; they don't have separate sign authority but their ambassadors do report violations, so a registered sign is good citizenship as much as compliance.

**Design that actually pays back the $114:** keep the message to one offer or one idea, use one heavyweight sans-serif at the largest size you can fit, high contrast (black on white or yellow), and put the call-to-action below eye level since people scan downward when walking. The classic mistake is treating the A-board like a full menu; treat it like a billboard with five seconds of attention.

## The one thing to do this week

Pull up [PortlandMaps](https://www.portlandmaps.com), search 1022 SW Morrison, and confirm the base zone shows CX with no overlay weirdness (historic district could add wrinkles, though the sign code itself doesn't restrict A-boards on that basis). Then book the free 15-minute appointment to confirm the entrance count for your specific tenancy, and mail in the form. You can be legally up by next week.

col2.response:
I'll dig into this carefully. A-frame signs in Portland have specific rules, and downtown locations like yours have extra considerations on top of the citywide code. Let me check both the rules and what's specific to your address.Let me check the specific details for 1022 SW Morrison St — zoning and any plan/historic overlays matter for sign rules.Let me check overlays and the specific zone allowances for portable signs:Let me get the canonical Portland page to confirm fee details and the placement rules in the right-of-way:Short answer: **yes, you can.** Your property at 1022 SW Morrison is zoned CX (Central Commercial), which is one of the zones where permanent A-boards are allowed. But there's a crucial location-specific catch I want you to see before you order one.

## The catch worth knowing

The MAX Blue and Red Lines stop at **Galleria/SW 10th Ave**, which sits right at the corner of your block (eastbound side of Morrison at 10th). City rules prohibit A-boards "near transit stops" and "at street corners," so the east end of your frontage is effectively off-limits. You'll want to plan your placement toward the western end of the property frontage (toward 11th).

## The rules in plain terms

For an A-board on the public sidewalk in front of your building, you must:

- **Register it.** $114 for 1 year, $203 for 2 years, or **$364 for 4 years** (the 4-year is the best deal — works out to ~$91/yr vs $114/yr). Mail the form + check to Portland Permitting & Development, 1900 SW 4th Ave Ste 5000.
- **Stay within size limits.** On the sidewalk: max **8 sq ft per face, 48" tall, 30" wide**. (On private property the limit is 12 sq ft, but you're almost certainly placing it on the sidewalk.)
- **Place it within 6" of the curb**, leaving a **6-foot clear pedestrian path**. SW Morrison's sidewalks downtown are wide enough for this — the rule requires the sidewalk be at least 8 ft wide, which yours is.
- **One sign per public entrance.** If your business shares an entrance with another tenant, only one sign total is allowed for the entrance.
- **Bring it in at night / off-hours.** It can't be attached to the ground, trees, poles, parking meters, or street furniture, so it has to be moveable in/out.
- **No lights, no electrical, no digital/changing displays.**

Avoid placing it: at the corner, near the MAX stop, at ADA curb ramps, at building exits or fire escapes, on bike lanes, or in the parking lane.

## A few things you'd otherwise find out the hard way

1. **The property owner is MUCCA LLC.** If you're a tenant rather than the owner, the registration form requires property owner coordination, and the city explicitly notes "tenants and property owners must coordinate registration and display." Get the landlord's sign-off in writing before you submit.
2. **You don't need Design Review** for an A-board, even though downtown has design overlays. That's a meaningful time/cost savings — Design Review for other sign types can take weeks.
3. **Three citations and the sign gets impounded** (and your registration can be revoked, plus you can be barred from future registrations). The most common citation triggers are placement violations, so it's worth measuring the 6-foot pedestrian clearance with a tape the first time you set it out.
4. **Free 15-minute consultation.** PP&D offers a [15-minute appointment with a Residential & Signs Permitting Technician](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments#rs-signs) — worth booking before you submit if anything about your specific frontage is ambiguous (e.g., where exactly the transit-stop exclusion zone ends). Phone: 503-823-7300.
5. **Wind matters more than you'd think on Morrison.** The block gets channeled wind and the rules require the sign not be anchored to the ground. If you go cheap on the A-board itself and it blows over, that's a liability issue, not just an inconvenience. Sign companies sell weighted/wind-resistant designs with a swinging center panel — worth the upgrade for downtown placement.

The registration form is here: [Portable (A-Board) Sign Registration Form](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/portable-board-sign-registration-form/download), and the canonical rules page is [portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards).

Want me to mock up a placement diagram showing the allowed zone along your frontage relative to the MAX stop, so you can hand it to whoever sets the sign out each day?
