col3.response:
I'll dig into this carefully — your address is in downtown Portland, which has extra layers of rules (transit mall, possible historic district), so I want to verify the specifics before recommending anything. Let me check the property and the current city rules.Excellent — I now have a clear property picture. Let me look up the actual current rules and fees for A-board signs in Portland.Let me verify the code section directly and also check whether the transit mall (SW Morrison MAX corridor) has special rules.Let me also check Title 32's plan district / Central City overlays and historic provisions to make sure I'm not missing anything specific to this address.I have everything I need. Let me also verify there are no Clean & Safe District rules or other downtown-specific extras that might surprise you, since this address sits inside that business improvement district.I have everything I need. Here's a complete answer you can copy and paste; every rule, fee, and contact has its specific code section and full URL right next to the claim. After the text, I'll add a visual placement diagram for your block as a bonus.

---

## 1. Plain-English answer first

**Yes — you can legally put an A-board (a portable two-sided sandwich-board sign) on the sidewalk in front of 1022 SW Morrison St.** Your property is in the **Central Commercial (CX) zone** inside the **Central City plan district / West End sub-district**, and A-boards are explicitly allowed in CX zones. The building is a designated historic landmark (the **Arminius Hotel**, built 1904) and sits inside a Design Overlay zone, but A-boards are exempt from both Design Review and Historic Resource Review, so neither of those will hold you up.

You don't need a permit from the streets bureau. You only need to **register the sign** with **Portland Permitting & Development (PP&D, the city department that handles building, sign, and trade permits, formerly known as BDS — Bureau of Development Services)**. It is a one-page mail-in form with a check. **First-year cost is $114** (roughly the price of a nice dinner out for two), with longer terms cheaper per year. There is no insurance requirement and no zoning hearing.

The catch: there are strict placement rules (size, sidewalk clearance, no street corners, no transit stops, no attaching to anything) and your block has a **MAX light-rail stop at SW 10th & Morrison** that creates a real "no-sign zone" at the east end of your building's frontage. Read the gotchas section before you put anything out.

---

## 2. What to do next, in order

These are listed in the order I'd actually do them. Marked **(free)** where it costs nothing.

**Step 1. Verify the zoning and overlays for your address. (free, 5 minutes)**
Go to **https://www.portlandmaps.com/** and search "1022 SW Morrison St". Confirm what I found: base zone CX (Central Commercial), Central City plan district / West End, Design ("d") overlay, individually designated Historic Landmark (the Arminius Hotel), no historic district, no conservation district. Property ID is P652977 if you need it. Direct property page: https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/1022-SW-MORRISON-ST/P652977_did/

**Step 2. Read the official A-board page, top to bottom. (free, 10 minutes)**
The single most authoritative page is the city's portable signs page: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards. Everything below comes from it or from the underlying code section it points to (Portland City Code 32.32.030, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030).

**Step 3. Measure your sidewalk. (free, 5 minutes with a tape measure)**
Stand at your storefront and measure the sidewalk from the building wall to the curb edge. **The sidewalk must be at least 8 feet wide for an A-board to be allowed in the right-of-way at all** (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.i.(3) for temporary, and the official guidance "Not allowed on sidewalks less than 8 feet wide" at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards). Most of SW Morrison's downtown sidewalks are around 12 to 15 feet, so you should be fine, but check yours specifically.

**Step 4. If you're a tenant, get the property owner's written OK. (free, 1 day)**
City rule says "Tenants and property owners must coordinate registration and display" (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards). The county lists the owner as MUCCA LLC. Get their permission in writing (an email is fine) before you spend money on the form or the sign itself, because if there's any conflict the registration can be revoked.

**Step 5. Buy or build a compliant sign. ($50 to $250 depending on style, 1 to 7 days)**
Required dimensions for placement in the public sidewalk: **maximum 8 square feet per face, maximum 4 feet (48 inches) tall, maximum 2.5 feet (30 inches) wide** (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.b, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). No lights, no electrical components, no digital or changing displays (Portland City Code 32.32.030 G.4, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030).

**Step 6. (Optional but recommended) Book a free 15-minute virtual appointment with a Sign Permitting Technician. (free, 15 minutes, slot usually available within a week)**
This is the "show your work to a human before you mail it" step. Schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments#rs-signs. They'll confirm placement, exact location, and answer any oddities about your block (the MAX stop, the historic landmark, etc.). Free and worth doing.

**Step 7. Fill out and mail the registration form with a check. ($114 for 1 year, about a week to process by mail)**
Form (PDF): https://www.portland.gov/ppd/documents/portable-board-sign-registration-form/download.

Fees, valid July 1, 2025 through July 1, 2026 (current published fee schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules):
- 1 year: **$114**
- 2 years: **$203** (about $101 per year)
- 4 years: **$364** (about $91 per year)

Source for fees: https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards.

Mail to:
> Portland Permitting & Development
> Attn: A-Board Sign Permits
> 1900 SW 4th Ave, Ste 5000
> Portland, OR 97201

Make checks payable to **City of Portland** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards).

**Step 8. Place the sign correctly the first day you use it.**
Within **6 inches of the curb**, leaving at least **6 feet of clear pedestrian path**, only **adjacent to your own property**, **not at street corners**, **not at the MAX stop area**, and **not attached to anything** (poles, meters, trees, street furniture). Citations for each rule are in the "Common gotchas" section below.

**Step 9. Bring it in if rules require, and keep your registration paperwork on-site.**
Inspections are random or complaint-based (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards). Citations can stack: three citations on the same sign and the city can impound it and revoke your registration (Portland City Code 32.32.030 and the city's enforcement language at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards).

---

## 3. What to ask the city — script

Call **503-823-7300** Monday through Friday, 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. (Portland Permitting & Development general line, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards), or schedule a 15-minute appointment with a Residential & Signs Permitting Technician at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/early-assistance/15-minute-question-appointments#rs-signs.

Read or paste this:

> "Hi, my name is [your name] and I run a small business at 1022 SW Morrison Street, Portland, 97205. The Multnomah County property ID is P652977. I'd like to register a portable A-board sign for the sidewalk in front of the business. I have a few questions before I mail in the form.
>
> First, can you confirm my zoning is CX with the 'd' Design overlay, and that A-boards are allowed in the public right-of-way at this address?
>
> Second, the building is a designated Historic Landmark (the Arminius Hotel). I read that A-boards are exempt from Historic Resource Review under Code 32.34.020 C.1.a. Can you confirm that's correct?
>
> Third, my address is between SW 10th and SW 11th. There is a MAX light-rail platform right at SW 10th & Morrison. How far from that platform do I need to keep the sign? Where exactly does the 'no signs near transit stops' zone end?
>
> Fourth, I'd like to confirm the current fee, $114 for one year, is correct as of today.
>
> Fifth, is there anything specific to the Central City plan district, the West End sub-district, the Design overlay, or the Clean & Safe enhanced service district that I should know about before I mail the form?
>
> Sixth, once I mail the form and check, how long until I'm registered and can put the sign out?"

Those six questions cover almost every place a confused city worker could trip you up.

---

## 4. Common gotchas — the things that actually get small businesses cited

**Gotcha 1: Placing the sign too close to the MAX stop at SW 10th & Morrison.** A-board signs cannot block "pedestrian and wheelchair access from the sidewalk to transit stop areas" (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.g.(1), https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). The TriMet Galleria/SW 10th Ave station is on Morrison at the SW 10th corner, immediately east of your building. Place the sign on the western half of your frontage (toward SW 11th), not the eastern half.

**Gotcha 2: Putting it at the corner.** Signs are flat-out prohibited "at street corners" — specifically the area between the curb and the lines extending the property line to the curb face (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.c, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). Your storefront is mid-block so this is mostly fine, but if you have any tenants near the SW 10th or SW 11th corners, this matters.

**Gotcha 3: Blocking the ADA accessible path.** You must leave a **continuous through pedestrian zone of at least 6 feet** so wheelchairs and people with mobility devices can pass (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.f, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). And because you're inside the **Downtown High Pedestrian Traffic Area** (Portland City Code 14A.50.030 F.1, https://www.portland.gov/code/14/a50/030), the practical "pedestrian use zone" downtown is **8 feet on sidewalks more than 10 feet wide** between 7 a.m. and 9 p.m. (Portland City Code 14A.50.030 A.3, https://www.portland.gov/code/14/a50/030). Plan for 8 feet of clear space, not 6.

**Gotcha 4: Not within 6 inches of the curb.** The sign must be **within 6 inches of the curb** (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.e, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). Leaning it against the building wall is a violation, even though that's the instinctive thing to do.

**Gotcha 5: Attaching the sign to anything.** No chains to street trees, no zip-ties to parking meters, no bungee cords to light poles, no bolts to the sidewalk. The sign must "remain portable" (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.h, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). If the wind keeps blowing it over, the answer is a heavier base, not a tether.

**Gotcha 6: Lights or a digital screen.** Even battery-powered LED edge lighting makes the sign noncompliant. "Electrical signs and changing image sign features are prohibited" for portable signs (Portland City Code 32.32.030 G.4, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030).

**Gotcha 7: Going bigger than 8 square feet in the right-of-way.** The "12 square feet" allowance you may see online is only for portable signs **kept entirely on private property** (Portland City Code 32.32.030 G.3, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). On the sidewalk it's **8 square feet maximum per face, 2.5 feet wide, 4 feet tall** (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.b, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). Adjustments and modifications to these limits are explicitly prohibited (Portland City Code 32.32.030 G.1, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030); no variance, no appeal, no "but my designer made it 9 square feet."

**Gotcha 8: Putting one out without registering.** Inspections are random or complaint-based, but downtown — especially in the Clean & Safe district where ambassadors walk the same blocks daily — complaints are realistic. Citations can lead to impoundment and revocation if you collect three on the same sign (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards).

**Gotcha 9: Forgetting that A-boards in the right-of-way must be next to your own property.** "Signs may only be placed adjacent to the property where they are registered. They may not be placed across the street or on neighboring property" (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards). If the sidewalk in front of your suite is awkward, you can't move the sign to the better-flowing sidewalk across the street.

**Gotcha 10: One sign per public entrance.** If your space shares an entrance with another tenant, you and the neighbor get **one sign total**, not one each (Portland City Code 32.32.030 G.2.a, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030). Coordinate before you both mail forms.

---

## 5. Friendly accuracy caveats

These are not legal disclaimers; these are "ask before you spend money" warnings.

The MAX platform location and the exact "no-sign zone" radius around it are not spelled out as a number in the code. Confirm the boundary with PP&D using the script above before you set the sign down for the first time. **I could not find a specific code section setting the radius around a transit stop; the rule (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.g.(1), https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030) says the sign must not "obstruct" access, which is judgment-based.** PP&D or PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation, the city department in charge of streets, sidewalks, and the right-of-way) can tell you in concrete feet on the phone.

Sidewalk width on SW Morrison between 10th and 11th is most likely well over the 8-foot minimum, but I did not measure it. Measure yours before you commit; the rule (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards) is hard.

The Clean & Safe District is a business improvement district that pays for extra cleaning and ambassadors in your 273-block area (https://downtownportland.org/). They do not write sign rules; the city does. But they are the eyes most likely to flag a noncompliant sign to PP&D, so a clean placement matters more here than in a quieter part of town.

The fees I quoted ($114 / $203 / $364) are the current published rates, valid July 1, 2025 to July 1, 2026 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits/portable-signs-boards). Portland updates its fee schedule every July 1; if you're reading this after that date, double-check the current schedule at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/current-fee-schedules.

I did not find any insurance or liability bond requirement for portable A-board signs anywhere in the sign code or on the PP&D page. Some cities require an encroachment-permit insurance rider for anything in the right-of-way; **Portland does not for A-boards specifically**, because the city wraps A-board placement into Title 32 (the sign code) rather than Title 17 (public improvements / right-of-way). That said, if you call PP&D, ask them to confirm — better to be told no than guess yes.

---

## 6. Glossary

**A-board / sandwich board / portable sign / sidewalk sign:** A movable two-sided fold-out sign, hinged at the top, that stands on the sidewalk like an "A" when open. Portland's official term is "portable sign."

**ADA:** Americans with Disabilities Act, the federal law that requires sidewalks to have a clear, navigable path for people using wheelchairs, walkers, canes, or guide animals.

**BDS (Bureau of Development Services):** The old name for the city department that handled building, sign, and development permits. Now called **PP&D**.

**Central City:** The city's plan district covering downtown Portland and the central east side. Your address is in this plan district, in the "West End" sub-district.

**Clean & Safe District:** A 273-block downtown business improvement district that funds extra street cleaning, ambassadors, and security in the area. It does not write sign rules but it does report violations.

**CX (Central Commercial):** Your base zoning. The most permissive commercial zone for signs.

**Design Overlay (the "d" zone):** A zoning layer that normally requires a Design Review for new signs and buildings. Portable signs are explicitly **exempt** (Portland City Code 32.34.020 B.1.a, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/34).

**Encroachment:** The legal term for putting something private into public space (the sidewalk, the parking strip). A-boards on the sidewalk are technically encroachments but are pre-authorized by Title 32 if they meet the rules.

**Historic Landmark:** A specific designated property that the city has identified for historic preservation. Your building (the Arminius Hotel) is one. Most sign and exterior changes need Historic Resource Review, but **portable signs are exempt** (Portland City Code 32.34.020 C.1.a, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/34).

**MAX:** Metropolitan Area Express, TriMet's light-rail system. The Blue and Red lines run westbound on SW Morrison; the Galleria/SW 10th Ave platform sits at SW 10th & Morrison, immediately east of your building.

**PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation):** The city department in charge of streets, sidewalks, and the right-of-way. For most things in the public sidewalk, you'd talk to PBOT — but for A-boards specifically, you don't, because A-boards are governed by the sign code under PP&D.

**PCC:** Portland City Code. So "PCC 32.32.030" means Title 32, Chapter 32, Section 030 of the Portland City Code.

**PP&D (Portland Permitting & Development):** The bureau that handles building, sign, trade, and most permit work; the renamed Bureau of Development Services. Your A-board registration goes here. 503-823-7300, 1900 SW 4th Ave Ste 5000, Portland OR 97201.

**Right-of-way (ROW):** The public-controlled strip that includes the street, the parking strip, the curb, and the sidewalk. The city owns it; abutting property owners have to maintain the sidewalk portion (Portland City Code 17.28, https://www.portland.gov/code/17/28).

**Title 17:** The chapter of city code covering public improvements (streets, sidewalks, ROW). Generally relevant for sidewalk repairs, café seating, sidewalk encroachment permits — but **not** for A-boards, which are pulled out and handled under Title 32.

**Title 32 (the Sign Code):** The chapter that governs every sign in Portland, including A-boards. Your specific section is 32.32.030, subsections C and G.

**Transit mall:** Strictly speaking, this is the SW 5th Avenue / SW 6th Avenue bus and MAX corridor, not your block. SW Morrison/Yamhill is the original east-west MAX corridor, which has stops but is not the "transit mall" proper. Either way, the relevant rule is "no signs blocking access to transit stops."

**West End:** The sub-district inside the Central City plan district that covers your address.

---

Now for the surprise — let me give you a visual you can keep next to your sign so the rules are obvious at a glance.The visualizer didn't respond, so let me give you something more practical instead — a one-page printable markdown cheat sheet you can pin behind the counter or fold into the sign itself. I'll save it to a file so you can download it.A few last things worth flagging that came out of the research and that a knowledgeable friend would mention:

**The MAX stop is the single biggest landmine on your block.** The Galleria/SW 10th Ave westbound MAX platform sits on Morrison at the SW 10th corner, immediately east of your storefront. The "no signs blocking transit access" rule (Portland City Code 32.32.030 C.3.g, https://www.portland.gov/code/32/32/030) is judgment-based, not a measured radius, so when you call PP&D ask them to put the answer in concrete feet. Place the sign on the western half of your frontage (toward SW 11th) to be safe.

**The fact that your building is a Historic Landmark is unusual but doesn't trigger anything extra here.** It's worth mentioning on the call so the city employee doesn't get confused and route you toward Historic Resource Review (which would not apply); the A-board exemption from historic review is in Portland City Code 32.34.020 C.1.a (https://www.portland.gov/code/32/34).

**You don't need a PBOT (right-of-way) permit at all.** This is the most common point of confusion. People assume any sign on the sidewalk goes through PBOT, but Portland intentionally pulls A-boards out of the right-of-way permit system and handles them through Title 32 / PP&D instead (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/sign-permits — see the line "For signs in the street right-of-way (except A-boards) and signs on utility poles, contact Transportation"). One bureau, one form, one fee.

**Title 17 (Public Improvements) does apply to your sidewalk in one indirect way:** Portland City Code 17.28 (https://www.portland.gov/code/17/28) makes the property owner responsible for keeping the sidewalk in good repair and creates liability for injuries from a defective sidewalk. If your A-board causes someone to fall because the sign was misplaced, the property owner (MUCCA LLC) and you both face exposure. That's the reason for the 6-foot clearance rule and the reason to take placement seriously, even though no insurance bond is required by the city.

If anything I said conflicts with what PP&D tells you on the phone, **trust PP&D** — they read the code every day and I'm reading it from outside.

a-board-sign.enrichedQuestion:
I want to know whether I can put an A-board sign (a portable, two-sided sandwich-board sign) on the sidewalk or right outside my business at 1022 SW Morrison St, Portland, OR 97205, and what I need to do to keep it legal. I run a small business and I have no background in city code, zoning, or permitting. Please answer me as a regular small-business owner who just wants to know: am I allowed to do this, what permission do I need, what does it cost, and how do I avoid getting fined or having the sign confiscated.

Please write your entire answer for a Portland resident or small-business owner with no code or permitting background. Aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level. Treat me as smart but completely unfamiliar with city processes. Lead with a plain-English summary that answers my question directly in 3–5 sentences before any tables, citations, or code language. Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it — for example, A-board, sandwich board, ROW (right-of-way, meaning the public sidewalk and street area the city controls), PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation, which manages streets and sidewalks), BDS (Bureau of Development Services, which handles building and sign permits), PP&D (Permitting & Development), encroachment, sign code, central city, historic district, etc. If you would not use a term in conversation with a neighbor over the fence, define it. Use everyday words instead of code language wherever possible — say "the rules for signs you put on the sidewalk" before saying "sidewalk sign / A-frame regulations under Title 17"; say "the city department in charge of streets and sidewalks" before saying "PBOT". Keep paragraphs short. Use bolded keywords inside sentences instead of dense tables. Use a table only if you are comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions, and put a one-sentence plain-English summary above any table. Translate any dollar figures and timelines into something I can plan around (e.g., "about the cost of a nice dinner out", "roughly two weeks — about the time to get a passport renewed").

Keep my core question intact (can I put an A-board out front, and what do I have to do?), but please also surface the things a knowledgeable friend or business neighbor would flag that I probably didn't think to ask, including:

- Is 1022 SW Morrison St in the **Central City / downtown** zone, and does that change the sign rules? Are there extra rules because it's downtown or near a transit mall (the SW Morrison/Yamhill MAX light-rail and bus corridor)?
- Is the property in a **historic district or design overlay zone** (such as a downtown historic conservation district or design review area), and would that require extra approval before I put a sign out?
- What **Title(s) of Portland City Code** govern A-boards specifically? I've heard Title 32 covers signs and Title 17 covers sidewalks/right-of-way — please tell me which ones actually apply to a sandwich board on the sidewalk in front of my business and cite the specific sections.
- Do I need a **sidewalk A-frame sign permit** (or whatever PBOT calls it), a **sign permit from BDS**, both, or neither? What triggers each one?
- What are the **size, placement, and clearance rules** (how tall, how wide, how far from the curb, how much clear sidewalk space must remain for wheelchairs, when it can be out, whether it has to come in at night)?
- Are there **insurance or liability requirements** (some cities require a small insurance rider before letting you put anything in the public right-of-way)?
- What does the **permit cost**, including any annual renewal, and how long does approval typically take?
- Who exactly do I **contact** — which bureau, which phone number or email, which website — and what's the order I should do things in?
- What **common mistakes or gotchas** trip up Portland small-business owners on A-board signs (e.g., placing too close to the curb, blocking ADA-required clear path, putting one out without a permit, putting one out in the transit mall area where they may be banned, double-sided vs. single-sided rules, lighting/electrical rules, attaching anything to street furniture or trees)?
- What happens if I just put one out without a permit — is it a warning, a fine, sign confiscation, or something more serious?

Please structure your answer this way:

1. **A plain-English answer first.** First paragraph: answer my actual question in everyday language with no citations or jargon. Yes/no/it depends, and what the next move is.

2. **A numbered "what to do next" list, in the exact order I should do it.** For each step, name who to call or which website to visit, what to bring, what it costs (in plain dollars), and roughly how long it takes. Mark which steps are **free**. Steps should include things like: verify the zoning and overlays for 1022 SW Morrison on PortlandMaps; call the right bureau to confirm A-boards are allowed at this address; apply for the right permit; measure the sidewalk to confirm clear path; etc.

3. **A "what to ask the city" script** I can read aloud on a phone call or paste into an email. Not just "call PBOT" — give me the actual sentences to say, including my address, what I want to do, and the exact questions to ask so the city employee gives me a useful answer on the first try.

4. **Full inline citations for every factual claim, written out in the body of the answer itself — not just a "Sources" list at the end, and never as vague references like "Portland City Code" or "the city website" with no specifics.** Every rule, number, fee, timeline, size limit, clearance distance, or contact must be followed in the same sentence by:
   - the exact code section (e.g., `PCC 32.32.030`, not just "Title 32"), AND
   - a direct, full working URL to the authoritative source (portland.gov, the specific Title chapter on portland.gov/code, the specific PBOT Administrative Rule or sidewalk sign permit page, the PortlandMaps page for my address, etc.) — full `https://...` URLs, not link text like "[here]" or "the city's website".

**The first thing to check is whether it's actually your tree.** In Portland, the city's right-of-way often extends past the sidewalk and a foot or two into what looks like your front yard. Any tree inside that zone (or even just touching the line) is legally a *street tree*, not a private tree, and street trees follow stricter rules.To find your actual property line, pull up your address on [portlandmaps.com](https://www.portlandmaps.com); the property outline is shown to scale and you can see exactly where the right-of-way sits. ## The decision flow

**If it's a street tree** (in or touching the ROW): permit always required, regardless of size, except for self-sown shoots under ½ inch. The bar for approval is high; usually only dead, dying, dangerous, or sidewalk-damaging trees are approved, and a replacement is required.

**If it's a private tree:**
- Under 12 inches diameter at 4.5 feet up (DBH): no permit needed, you can just remove it.
- 12 inches or larger: permit required, with a replanting condition.

A permit will be granted for a regulated private tree if at least one of these applies:
- It's dead, dying, or dangerous (arborist report helps).
- It's within 10 feet of a building or attached structure.
- It's a [nuisance species](https://www.portland.gov/bes/portland-plant-list) (English holly, English hawthorn, tree of heaven, etc.).
- It's healthy and ordinary, but you get a quota: up to **4 healthy trees under 20 inches** per site per calendar year (and not Heritage Trees, and not trees required by a prior permit). A single large healthy oak well clear of the house may not be approvable.

**Heritage Trees** are a separate category; if your tree has a small bronze plaque or sign on the trunk, stop and call Urban Forestry first. Any work on a Heritage Tree needs a permit even if it's just a prune.

## Process and cost

Permits are **free** as of July 1, 2025 (funded through the Portland Clean Energy Community Benefits Fund); apply through the city's DevHub portal at [portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits](https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits). You'll need a free City of Portland account. An inspector will visit; you mark the tree with an orange ribbon. Turnaround is generally a couple of weeks.

The actual removal job is what costs money: typically **$400–$2,000+** depending on size, access, and whether stump grinding is included. Get 2–3 written quotes, and verify the contractor is a licensed and insured arborist; ideally [ISA-certified](https://www.treesaregood.org/findanarborist).

## Things you might not have known

- **Free replacement trees.** PP&R Urban Forestry's [Find Your Free Trees](https://www.portland.gov/trees/get-involved/find-your-free-trees) program (and Friends of Trees) can supply your replanting tree at no cost. The replacement doesn't have to go in the front; you can plant it side or back yard, or even pay $50 (reduced from $100) for a replanting waiver if you genuinely have nowhere to put it.
- **Best planting window is October through April.** If you're removing in the warm months, the city expects you to replant in the next dormant season.
- **Emergency removal is allowed** for genuinely dangerous trees (cracking, uprooting, leaning on a structure), but you must file a retroactive permit within **7 days** with proof (arborist report or photos). Skipping this if it wasn't a real emergency turns into a code violation.
- **Watch for the "we don't need a permit" pitch.** A small number of tree services will quietly tell homeowners they can skip the permit; the homeowner is the one who eats the violation, which can run into thousands of dollars per tree, plus mandatory replanting at full caliper. If a contractor is dismissive about permits, that's a red flag, not a time-saver.
- **Neighbor trees and shared trees:** if the tree straddles a property line with your neighbor, it's a shared tree and you need their written agreement before any work.

## Quickest path forward

1. Check the property line on portlandmaps.com to confirm street tree vs private.
2. Measure trunk diameter at 4.5 feet up.
3. If it's clearly a private tree under 12 inches, you're free to remove it — no permit, no replanting.

# Tree removal at 3413 NE 33rd Ave, Portland, OR 97212: a homeowner's walkthrough

## Plain-English summary (read this first)

**Yes, you can almost certainly remove a tree at this address, but exactly what you need to do depends on which tree you mean.** If the tree sits in the planting strip between the sidewalk and the curb, it is a **street tree**, and you need a permit before any work, no matter how small. If the tree is fully on your private property and is **less than 12 inches thick** (measured 4.5 feet up the trunk), you do not need a permit. If a private tree is **12 inches thick or larger**, you need a permit, and since **July 1, 2025**, the permit itself is **free**; you will be asked to plant a replacement tree on your property. Good news: I checked your address on PortlandMaps, and your lot has **no historic, environmental, or heritage-tree complications**, so the rules apply in their normal form.

---

## What I found about your specific property

I looked your address up on PortlandMaps (https://www.portlandmaps.com/) so the rest of this answer is grounded in what's actually true for your lot, not generic Portland advice.

- **Address:** 3413 NE 33rd Ave, Portland, OR 97212 (Property ID R288441, neighborhood **Alameda**, https://www.portlandmaps.com/detail/property/3413-NE-33RD-AVE/R288441/)
- **Base zoning:** **R5** (Residential 5,000), which is standard single-dwelling residential and does not add tree-specific rules (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/base/R5).
- **Overlay zone:** Just one, the "**h" Aircraft Landing overlay**, which restricts how tall *buildings* can be near PDX flight paths and does **not** add tree-removal restrictions (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/overlay/h).
- **No historic district, no conservation district, no environmental overlay, no plan district** flagged for your lot in the city's data.
- **No heritage trees** registered on your property or on the lots immediately next door, based on PortlandMaps' heritage-tree layer.

What this means in plain English: your address is one of the **simpler cases** in Portland for tree removal. There are no special overlays that pull a smaller tree into the permit system, and no protected trees on the property that would require extra hoops.

> **Friendly caveat:** PortlandMaps is the city's own data, but it can lag behind real life by a few weeks in rare cases. Before you cut anything or pay anyone, **plug your address in yourself** at https://www.portlandmaps.com/ and confirm the same overlays I listed, and call **Urban Forestry at 503-823-8733** to confirm out loud. Belt and suspenders.

---

## 1. Whose tree is it? (this is the most important question)

Portland sorts trees into three buckets, and the bureau in charge changes for each one. Before you do anything else, figure out which bucket your tree is in.

**Street tree (in the right-of-way along the curb).** A "street tree" is any tree growing in the city's strip of land along the street; this strip is called the **right-of-way (ROW)**, and the planting strip between the sidewalk and the curb is part of it (PCC 11.80, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/80). On NE 33rd Ave, this is usually the planting strip with grass between the curb and sidewalk. Even though *you* mow it, it is the city's land, and the tree is a street tree managed by **Urban Forestry** (Portland Parks & Recreation, the city's tree office). Any tree that *straddles* the property line and the right-of-way also counts as a street tree (Tree Removal FAQ, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/tree-removal-and-replanting-faqs).

**Private tree (fully on your lot).** A tree completely inside your property line, including in the front yard but inside the sidewalk, is a **private tree** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit). Private trees follow Chapter 11.40 of the tree code (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40).

**Heritage tree (a specially designated tree).** Heritage trees are individual trees the city has formally listed for their size, age, or historic value, and **any** work on them needs a permit, even pruning (https://www.portland.gov/trees/heritage-trees-portland). Your lot is **not** flagged for one, based on PortlandMaps' heritage-tree layer for your address.

**How to tell which one yours is, in 60 seconds:**

1. Open https://www.portlandmaps.com/ and search **3413 NE 33rd Ave**.
2. Zoom in on your lot. Your **property line** is the inner edge of the sidewalk on the NE 33rd side.
3. Anything between the sidewalk and the street curb is the **public right-of-way** (street tree).
4. Anything inside the sidewalk on the lot is **private** (your tree).
5. If the tree's trunk straddles that line, treat it as a **street tree** to be safe (Tree Removal FAQ, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/tree-removal-and-replanting-faqs).

If you are still unsure, leave a voicemail at **503-823-8733** or email **trees@portlandoregon.gov**, and the city's tree office (Urban Forestry) will tell you for free which kind it is (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/contact-urban-forestry).

---

## 2. Which Portland code chapters apply

Portland's tree rules live in **Title 11 (Trees)** of the **Portland City Code (PCC)**. The chapters that matter for a homeowner removing a tree (no construction project on the property) are:

- **Chapter 11.05 (Legal Frameworks and Relationships)** explains what trees the code covers and how it relates to other laws (PCC 11.05, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/05).
- **Chapter 11.40 (Tree Permit Requirements – No Associated Development)** is the main one for you; this is where the permit rules for removing a tree without a construction project live (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40).
- **Chapter 11.60 (Technical Specifications)** covers replacement-tree size, planting standards, and how to actually remove the tree (PCC 11.60, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/60).
- **Chapter 11.70 (Enforcement)** lays out the penalties if you cut without a permit (PCC 11.70, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/70). Cutting first and asking later is in here, and the fines are not small (more on this below).
- **Chapter 11.80 (Definitions and Measurements)** is where terms like "street tree," "private tree," and how to measure thickness are defined (PCC 11.80, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/80).

You can also click "View all chapters" at https://www.portland.gov/code/11/all to see the whole title in one place.

**Title 33 (Planning and Zoning)** only kicks in if you're doing a construction project on the lot at the same time (PCC 33, https://www.portland.gov/code/33). You said you just want to remove a tree, so Title 33 is unlikely to apply unless you're also adding a deck, ADU, etc.

---

## 3. The permit thresholds, in plain English

This is the meat of the answer. Here are the four cases:

**Case A: Small private tree, under 12 inches around (measured 4.5 feet up the trunk).** **No permit, no fee.** A removal permit is required for trees on private property only when the tree is **12 inches in diameter or larger**, measured 4.5 feet above the ground; this measurement is called the **diameter at breast height (DBH)** (Tree Removal and Replanting Permits, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits). To check if your tree is over the threshold, wrap a tape measure around the trunk at 4.5 feet up: if the *circumference* is about **38 inches or more**, the diameter is 12 inches or more. (Circumference divided by ~3.14 equals diameter; the city has a how-to with photos at https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/how-measure-tree.)

**Case B: Private tree, 12 inches thick or larger.** **Permit required; fee is $0.** A non-development tree removal permit is required, but as of **July 1, 2025**, the **Tree Removal and Replanting permit fee was eliminated** thanks to funding from the **Portland Clean Energy Community Benefits Fund (PCEF)** (Do I Need a Tree Permit, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit). You will need to **plant a replacement tree** as a condition of the permit (PCC 11.40 + 11.60, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40). You apply through the city's online portal **DevHub** (the Development Hub) at https://www.portland.gov/permitting/development-hub.

**Case C: Street tree (in the planting strip), any size.** **Permit always required; fee is $0.** A permit is required to remove **any** street tree, except for "sucker shoots" or self-sown volunteer trees less than half an inch thick (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40 and https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit). Street tree removal permits are also free as of July 1, 2025 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit).

**Case D: Heritage tree, any size.** **Permit required and harder to get.** All heritage tree work, including pruning, requires a permit, and the city is much more protective (https://www.portland.gov/trees/heritage-trees-portland). Your address is **not** on the heritage tree list based on PortlandMaps, so this is unlikely to apply.

**What about getting a permit approved?** For private trees, the city will generally approve a removal if at least one of these is true: the tree is **dead, dying, or dangerous**; the tree is **within 10 feet of a building**; the tree is a **nuisance species**; or you remove **fewer than 4 healthy trees smaller than 20 inches** per site per calendar year (PCC 11.40 + Qualifying for a Permit, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit). For street trees, the standard is mostly **dead, dying, or dangerous** with replacement (same source).

**Special rule for very large trees (36 inches and up).** If the private tree's diameter is **36 inches or greater**, you must give written notice to the neighborhood association before the permit decision (Tree Removal Forms list, https://www.portland.gov/trees/trees-forms-and-application-list).

**Species-specific rule worth knowing about.** Oregon has a state-wide quarantine on **elm trees** because of Dutch elm disease; if your tree is an elm, the wood must be chipped or de-barked and buried, not left in chunks (PCC 11.60 + Qualifying page, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit).

---

## 4. The exact bureau and contact info

The city's tree office is called **Urban Forestry**, and it sits inside **Portland Parks & Recreation (PP&R)**, not inside the building permits bureau. (The building permits bureau is a separate group called **Permitting & Development (PP&D)**, formerly the Bureau of Development Services or **BDS**; you only deal with PP&D if you're doing construction at the same time.)

**Urban Forestry contact info, verified today:**

- **Phone:** **503-823-8733** (also written as 503-823-TREE). The hotline is **not answered live**; you leave a voicemail and they return calls within about 3 business days, currently extended due to volume (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/local-tree-care-providers).
- **Email:** **trees@portlandoregon.gov** (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/contact-urban-forestry).
- **Phone menu:** Press **1** for tree emergencies (24/7), **3** for general inquiries (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit).
- **Office address (in-person, by appointment only):** **Development Services Center, 1900 SW 4th Ave, Suite 5000, Portland, OR 97201** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits).
- **Where to apply online:** the **DevHub** portal at https://www.portland.gov/permitting/development-hub (you'll need a free Portland account; the application form is also downloadable at https://www.portland.gov/trees/trees-forms-and-application-list).

**If you need building permits at the same time** (say, you're removing the tree to make room for an addition), then **Permitting & Development (PP&D)**, the building permits bureau, also gets involved, and the tree review is folded into the construction permit (PCC 11.50, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50). For a simple stand-alone tree removal, you only deal with Urban Forestry.

---

## 5. What to do next, in order

Here is the path I'd take if this were my house, ordered so you don't do work twice.

1. **(Free) Confirm whose tree it is on PortlandMaps.** Go to https://www.portlandmaps.com/, search **3413 NE 33rd Ave**, and look at where the tree's trunk sits relative to the sidewalk. If the trunk is between the curb and the sidewalk, it's a **street tree**. If it's behind the sidewalk on the lot, it's **private**. (~5 minutes.)

2. **(Free) Measure the tree.** Wrap a tape measure around the trunk at **4.5 feet above the ground**. If the circumference is **less than about 38 inches** (and the tree is fully private, not in the planting strip), you can probably remove it without a permit at all (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40). If it's larger, or if it's a street tree, go to step 3. (~5 minutes; instructions at https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/how-measure-tree.)

3. **(Free) Take a few photos** of the tree (whole tree, the trunk, any decay or damage, distance from the house if it's within 10 feet of a structure). This is what an Urban Forestry inspector will want to see, and it helps your "dead, dying, dangerous, or within 10 feet of a building" case (Qualifying page, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit). (~10 minutes.)

4. **(Free) Email Urban Forestry first** at **trees@portlandoregon.gov** with the script in the next section, before you call. Email creates a paper trail. If you'd rather talk, leave a voicemail at **503-823-8733** and press **3** (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/contact-urban-forestry). Expect a response in about **3 business days**, possibly longer right now (same source). (~5 minutes; about a long weekend to hear back.)

5. **(Free) Apply for the permit on DevHub.** Once Urban Forestry confirms which permit type you need, you submit the **Tree Removal and Replanting Permit** application via DevHub at https://www.portland.gov/permitting/development-hub (or download the PDF at https://www.portland.gov/trees/trees-forms-and-application-list). Have ready: your address, the tree's diameter at 4.5 feet, photos, and your reason for removal. **Permit fee: $0** as of July 1, 2025 (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit). A site plan is **optional** but speeds things up; without one, an inspector will visit (https://www.portland.gov/trees/treepermits/news/2025/1/4/new-tree-removal-replanting-permit-application). (~30 minutes to fill out; **roughly 1-3 weeks** for the city to issue, my estimate based on their current 3-business-day response window plus inspection time. Verify timing with the office.)

6. **(Free) Call 811 before you cut.** Oregon's utility-locate hotline is **811** (or 503-246-6699). They mark underground utilities for free so you don't slice into your sewer lateral when grinding the stump (Qualifying page, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit). Two business days' notice required. (5-minute call.)

7. **Hire a licensed tree service** (not free; see section below for what to ask). Get **at least three quotes**. Cost varies wildly by tree size; a typical mid-size tree (say 20-30 inches thick) often runs **$1,200-$3,000** in Portland; a very large tree near a house can be **$5,000+**. (Range is from current Portland tree-service market data; verify with quotes.)

8. **Have the tree removed within the permit's 365-day window.** Removal/replant permits are valid for **365 days**, and **for street trees** the stump must be ground below grade and debris cleared while the permit is valid (PCC 11.40 + Qualifying page, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit). Roughly: do the cutting any time in the year after the permit issues.

9. **(Free or low-cost) Plant your replacement tree.** Required for permitted private removals. The city has a **Free Trees** program that may give you a qualifying replacement tree at no cost; check https://www.portland.gov/trees/find-your-free-trees. Replacement standards (size, species, planting method) are in PCC 11.60 (https://www.portland.gov/code/11/60), with species lists at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/private-property-tree-replacem. (Roughly **a weekend** of effort to plant a young tree.)

10. **(Free) Self-certify or request final inspection.** Before your permit expires, either submit a **self-certification form** with photos of the planted replacement, or ask Urban Forestry to come do a final inspection (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/tree-removal-and-replanting-faqs). Done.

---

## 6. What to say to Urban Forestry: copy-paste script

**Email version** (paste into your email body, edit the bracketed parts):

> Subject: Tree removal question, 3413 NE 33rd Ave (97212)
>
> Hi Urban Forestry team,
>
> I'm a homeowner at **3413 NE 33rd Ave, Portland, OR 97212** (Property ID R288441, Alameda neighborhood), and I'd like to remove a tree in my front yard. Before I apply, I want to make sure I'm doing this the right way.
>
> Here's what I know so far:
>
> - **Location of the tree:** [for example: "in the planting strip between the sidewalk and the curb on NE 33rd Ave" OR "fully on my private property, about 8 feet behind the sidewalk"].
> - **Approximate diameter at 4.5 feet up the trunk:** [for example: "about 14 inches"].
> - **Species (if known):** [for example: "I think it's a maple, but I'm not sure"].
> - **Reason I want it removed:** [for example: "the trunk has split and the upper third is dead" OR "it's within 6 feet of my foundation and the roots are lifting the sidewalk"].
> - **Photos attached:** [attach 2-4 photos: whole tree, base, any damage, distance to structure].
>
> Could you confirm:
>
> 1. Is this a street tree or a private tree based on its location?
> 2. Do I need a Tree Removal and Replanting Permit, given the size and reason?
> 3. Are there any overlay zones, plan districts, or heritage-tree designations on my parcel I should know about before I apply? My PortlandMaps lookup shows R5 base zoning with the "h" Aircraft Landing overlay only, and no historic, environmental, or heritage flags.
> 4. What's the typical timeline right now to get a permit issued?
>
> Thanks very much for your help.
>
> [Your name]
> [Your phone number]

**Phone version** (read aloud after pressing 3 on the menu at 503-823-8733):

> Hi, my name is [your name]. I'm calling from **3413 NE 33rd Ave in the Alameda neighborhood, zip 97212**. I'd like to remove a tree from my front yard and I want to make sure I do it correctly. The tree is **[in the planting strip / fully on my property]**, the diameter at 4.5 feet is about **[X] inches**, and the reason I want to remove it is **[dead / dying / hazardous / within 10 feet of my house / nuisance species]**. Could you tell me whether I need a permit for this, what the typical timeline looks like right now, and anything specific to my parcel I should know about? Please call me back at **[your number]** or email me at **[your email]**. Thank you.

---

## 7. Replacement and mitigation: what you'll likely have to plant

If your removal needs a permit (Cases B, C, or D above), the city will require a **replacement tree** as a condition of approval. The rules are in PCC 11.60 (https://www.portland.gov/code/11/60).

**For a private tree replacement,** the city expects you to plant a tree from the **Private Property Tree Replacement List** (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/private-property-tree-replacem). The minimum size is typically a **2-inch caliper** tree (about as thick as a thumb at the base), or a smaller bare-root tree of an approved species.

**If you genuinely don't have room** for a replacement on your private property, you can pay into the **Tree Planting and Preservation Fund** instead, which goes toward planting trees elsewhere in Portland (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40 and PCC 11.50.040, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/50). The mitigation amount is calculated using the per-inch fee in the city's adopted fee schedule (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit/tree-permitting-fee-schedule). For a typical homeowner in your situation, **planting your own replacement is much cheaper than the in-lieu fee**, and **PP&R Urban Forestry may give you the replacement tree for free** through the Free Trees program at https://www.portland.gov/trees/find-your-free-trees.

**There's also a Replanting Waiver** for situations where the property already has enough other trees to count toward the city's tree density rules; the application fee is $50 (reduced from $100 as of July 1, 2025) and is filed with your removal permit (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit and https://www.portland.gov/trees/trees-forms-and-application-list).

> **Realistic numbers for your situation, Alameda lot, R5 zoning:** Most homeowners pay **$0 for the permit** plus **$0-$250 for a replacement tree** (free if you get one through PP&R), plus the cost of the actual removal work (usually $1,200-$3,000+). The most expensive part of this whole project is the tree service, not the city.

---

## 8. Common Portland tree-removal traps

These are the specific mistakes that bite homeowners doing exactly your kind of project:

**"It's in my front yard, so it must be mine."** Trees in the planting strip between the curb and sidewalk are **street trees**, owned/regulated by the city even though *you* mow that strip; cutting one without a permit is a Title 11 violation (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/tree-removal-and-replanting-faqs). Always check the property line on PortlandMaps first.

**"It's just a small tree, I'll cut first and ask later."** Conducting regulated tree work without a permit is unlawful under PCC 11.70.050.B (https://www.portland.gov/code/11/70/050), and Portland's enforcement is real; civil penalties for unpermitted removal can run **up to $1,000 per day, per tree**, plus required restoration (PCC 11.70, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/70). Get the permit even when it costs nothing.

**Hiring an unlicensed tree service.** Oregon law requires tree-work companies to be registered with the **Construction Contractors Board (CCB)** or the **Landscape Contractors Board (LCB)**, and a Portland business license is required for any company operating in the city (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/tips-hiring-arborist). Unlicensed crews can leave you on the hook for injuries on your property. Verify CCB at https://search.ccb.state.or.us/search/ and LCB at https://www.oregon.gov/lcb/ before you sign anything.

**Removing a tree during nesting season without checking for nests.** Tree work is also subject to the federal **Migratory Bird Treaty Act**, which Portland's tree code explicitly references (PCC 11.05, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/05). Active nests with eggs or chicks (most common **April through July**) make removal illegal under federal law until the nest is empty. A good arborist will check before cutting.

**Missing a heritage tree designation.** Some Portland trees are designated heritage trees and require permits even for pruning (https://www.portland.gov/trees/heritage-trees-portland). I checked your address: **no heritage trees** are flagged on or immediately next to your lot, but always confirm on PortlandMaps before cutting.

**Assuming an "h" overlay or other overlay restricts trees.** Your lot's only overlay is the **"h" Aircraft Landing overlay**, which restricts building heights near PDX flight paths but does **not** add tree-removal restrictions (https://www.portlandmaps.com/bps/zoning/#/zones/overlay/h). The overlays that *do* add tree restrictions are the environmental overlays ("c" and "p") and certain plan districts; you have **none** of those.

**Not calling 811 before stump grinding.** If you grind a stump without locating utilities, you can hit a sewer lateral or gas line. **Call 811** at least two business days before, free (Qualifying page, https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit).

**Forgetting the 36-inch neighborhood-notice rule.** If your tree is **36 inches or larger** in diameter, you must send a notice to the neighborhood association (Alameda Neighborhood Association, in your case) and post a sign on the property before the permit decision (https://www.portland.gov/trees/trees-forms-and-application-list).

**Letting your permit expire.** The permit is good for **365 days**, and the replanting must be done before it expires or it's a code violation (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/removal-and-replanting-permits/qualifying-tree-removal-and-replanting-permit).

---

## 9. Hiring a tree service: what to check, what to ask

Portland does **not** have its own tree-service license, but Oregon does, and Portland requires any business operating in the city to hold a city business license (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/tips-hiring-arborist).

Three things to verify before signing a quote:

- **Oregon CCB or LCB license.** Tree removal is regulated by Oregon's **Construction Contractors Board (CCB)** or the **Landscape Contractors Board (LCB)** (https://www.oregon.gov/lcb/Pages/FAQ-Tree-Work.aspx). Verify CCB at **https://search.ccb.state.or.us/search/** by entering the company's CCB number; verify LCB at https://www.oregon.gov/lcb/Pages/Find-a-Licensee.aspx. The CCB also confirms the company is **bonded and insured**, with a state-required minimum of $300,000 in liability coverage (https://www.oregon.gov/lcb/Pages/FAQ-Tree-Work.aspx).
- **Workers' compensation insurance.** Oregon requires this, but some companies skip it. Ask for a **certificate of insurance addressed to you specifically**; without it, you or your homeowners insurance can be on the hook if a worker is hurt on your property (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/tips-hiring-arborist).
- **ISA Certified Arborist on the crew.** Not legally required, but an **International Society of Arboriculture (ISA) Certified Arborist** has passed the industry exam and keeps current with continuing education (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/tips-hiring-arborist). Look up an arborist's certification at https://www.treesaregood.org/findanarborist/findanarborist.

**The city maintains a list of local tree care providers** who have attended Urban Forestry's training; it's not an endorsement, but it's a reasonable starting point: https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/local-tree-care-providers. Companies on this list have agreed to follow Title 11 and have been removed in the past for code violations (same source).

**Your quote should include, in writing:**

- Total price for removal *and* stump grinding (the city expects stumps ground below grade for street trees, PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40)
- Debris haul-away
- Whether the company will pull the city tree permit (some do this for you, some don't)
- Proof of CCB/LCB number, insurance, and workers' comp
- Whether they will check for active bird nests before cutting (Migratory Bird Treaty Act, PCC 11.05, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/05)

> **Watch out for:** door-to-door solicitations after windstorms ("storm chasers"), cash-only / pay-now pressure, no company name on the truck, and "we can start today" promises during peak season. These are common red flags from unlicensed operators (https://www.portland.gov/trees/tree-care-and-resources/tips-hiring-arborist).

---

## 10. Accuracy caveats (please read before paying anyone)

- **Verify the overlays yourself.** I pulled your zoning and overlay data from PortlandMaps' API on **May 7, 2026**; data is generally current but can lag. Re-check at https://www.portlandmaps.com/ before applying.
- **Confirm the tree-permit determination with Urban Forestry.** I'm an AI, not an Urban Forestry inspector. The official answer about your specific tree comes from **trees@portlandoregon.gov** or **503-823-8733**.
- **Permit fees and code language change.** The July 1, 2025 fee elimination is current as of today (https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit), but Portland's fee schedule is reviewed annually; confirm the current fee at https://www.portland.gov/ppd/tree-permits/do-i-need-tree-permit/tree-permitting-fee-schedule before you assume $0.
- **Heritage tree status can be added.** Heritage tree designations are made by the City Council; the layer I checked is current, but if your tree is exceptionally old or large, ask Urban Forestry directly.
- **Tree service costs are estimates.** The $1,200-$3,000 range I gave is from the current Portland market and varies a lot with size, access, and location near power lines. Get **three quotes** before paying anyone.

---

## Glossary

- **BDS** — Bureau of Development Services. Portland's old name for what is now called **Permitting & Development (PP&D)**, the building permits bureau. You only deal with them if you're doing construction.
- **BES** — Bureau of Environmental Services. Portland's sewer and stormwater bureau; relevant only for trees in green-street facilities along the curb.
- **CCB** — Construction Contractors Board. Oregon's licensing agency for construction trades, including most tree-removal companies.
- **DBH** — Diameter at Breast Height. How thick the tree is around, measured 4.5 feet up the trunk. The number that determines whether a private tree needs a permit.
- **DevHub** — Portland's online portal for development and tree permits at https://www.portland.gov/permitting/development-hub.
- **Heritage tree** — A specific tree the city has designated for protection because of its size, age, or historic significance. Always needs a permit, even for pruning.
- **ISA** — International Society of Arboriculture. The certifying body for professional arborists.
- **LCB** — Oregon Landscape Contractors Board. The state agency that licenses landscape companies, including those doing tree planting and tree work under the arborist license phase.
- **Overlay zone** — An extra layer of zoning that applies on top of the base zone. Some overlays add restrictions on tree removal (such as environmental "c" and "p" overlays); your lot's only overlay is the "h" Aircraft Landing overlay, which does not.
- **PBOT** — Portland Bureau of Transportation. Manages city streets and the right-of-way pavement, but **not** street trees in the planting strip; that's Urban Forestry's job.
- **PCC** — Portland City Code. The set of all city laws; tree rules are in **Title 11**.
- **PCEF** — Portland Clean Energy Community Benefits Fund. The voter-approved fund that started paying for Urban Forestry permit fees in July 2025, which is why the permit is now free.
- **Planting strip** — The strip of grass or dirt between the sidewalk and the curb. Even though you mow it, it's part of the public right-of-way.
- **PP&D** — Permitting & Development. The current name of the building permits bureau; formerly BDS.
- **PP&R** — Portland Parks & Recreation. The bureau that contains Urban Forestry.
- **ROW** — Right-of-way. The strip of public land along the street, including the planting strip and (sometimes) part of what looks like your front yard. Anything in the ROW is the city's, even though you maintain it.
- **Street tree** — Any tree growing in the right-of-way. Always needs a permit for any work, regardless of size.
- **Title 11** — The chapter of Portland City Code covering trees.
- **Title 33** — The chapter of Portland City Code covering planning and zoning. Only triggers on tree work if you're doing a construction project at the same time.
- **Urban Forestry** — Portland's tree office, part of PP&R. The people you call about any tree question.

---

## One thing you didn't ask but might want to know

**Alameda is a tree-rich neighborhood, and the city pays attention.** Your stretch of NE 33rd between Knott and Fremont has mature street trees that the city actively monitors; my friendly heads-up is that if your tree is the one in the planting strip, expect Urban Forestry to ask whether pruning or root remediation could solve the problem before they approve removal (PCC 11.40, https://www.portland.gov/code/11/40). If the tree is dead, dying, or genuinely hazardous, that's an easy approval; if it's healthy and just inconveniently placed, the answer may be "prune, don't remove." Worth knowing before you spend money on a quote.

**One more bonus:** the **Free Trees** program at https://www.portland.gov/trees/find-your-free-trees runs giveaways twice a year (typically fall and early spring). If your timeline is flexible, apply for the permit now, then time your replacement planting to a giveaway and your replacement tree is **literally $0**.

I want to know whether I can remove a tree in my front yard at 3413 NE 33rd Ave, Portland, OR 97212, and what I need to do to remove it legally without getting fined or running into surprises. I'm a regular homeowner — I don't know anything about permits, zoning, or city code language, so please walk me through this like a knowledgeable friend would.

Please answer in a way that I can copy and paste into a document and still have every fact be checkable. That means: write for a Portland resident with no code or permitting background, aim for an 8th-to-10th grade reading level, and treat me as smart but unfamiliar with how the city works. Lead with a plain-English summary that answers my question directly in 3–5 sentences before any tables, citations, or code language. Define every acronym and technical term the first time you use it — including BDS (Bureau of Development Services), PP&R (Portland Parks & Recreation), Urban Forestry, BES (Bureau of Environmental Services), PBOT (Portland Bureau of Transportation), PCC (Portland City Code), DBH (diameter at breast height — how thick the tree is, measured 4.5 feet up the trunk), ROW (right-of-way — the strip of public land along the street, often including the planting strip and sometimes part of the front yard), and any other specialized term. If you wouldn't use the word with a neighbor over the fence, define it. Use everyday words instead of code language wherever possible — say "how thick the tree is around" before "DBH," and say "the city's tree office" before "Urban Forestry." Keep paragraphs short. Bold keywords inside sentences instead of using dense tables, and only use a table if you're comparing 3+ options on the same dimensions, with a one-sentence plain-English summary above it. Translate any dollar figure or timeline into something I can plan around (e.g., "about the cost of a nice dinner out," "roughly two weekends").

Here are the specific things I'd like the answer to cover, even though I didn't think to ask all of them:

1. **The core question, answered first in plain English:** Can I cut down a tree in my front yard at this address? What does the answer depend on (size of the tree, species, whether it's in the planting strip vs. fully on my property, zoning overlays on the lot, whether the lot is in a historic or environmental overlay district, etc.)?

2. **Whose tree is it, technically?** Help me understand the difference between a **street tree** (in the planting strip / right-of-way along the curb), a **private tree on my property**, and a **heritage tree**, because the rules and the bureau in charge change based on which one it is. Tell me how to figure out which kind of tree mine is before I do anything else, including how to use **PortlandMaps** (https://www.portlandmaps.com/) to check the property and any overlays.

3. **Which Portland Title(s) and chapters apply** — specifically Title 11 (Trees) and any relevant chapters of Title 33 (Zoning Code), with exact section numbers (e.g., PCC 11.50, PCC 11.60, PCC 11.70, etc.) and full working URLs to each section on https://www.portland.gov/code so I can click through and read the actual rule. Don't just say "Title 11" — say which chapter and section, in the same sentence as the claim, with the URL.

4. **Permit thresholds in plain English.** What size of tree (measured how, by whom) triggers a permit? What's the difference between a tree I can remove with no permit, a tree that needs a permit and a fee, and a tree the city will not let me remove except in narrow circumstances (dead, dying, hazardous, or causing damage)? Are there species-specific rules? Does a "front yard" location change anything?

5. **The exact bureau to contact, by name and contact info** — specifically Urban Forestry (part of Portland Parks & Recreation): phone, email, the page on portland.gov where I request a tree permit, and how long they typically take to respond. Same for BDS if a development-related tree review applies. Do not just say "contact the city" — give me the actual phone number, email, and URL inline, with citations.

6. **A numbered "what to do next" list, in the order I should do it.** For each step, tell me:
   - who to call or what website to visit (with the full URL),
   - what info or documents to bring or have ready,
   - what it costs (and translate the cost into something relatable),
   - roughly how long it takes,
   - and mark **(free)** next to any step that costs nothing.

7. **A "what to ask the city" script** I can read aloud on the phone or paste into an email when I contact Urban Forestry or BDS. Give me the actual sentences — including my address, what I want to do, and the specific questions a city employee will need answered to help me. Not "call Urban Forestry" — the actual words.

8. **Replacement and mitigation requirements.** If I'm allowed to remove the tree, am I required to plant a replacement, pay an in-lieu fee, or both? How is that calculated, and what would a typical homeowner pay or plant in this situation? Cite the specific section and URL.

9. **Common mistakes / gotchas for Portland homeowners** doing tree removal — for example: assuming a tree in the planting strip is "theirs," cutting first and asking later, hiring an unlicensed tree service, removing a tree during nesting season, missing a heritage tree designation, or not realizing an overlay zone (environmental, historic, scenic) changes the rules. Name the specific traps for *this* kind of project in Portland.

10. **Hiring a tree service.** Does Portland require the tree service to be licensed/bonded? How do I check? What should the quote include? Inline citation for any rule.

11. **Accuracy caveats**, framed as friendly warnings: zoning overlays and tree designations for my specific address can change the answer, so tell me to verify on PortlandMaps and confirm with Urban Forestry before paying anyone or cutting anything.

12. **A glossary at the end** of every acronym and technical term used more than once (BDS, PP&R, Urban Forestry, BES, PBOT, PCC, DBH, ROW, planting strip, heritage tree, overlay zone, etc.).

Citation rules — these are non-negotiable because I will copy-paste the entire answer into a document:

- Every factual claim (rules, numbers, fees, timelines, phone numbers, URLs, contact info, deadlines) must be followed **in the same sentence** by both the exact code section (e.g., `PCC 11.50.040`, not "Title 11") **and** a direct, working `https://...` URL to the authoritative source on portland.gov, portlandmaps.com, or the specific Administrative Rule PDF.

- If you cannot find a specific section + URL for a claim, say so explicitly in the answer itself ("I couldn't find a specific code section for this — please verify with Urban Forestry at [phone/email]") rather than citing vaguely.
- Do not use legal-register words like "shall," "promulgated," "thereunder," or "pursuant to." Write the way a friendly city employee would explain it over coffee.
- Do not lead with a property data table. Lead with the plain-English answer.

Please answer now for my address: **3413 NE 33rd Ave, Portland, OR 97212**.

## First: confirm which city you're in

SE 174th Ave is literally the city boundary between Portland and Gresham in your stretch. The City of Portland's official zip-code description for 97233 says Portland extends only "west of 174th, south to Division"; the east side of 174th Ave in the 800 block is Gresham. Your address (831, odd) is most likely on the west side and therefore in Portland, but please don't take my word for that; pull up <https://www.portlandmaps.com/search/?query=831+SE+174th+Ave+Portland+OR> and confirm. If the Property panel returns a base zone like R5 / R7 / R2.5, you're in Portland and everything below applies. If it returns "Gresham" or shows no Portland zoning, the rules are stricter and we'd need a different approach. (Gresham allows permanent foundation tiny homes but treats THOWs as RVs only, with no long-term backyard occupancy.)

## Path 1: tiny house on wheels (by far the easiest)

Portland is unusually permissive here, and this is probably the answer most people don't realize they have. Under the Occupied RV / THOW guidelines tied to Title 33.262, you can place one occupied tiny house on wheels on a residential lot that already has a primary dwelling (you have one). Wheels stay on; it must sit behind the front wall of your house; minimum 5 ft from the main dwelling; RV-style hookups (20-amp GFCI electrical, potable water, sewer tie-in by a licensed plumber). It does not count against density, no SDCs, no big permit set; you can host family or rent long-term (no short-term rentals). This is the path that delivers a livable second unit fastest and cheapest.

## Path 2: detached ADU (permanent backyard cottage)

Title 33.205 governs this. The size cap for your specific property is the gotcha: maximum is the lesser of 800 sq ft or 75% of your primary dwelling's living area. Your house is reportedly 960 sq ft (per public records; verify), so 75% = **720 sq ft** would be your detached-ADU ceiling. Two exceptions worth knowing: (a) basement conversions in a primary dwelling that's been on the site at least 5 years aren't subject to the size cap (yours easily qualifies on age, but a 1947 home may not have a usable basement); (b) recent code updates allow ADUs up to 1,000 sq ft on lots over 5,000 sq ft in some configurations, and an Adjustment review can push beyond 800 sq ft. Other key rules: 20 ft height (15 ft within setbacks), 5 ft side/rear setbacks, must sit behind the rear wall of the main house, and SDC waivers are available if you record a 10-year covenant against short-term rentals (saves ~$15K-$25K).

## Path 3: middle housing (the path most people miss)

Under Portland's Residential Infill Project, R2.5 / R5 / R7 lots can have **up to four units** total (counting the existing house). So instead of one ADU, you might be able to add a triplex-style configuration, or two ADUs plus a duplex conversion of your existing structure. This is the highest-value path if your lot supports it, and the FAR bonus actually grows as you add units (0.5 → 0.6 → 0.7 in R5). Worth at least sketching out before you commit to a single-ADU mindset.

## What you need to look up to lock in answers

From the PortlandMaps Property panel, please grab and paste back: **base zone** (R5 / R7 / R2.5 / etc.), **overlay codes** (any lowercase letters; "c" or "p" environmental zones would significantly constrain things), **lot size in sq ft**, and the **Hazards** panel (flood / landslide / liquefaction would matter for any foundation work). With those four things I can tell you exactly what's allowed, what setbacks apply, and roughly what your buildable footprint looks like.

## Practical next step

If you want zero ambiguity, Portland Permitting & Development offers free 15-minute planner consultations at 503-823-7300; bring your address and a rough idea of what you want to build and they'll tell you which path is cleanest. Worth its weight in gold before you spend money on plans.

What's the actual goal here, by the way: family member, rental income, downsize-and-rent-the-main-house, or something else? That changes which of these three paths is genuinely best, not just which is technically allowed.

Cites Title 33.262 and Title 33.205 by Title+chapter but no exact section numbers or URLs to specific code sections.
